5. **Latency Sensitivity:** Orchestrator decision-loop itself must be fast (< 100 ms) — centralized MANO is too slow, so **distributed MANO** with ETSI MEC is preferred.

6. **Interoperability:** Slices from different vendors (Cisco, Ericsson, Nokia) need standard APIs (3GPP, ETSI MEC, IETF).

7. **Energy Efficiency:** Fog nodes are battery/solar powered in remote deployments; slice placement must consider green KPIs.

(7 marks: 1 each for any 7 challenges, 2 bonus for examples.)

**(b)** [7 Marks] *Describe the ETSI MEC framework and explain how it integrates with Network Slicing for slice lifecycle management.*

**Model Solution:**

**ETSI MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing)** standardizes an edge platform that hosts MEC apps at the radio access network (e.g., 5G gNB or 5G UPF site).

**Components:**
- **MEC Host** — runs MEC apps and platform services.
- **MEC Platform Manager (MEPM)** — manages MEC app lifecycles.
- **MEC Orchestrator (MEO)** — orchestrates MEC app resources across MEC hosts.
- **Mx2 Interface** — communicates with NFVO for slice-aware MEC app deployment.

**Integration with Slicing:**
1. Tenant requests a slice; NFVO + MEO jointly decide on which MEC host the MEC app (e.g., AR rendering, video analytics) should be deployed.
2. The slice's **User Plane Function (UPF)** can be co-located with MEC host for traffic breakout at the edge (e.g., local break-out for a factory slice).
3. SLA monitoring uses MEC platform services (RNIS, Location Service) to feed back to the slice assurance system.
4. Slice mobility is handled by MEO re-routing to the nearest MEC host when the user moves.

(7 marks: 2 for ETSI MEC components, 3 for integration, 2 for lifecycle diagram or example.)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Edge = Tier 1** (closest to device); **Fog = Tier 2** (mid); **Cloud = Tier 3** (data center). Always mark on diagrams.
- **Network Slicing ≠ VLAN ≠ QoS** — slicing is end-to-end across compute + storage + transport, enabled by **SDN + NFV**.
- **3GPP TS 28.530** defines the slice architecture; **TS 28.531** defines templates; **TS 28.533** defines management.
- **5G slice types (memorize):** **eMBB** (high bandwidth), **URLLC** (low latency, high reliability), **mMTC** (massive IoT).
- **Key enablers** = SDN, NFV, MANO, Cloud-native (Kubernetes), AI/ML for zero-touch orchestration.
- **ETSI MEC** is the de-facto edge platform, while **ETSI NFV MANO** orchestrates slices.
- **SLA metrics** to remember: latency (ms), jitter (ms), bandwidth (Mbps), availability (%), packet loss (%), reliability (99.999 % for URLLC).
- **Slice lifecycle = Design → Onboard → Instantiate → Configure → Operate → Heal → Decommission.**
- **Fog Computing** was coined by **Cisco in 2012**; **MEC** was standardized by **ETSI in 2014**.
- **Common exam trap:** do not write "edge is the same as fog" — fog is a *hierarchical layer* between edge and cloud.
- **Isolation mechanisms:** dedicated vCPU pinning, SR-IOV, DPDK, hardware-rooted trust, encrypted tunnels.
- **Slice migration (handover):** critical for V2X/URLLC; uses **Follow-Me** cloud concept.
- **Real KTU past-year weightage:** Network Slicing in SDC has appeared as a **14-mark full question** twice in the last 3 years — high-priority topic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture, Protocol and Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Architecture, Protocol and Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Fog/Edge Computing Architecture?

**Fog Computing Architecture** is a horizontally distributed, layered continuum of computing infrastructure that sits between the physical *sensing endpoints* (sensors, IoT devices, mobile phones) and the centralized *cloud data centers*. It is engineered to provide compute, storage, and networking services closer to where data is generated.

**Edge Computing Architecture** is a more aggressive variant where computation, storage, and intelligence are pushed all the way to (or near) the data source itself — directly on the device or on an on-premise gateway. It minimizes the physical distance data must travel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Fog Architecture* = Device → Fog Node → Cloud (3-Tier Horizontal Model)
> *Edge Architecture* = Device/Gateway → Cloud (Device-Side Vertical Model)
> Both share a common goal: **reduce end-to-end latency, save backbone bandwidth, and improve Quality of Service (QoS).**

## 1.2 Protocol Stack in Fog/Edge Environments

**Protocols** in this domain are the standardized communication languages used by heterogeneous devices, fog nodes, and the cloud to exchange telemetry, control commands, and security handshakes. The fog/edge protocol stack is fundamentally different from traditional web protocols because it must operate over **constrained, lossy, low-power networks** (LLNs).

> [!NOTE]
> The three most application-layer protocols KTU expects you to know are:
> 1. **MQTT** (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport) — TCP-based, pub/sub
> 2. **CoAP** (Constrained Application Protocol) — UDP-based, request/response
> 3. **AMQP** (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol) — TCP-based, message-oriented middleware

## 1.3 Security in Fog/Edge

**Fog/Edge Security** is the set of policies, cryptographic primitives, and trust frameworks that protect data, identities, and computational integrity across the highly distributed and physically exposed fog-edge continuum.

The **CIA Triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) is the foundational security model:
- **Confidentiality:** Only authorized entities can read the data.
- **Integrity:** Data is not tampered with in transit or at rest.
- **Availability:** Services remain accessible despite attacks (e.g., DDoS).

## 1.4 Real-World Conceptual Analogy 🍕

> [!TIP]
> **The Pizza Delivery Analogy (Perfect for First-Time Learners):**
>
> Imagine a city with **10,000 hungry customers** craving pizza:
> - **Cloud Computing** = A single giant central kitchen 500 km away. Every order must travel far, every pizza is uniform, but delivery is slow.
> - **Edge Computing** = Each apartment building has a *mini-oven* in the lobby. You bake your pizza *where you live* — super fast, but expensive and limited menu.
> - **Fog Computing** = The city has **20 local pizzerias** (fog nodes) placed in every neighborhood. They take bulk orders from nearby buildings, pre-bake partially, and only the tricky ones go to the central kitchen (cloud).
>
> **The "fog" is the in-between layer** that aggregates, pre-processes, and routes intelligently. **Protocols** are the standardized order tickets (handwritten slip, app, phone call). **Security** is the bouncer checking IDs and the locked cash register.

## 1.5 Physical & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

Key standard metrics you must memorize:
- **MQTT Default Port: 1883** (Plain) / **8883** (TLS)
- **CoAP Default Port: 5683** (Plain) / **5684** (DTLS)
- **AMQP Default Port: 5672** (Plain) / **5671** (TLS)
- **HTTP Default Port: 80** / **HTTPS: 443**
- **Typical Edge-to-Fog Latency Target: < 10 ms**
- **Typical Fog-to-Cloud Latency Target: 50–200 ms**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reference Three-Tier Fog-Edge Architecture

The **OpenFog Consortium / IEEE 1934** reference architecture describes a three-tier hierarchy:

### Tier 1 — Edge / Device Tier (Sensing & Actuation)
- **Hardware:** Sensors (temperature, pressure, camera, LIDAR), actuators, microcontrollers (Arduino, ESP32), wearables, smartphones, vehicles.
- **Function:** Generate raw data; perform trivial preprocessing (filtering, normalization, threshold checks).
- **Constraints:** Battery-limited, **computational power: 10–1000 MIPS**, RAM often **< 512 KB**.
- **Example:** A heart-rate sensor in a smartwatch computing a 1-second moving average.

### Tier 2 — Fog Tier (Intermediate Compute)
- **Hardware:** Fog nodes (industrial gateways, Raspberry Pi clusters, micro-datacenter racks, 5G MEC servers).
- **Function:** Aggregation, stream analytics, ML inference, temporary caching, local decision-making, protocol translation (MQTT → HTTP).
- **Characteristics:** **Low latency (1–10 ms)**, supports virtualization (containers, VMs), geo-distributed.
- **Example:** A smart-traffic fog node controlling 50 nearby intersections.

### Tier 3 — Cloud Tier (Global Orchestration)
- **Hardware:** Hyperscale data centers (AWS, Azure, GCP).
- **Function:** Long-term storage, heavy training of ML models, global analytics, billing, fleet management.
- **Characteristics:** **Elastic scalability**, **high latency (50–500 ms)**, near-infinite storage.

## 2.2 Logical Layered View (Cross-Cutting)

Orthogonal to the three tiers, an architectural *layered view* exists:
1. **Application / Service Layer** (top) — User-facing apps, dashboards, alerting.
2. **Orchestration Layer** — Resource scheduling, container orchestration (Kubernetes K3s at edge).
3. **Compute / Storage Layer** — Virtual machines, containers, distributed storage.
4. **Network / Communication Layer** — Wired + wireless links, SDN controllers.
5. **Sensing / Hardware Layer** (bottom) — Physical devices.

## 2.3 Why Fog/Edge? — The "Why" Logic

| # | Problem with Pure Cloud | Fog/Edge Solution |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Latency 100–500 ms unacceptable for autonomous cars / robotic surgery | Fog node responds in **< 10 ms** |
| 2 | Backbone bandwidth saturated by 1 million cameras streaming 1080p | Edge filters out 90% of frames; only events sent |
| 3 | Single point of failure at cloud | Geo-distributed fog nodes provide resilience |
| 4 | Privacy laws (GDPR) — raw video can't leave EU | Edge anonymizes faces locally before cloud upload |
| 5 | Cloud cost: $0.0001/GB egress — at petabyte scale, ruinous | Local processing slashes egress by 60–95% |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📐

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **direct KTU 2024 ESE favorites** — memorize units and boundary conditions.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | **End-to-End Latency** | $T_{e2e} = T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{queue} + T_{proc}$ | Sum of propagation + transmission + queuing + processing delays |
| F2 | **Propagation Delay** | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = link distance, $v$ = signal propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber) |
| F3 | **Transmission Delay** | $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |
| F4 | **Bandwidth Savings Ratio** | $BSR = 1 - \dfrac{D_{uploaded}}{D_{generated}}$ | $D$ in bytes; **0.0 = no savings, 1.0 = full savings** |
| F5 | **Effective Throughput (Security)** | $T_{eff} = \dfrac{P_{payload}}{T_{handshake} + T_{trans}}$ | Post-TLS handshake throughput |
| F6 | **Amdahl's Edge Speedup** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | $f$ = parallelizable fraction, $n$ = edge worker count |
| F7 | **Energy per Bit Transmission** | $E_b = V \cdot I \cdot T_{trans} / L$ | Joules per bit; key for battery-limited sensors |
| F8 | **CIA Triad Boolean State** | $S_{sec} = C \land I \and A$ | System secure **iff all three** properties hold |
| F9 | **MQTT QoS Levels** | $\text{QoS} \in \{0, 1, 2\}$ | 0 = at-most-once, 1 = at-least-once, 2 = exactly-once |
| F10 | **RTT for 3-Way Handshake** | $T_{conn} \approx 1.5 \times RTT$ | TCP setup cost; motivates UDP-based CoAP for fog |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units convention:** Latency in **ms**, Throughput in **Mbps**, Bandwidth in **Hz**, Energy in **Joules**. Always include units in your KTU answers — examiners award partial marks for correct units.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Edge ECU processes LiDAR in **< 5 ms**; fog node coordinates with neighboring cars; cloud receives aggregated telemetry.
- **Smart Grid:** Edge smart meters sample every 15 min; fog substation detects anomalies locally; cloud runs long-term demand forecasting.
- **Healthcare IoT:** Wearable edge ECG detects arrhythmia in 200 ms; fog hospital server triggers ambulance dispatch.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Latency Budgeting

A fog/edge system engineer must prove that placing a service at the edge satisfies a latency SLA. We derive the **total time from sensor reading to actuator response**.

$$
T_{e2e} \;=\; T_{sense} + T_{prop}^{s-f} + T_{proc}^{fog} + T_{prop}^{f-a} + T_{act}
$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

**Step 1 — Sensing delay** $T_{sense}$: Time for ADC to capture one sample.

$$
T_{sense} = \frac{1}{f_s}
$$

where $f_s$ is the sampling rate in Hz.

**Step 2 — Sensor-to-Fog propagation** $T_{prop}^{s-f}$: Signal travel time over the wireless link (e.g., LoRa, Wi-Fi).

$$
T_{prop}^{s-f} = \frac{d_{s-f}}{c}
$$

with $c \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in air.

**Step 3 — Fog processing delay** $T_{proc}^{fog}$: Time the fog node spends running the inference or aggregation algorithm. Modeled as:

$$
T_{proc}^{fog} = \frac{C \cdot I}{P_{fog}}
$$

where:
- $C$ = cycles per instruction
- $I$ = number of instructions
- $P_{fog}$ = fog node CPU clock (cycles/sec)

**Step 4 — Fog-to-Actuator propagation** $T_{prop}^{f-a}$: Same physics as Step 2, but on the downstream link.

**Step 5 — Actuator mechanical delay** $T_{act}$: Physical movement time (motor, valve).

### Worked Numerical Example

> A vibration sensor samples at $f_s = 1\,\text{kHz}$ and sends data to a fog node 50 m away. The fog node runs a 50,000-instruction filter with $C = 4$ cycles/instruction on a 200 MHz CPU. The actuator is 30 m from the fog node. Compute $T_{e2e}$.

**Step 1:**
$$
T_{sense} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1.0\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 2:**
$$
T_{prop}^{s-f} = \frac{50}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.67 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s} \approx 0.000167\ \text{ms}
$$

(Usually negligible for short radio links; becomes significant over long fiber.)

**Step 3:**
$$
T_{proc}^{fog} = \frac{4 \times 50000}{200 \times 10^6} = \frac{200000}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{s} = 1.0\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 4:**
$$
T_{prop}^{f-a} = \frac{30}{3 \times 10^8} = 1.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{s} \approx 0.0001\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 5:** Assume $T_{act} = 0.5\ \text{ms}$ (small solenoid).

**Total:**
$$
T_{e2e} = 1.0 + 0.000167 + 1.0 + 0.0001 + 0.5 = 2.5003\ \text{ms}
$$

**Inference:** Total latency is dominated by sensing and processing — propagation is negligible. This justifies placing inference at the fog rather than the cloud (where an additional 100 ms round-trip would violate any real-time SLA).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bandwidth Savings Ratio (BSR)

Fog/edge reduces uplink traffic by preprocessing. Let:
- $D_g$ = total raw data generated at the edge (bytes/sec)
- $D_u$ = data uploaded to cloud (bytes/sec)
- $D_d$ = data dropped/filtered locally (bytes/sec)

Conservation of data:
$$
D_g = D_u + D_d
$$

**Definition of BSR:**
$$
BSR = 1 - \frac{D_u}{D_g}
$$

Equivalent form using filter ratio $r = D_u / D_g$:
$$
BSR = 1 - r
$$

### Worked Numerical Example

> A 4K surveillance camera generates 25 MB/s. The edge gateway runs a motion-detection filter that only uploads frames with detected motion, which is 8% of frames. Compute BSR and monthly cloud egress cost (assume $0.05/GB).

**Step 1 — Filter ratio:**
$$
r = 0.08
$$

**Step 2 — BSR:**
$$
BSR = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92 = 92\%
$$

**Step 3 — Uploaded data rate:**
$$
D_u = r \times D_g = 0.08 \times 25 = 2.0\ \text{MB/s}
$$

**Step 4 — Monthly volume:**
$$
V = 2.0 \times 10^6 \times 86400 \times 30 = 5.184 \times 10^{12}\ \text{bytes}
$$

Convert to GB:
$$
V = \frac{5.184 \times 10^{12}}{10^9} = 5184\ \text{GB}
$$

**Step 5 — Monthly cost:**
$$
\text{Cost} = 5184 \times 0.05 = \$\,259.20
$$

Without edge filtering, cost would be $6480 — a **96% savings**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Edge vs Fog vs Cloud

| Parameter | Edge | Fog | Cloud |
|---|---|---|---|
| Distance from data source | 0–1 m | 1–50 km | 100+ km |
| Typical latency | **< 1 ms** | **1–10 ms** | **50–500 ms** |
| Computational power | Very low | Moderate | Virtually unlimited |
| Storage | KB–MB | GB–TB | PB–EB |
| Energy source | Battery | Mains / solar | Grid (massive) |
| Autonomy when disconnected | High | Medium | None |
| Best for | Time-critical control | Local analytics | Big-data, training |
| Example device | Smartwatch | 5G MEC server | AWS region |

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Protocol Comparison

| Property | MQTT | CoAP | AMQP | HTTP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | UDP | TCP | TCP |
| Architecture | Pub/Sub | Req/Resp | Pub/Sub + Queues | Req/Resp |
| Header size | **2 bytes** | **4 bytes** | 8 bytes | ~20+ bytes |
| Default port | 1883 | 5683 | 5672 | 80 |
| QoS levels | 3 (0,1,2) | 2 (CON/NON) | 4 (settle modes) | None |
| Security | TLS | DTLS | TLS / SASL | TLS |
| Best for | Telemetry | Constrained sensors | Enterprise messaging | Web apps |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for exams:** "**M**QTT for **M**any devices, **C**oAP for **C**onstrained devices, **A**MQP for **A**dvanced queues, **H**TTP for **H**uman browsers."

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Tier Fog-Edge-Cloud Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TIER1["TIER 1: EDGE / DEVICE LAYER"]
        E1["Sensor 1: Temperature"]
        E2["Sensor 2: Camera"]
        E3["Sensor 3: Smartwatch"]
        E4["Actuator: Motor"]
    end

    subgraph TIER2["TIER 2: FOG LAYER"]
        F1["Fog Node A: Gateway"]
        F2["Fog Node B: MEC Server"]
        F3["Local Analytics Engine"]
    end

    subgraph TIER3["TIER 3: CLOUD LAYER"]
        C1["Cloud Data Center"]
        C2["Big Data Analytics"]
        C3["ML Training Pipeline"]
        C4["Long-Term Storage"]
    end

    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F2
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C1 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
    F2 -. Control .-> E4
```

## 4.2 MQTT Publish-Subscribe Flow with Broker

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Pub1 as Publisher: Sensor
    participant Pub2 as Publisher: Mobile App
    participant Brk as MQTT Broker
    participant Sub1 as Subscriber: Fog Node
    participant Sub2 as Subscriber: Cloud Service

    Pub1->>Brk: PUBLISH topic=sensor/temp payload=25.4
    Pub2->>Brk: PUBLISH topic=cmd/motor payload=START
    Brk-->>Sub1: Forward sensor/temp
    Brk-->>Sub2: Forward sensor/temp
    Sub1->>Brk: SUBSCRIBE topic=sensor/#
    Brk-->>Sub1: SUBACK
    Note over Brk,Sub2: Decouples publishers from subscribers
```

## 4.3 Security Threat Model in Fog-Edge

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DEV["Compromised Edge Device"] -->|"Malicious Data"| FOG["Fog Node"]
    FOG -->|"MITM Attack"| CLOUD["Cloud"]
    ATTACKER["External Attacker"] -->|"DDoS"| FOG
    ATTACKER -->|"Eavesdropping"| DEV
    FOG -->|"Insider Misuse"| FOGDB["Local DB"]

    subgraph DEFENSES["Defensive Layers"]
        D1["Authentication: X.509 / OAuth2"]
        D2["Encryption: AES-256, TLS 1.3"]
        D3["Integrity: SHA-256, HMAC"]
        D4["Trust: Blockchain Ledger"]
        D5["IDS: Anomaly Detection"]
    end

    DEV -.->|protected by| D1
    FOG -.->|protected by| D2
    CLOUD -.->|protected by| D3
    FOG -.->|protected by| D5
```

## 4.4 Trust & Key Management Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Device Boot"] --> B["Generate Key Pair"]
    B --> C["Register with Fog CA"]
    C --> D{"Certificate Valid?"}
    D -->|Yes| E["Join Secure Cluster"]
    D -->|No| F["Quarantine Device"]
    E --> G["Periodic Re-Keying"]
    G --> H["Monitor Trust Score"]
    H -->|"Trust < Threshold"| F
    H -->|"Trust >= Threshold"| E
    F --> I["Alert Security Admin"]
```

## 4.5 Protocol Stack Vertical Layering

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L7["Application: MQTT / CoAP / AMQP"]
    L6["Presentation: TLS / DTLS Encryption"]
    L5["Session: QoS Negotiation"]
    L4["Transport: TCP for MQTT, UDP for CoAP"]
    L3["Network: IPv6 / 6LoWPAN / RPL"]
    L2["Data Link: IEEE 802.15.4 / Wi-Fi / 5G"]
    L1["Physical: Radio / Fiber / Wired"]
    L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "draw the architecture," include the three horizontal tiers, label data flow direction, and annotate **at least one latency/bandwidth value** to score full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Differentiate between Edge Computing and Fog Computing with two key distinctions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Edge Computing | Fog Computing |
|---|---|---|
| Location of processing | Directly on the device or on-premise gateway (0 m) | On an intermediate node between device and cloud (1–50 km) |
| Hierarchy | Vertical: device talks to cloud | Horizontal: 3-tier hierarchy (device → fog → cloud) |
| Typical latency | **< 1 ms** | **1–10 ms** |
| Role | Filtering, thresholding, local control | Aggregation, analytics, coordination across multiple edges |

**Closing sentence (mandatory for full marks):** *"While Edge pushes intelligence to the device, Fog distributes intelligence across a layer of intermediate nodes — both aim to reduce cloud dependency, but Fog preserves a structured hierarchy."*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three application-layer protocols used in IoT/Fog environments and state their default ports.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **MQTT** — Default port **1883** (plain) / **8883** (TLS). TCP-based, publish-subscribe, 2-byte header, ideal for unreliable networks.
2. **CoAP** — Default port **5683** (plain) / **5684** (DTLS). UDP-based, request-response, 4-byte header, mimics HTTP for constrained devices.
3. **AMQP** — Default port **5672** (plain) / **5671** (TLS). TCP-based, supports message queuing, transactions, used in enterprise IoT gateways.

*(Alternative acceptable: HTTP 80, HTTPS 443, LWM2M 5683/5684, DDS using dynamic ports.)*

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ✏️ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the three-tier architecture of Fog Computing. Discuss the roles of Edge, Fog, and Cloud layers with at least two real-world use cases for each tier. (14 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Three-Tier Architecture Diagram & Description (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Fog Computing Reference Architecture (as standardized by **IEEE 1934 / OpenFog Consortium**) is organized as a three-tier horizontal hierarchy:

**Tier 1 — Edge Layer:**
Comprises the physical sensors, actuators, and embedded devices that interface with the real world. These are resource-constrained nodes with limited CPU (often **< 200 MHz**), RAM (**< 512 KB**), and battery power. They perform rudimentary operations: sampling, thresholding, and short-term buffering.

**Tier 2 — Fog Layer:**
The middle layer consisting of geographically distributed *fog nodes* — industrial gateways, 5G Multi-access Edge Computing (MEC) servers, or micro-clusters. This layer provides:
- Compute (virtualization via containers/VMs)
- Storage (temporary caching)
- Networking (aggregation, protocol translation)
- Latency: **typically 1–10 ms**

**Tier 3 — Cloud Layer:**
Hyperscale centralized data centers offering elastic compute, durable storage, and heavy analytics. Latency to a regional user is **50–200 ms**. Used for long-term archival, ML model training, and global orchestration.

*(Insert the three-tier diagram from Section 4.1 here — examiners allocate 2 marks for diagram.)*

**Data flow:** Devices generate data → fog node aggregates & analyzes → only summaries/insights reach the cloud → decisions may flow back down (e.g., model updates) → edge.

#### Part (b) — Real-World Use Cases (7 marks)

**Edge Layer Use Cases:**
1. **Pacemaker / Wearable ECG Monitor:** Edge continuously monitors cardiac rhythm; raises local alarm within milliseconds without cloud round-trip. (3.5 marks allocation)
2. **Anti-lock Braking System (ABS) in cars:** Wheel-speed sensor + edge ECU actuates brake within **< 5 ms** — physically impossible with cloud.

**Fog Layer Use Cases:**
1. **Smart Traffic Management:** A fog node at a road intersection aggregates feeds from 10 nearby cameras and 50 vehicle sensors; runs computer-vision-based vehicle counting; controls local signal timing every 2 seconds. (2 marks)
2. **Wind Farm Cluster Controller:** Fog node coordinates 20 turbines, performing local pitch optimization, before reporting aggregated power output to the cloud every 5 minutes.

**Cloud Layer Use Cases:**
1. **National Power Grid Demand Forecasting:** Cloud trains LSTM neural networks on TBs of historical demand data; pushes retrained models to fog nodes monthly. (1.5 marks)
2. **Healthcare Population Analytics:** Cloud de-identifies and aggregates patient data from thousands of hospitals to detect epidemiological patterns (e.g., flu outbreaks).

---

### ✏️ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q: (a) Compare MQTT, CoAP, and AMQP protocols with respect to transport, header size, QoS, and typical use cases. (7 marks)**
**(b) Explain the CIA Triad in the context of Fog/Edge Security. Discuss any four major attack vectors and the corresponding countermeasures. (7 marks)**

#### Part (a) — Protocol Comparison (7 marks)

| Feature | MQTT | CoAP | AMQP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP (connection-oriented) | UDP (connectionless) | TCP (connection-oriented) |
| Header | **2 bytes** (fixed) | **4 bytes** (fixed) | 8-byte frame header |
| Messaging pattern | Publish/Subscribe (asynchronous) | Request/Response (synchronous) | Pub/Sub + Queues + Routing |
| QoS | 3 levels (0, 1, 2) | 2 modes (CON, NON) | 4 settle modes |
| Default port | 1883 (8883 TLS) | 5683 (5684 DTLS) | 5672 (5671 TLS) |
| Security | TLS 1.2/1.3 | DTLS | TLS + SASL |
| Typical use | Telemetry from many devices | Resource-constrained sensors | Enterprise messaging |
| Latency overhead | Moderate (TCP handshake) | Low (no handshake) | High (heavy framing) |

**Key distinction (1 mark for closing sentence):** MQTT sacrifices header efficiency for reliability (TCP), CoAP sacrifices reliability for efficiency (UDP), and AMQP sacrifices simplicity for enterprise-grade features (queuing, transactions, routing).

#### Part (b) — Security: CIA Triad & Threat Vectors (7 marks)

**The CIA Triad in Fog/Edge (2 marks):**
- **Confidentiality** (C): Data is encrypted so eavesdroppers on the wireless medium cannot interpret it.
- **Integrity** (I): Hashes and digital signatures detect tampering during transmission.
- **Availability** (A): Services remain operational even under DDoS or hardware failure.

**Four Attack Vectors and Countermeasures (5 marks — 1.25 each):**

1. **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack:** Attacker intercepts messages between sensor and fog node.
   *Countermeasure:* Mutual TLS authentication with X.509 certificates; pin certificates to devices.

2. **Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS):** Flood of packets from compromised botnet devices overwhelms fog node.
   *Countermeasure:* Rate-limiting, anomaly-based IDS at fog gateway, device authentication before accepting traffic.

3. **Eavesdropping / Sniffing:** Attacker passively listens to wireless transmissions.
   *Countermeasure:* AES-256 link-layer encryption; rotate session keys periodically.

4. **Data Tampering / Replay Attack:** Attacker modifies a sensor reading or replays an old one.
   *Countermeasure:* HMAC-SHA256 integrity tags, monotonically increasing timestamps, nonce-based sequence numbers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout ⚠️**
> - **Common 1-mark loss:** Writing "MQTT" without mentioning it is **TCP-based pub/sub on port 1883**. Examiners look for protocol-specific keywords.
> - **Common 2-mark loss:** Forgetting the **diagram in Q.A** — even a hand-drawn three-tier block diagram is mandatory for full architecture marks.
> - **Common 3-mark loss:** Listing attack names (MITM, DDoS) without stating the **countermeasure**. KTU 2024 rubric awards 50% weight to mitigation.
> - **Common 2-mark loss:** Confusing "Edge" and "Fog" — remember: **Edge is on the device, Fog is in between.**
> - **Formula sheet trap:** Writing $T_{e2e}$ without units (ms) — KTU deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Architecture is 3-tier:** Device (Edge) → Fog Node → Cloud. Draw this every time.
- ✅ **Edge** = on-device, **< 1 ms** latency, battery-powered, < 512 KB RAM typical.
- ✅ **Fog** = intermediate, **1–10 ms** latency, virtualized, geo-distributed.
- ✅ **Cloud** = centralized, **50–500 ms** latency, unlimited compute/storage.
- ✅ **MQTT** = TCP, port **1883**, pub/sub, 2-byte header, 3 QoS levels.
- ✅ **CoAP** = UDP, port **5683**, req/resp, 4-byte header, mimics HTTP.
- ✅ **AMQP** = TCP, port **5672**, queuing + routing, enterprise-grade.
- ✅ **CIA Triad** = Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. All three must hold.
- ✅ **Encryption** = AES-256 for data, TLS 1.3 for transport, SHA-256 for integrity.
- ✅ **BSR formula:** $BSR = 1 - D_u / D_g$ — measures edge filtering efficiency.
- ✅ **End-to-end latency** = sum of sensing + propagation + processing + actuation delays.
- ✅ **OpenFog / IEEE 1934** = standard reference architecture body.
- ✅ **5G MEC** = Multi-access Edge Computing — a real-world fog deployment.
- ✅ **Key threats:** MITM, DDoS, Eavesdropping, Tampering, Replay, Insider misuse.
- ✅ **Defenses:** Mutual TLS, IDS, rate-limiting, blockchain-based trust, HMAC tags.
- ✅ **Remember the ports:** 1883, 5683, 5672, 8883 (TLS), 5684 (DTLS), 5671 (TLS).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Seven Layered Model Architecture for Internet of Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Seven Layered Model Architecture for Internet of Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Internet of Vehicles (IoV)** is defined as a distributed, multi-layered cyber-physical network that interconnects heterogeneous vehicular entities (cars, trucks, drones, RSUs, pedestrians, smart infrastructure) using vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication protocols, edge/fog computing paradigms, and cloud backends to enable real-time perception, decision-making, and actuation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):**
> The **Seven-Layered Reference Architecture for IoV** is a hierarchical, modular design framework that decomposes the entire IoV stack into seven logically isolated, protocol-bound functional planes. Each plane owns exclusive responsibilities (sensing, transport, edge intelligence, service composition, application logic, business governance, and cross-cutting security/trust), thereby enabling **interoperability**, **scalability**, **low-latency response**, and **end-to-end Quality of Service (QoS)** across vehicular ecosystems.

The seven canonical layers (bottom-up) are:

| # | Layer Name | Primary Function |
|---|------------|------------------|
| 1 | Perception / Physical Layer | Sensing & actuation |
| 2 | Network / Communication Layer | V2X data transport |
| 3 | Edge / Fog Computing Layer | Local intelligence |
| 4 | Middleware / Service Layer | Abstraction & orchestration |
| 5 | Application Layer | User-facing services |
| 6 | Business / Management Layer | Policies, billing, governance |
| 7 | Security & Trust Layer (Cross-Layer) | Identity, privacy, resilience |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Smart Highway Restaurant"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for B.Tech Students:**
> Imagine a smart highway restaurant (a *dhaba* on a Kerala highway that is fully automated). Vehicles passing by are *customers*.
>
> - **Layer 1 (Perception):** The CCTV cameras, fuel sensors, and number-plate readers at the entrance — they *sense* who is arriving.
> - **Layer 2 (Network):** The Wi-Fi/4G/5G towers that *carry* the order from the car to the kitchen.
> - **Layer 3 (Edge/Fog):** The local kitchen that prepares the dosa *immediately* without calling the head chef in Delhi — **latency-critical** cooking.
> - **Layer 4 (Middleware):** The waiter who translates the customer's order (in any language) into a kitchen-friendly format and routes it to the right chef.
> - **Layer 5 (Application):** The menu app on the customer's phone showing "Order Ready in 4 min."
> - **Layer 6 (Business):** The billing, GST, and inventory management system.
> - **Layer 7 (Security):** The bouncer at the door verifying IDs, checking for malware in the order, and ensuring data privacy.
>
> Just as a dhaba cannot operate without a bouncer OR a local kitchen, an IoV system needs **all seven layers** to function safely.

## 1.3 Why a *Seven*-Layer Model? (Motivation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why not 3 or 5 layers?**
> Classical IoT uses a 3-layer (Perception, Network, Application) model. **IoV is far more demanding** because vehicles are *high-mobility safety-critical systems* operating at speeds up to **120 km/h** with sub-**10 ms** reaction deadlines. A 3-layer model collapses critical fog-compute, security, and business roles into over-loaded layers, making the system:
> - Un-debuggable
> - Impossible to certify (ISO 26262 functional safety)
> - Vulnerable to single-point trust failures

Hence the KTU 2024 syllabus mandates an **extended 7-layer decomposition** that explicitly carves out Edge/Fog, Middleware, and Security as *first-class citizens*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Layer-by-Layer Theoretical Breakdown

### **Layer 1 — Perception / Physical Layer (The "Senses")**

- **Responsibility:** Raw data acquisition from the physical world.
- **Sensors used in vehicles:**
  - LiDAR (range ≈ 100 m, accuracy ±2 cm)
  - Radar (77 GHz, range 250 m)
  - Cameras (RGB, IR, stereo)
  - Ultrasonic (parking, < 5 m)
  - Inertial Measurement Unit (IMU), GPS, OBD-II port
- **Why it matters in IoV:** A self-driving car generates roughly **4 TB/day** of sensor data. Only pre-filtered, relevant data should climb the stack.
- **KTU keyword to remember:** *Physical / Sensing plane.*

### **Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer (The "Nervous System")**

- **Responsibility:** Reliable, low-latency transport of packets among entities.
- **Protocol families:**

  $$ \text{V2X} = \{ \text{V2V}, \text{V2I}, \text{V2P}, \text{V2N}, \text{V2G}, \text{V2S} \} $$

- **Standards:** DSRC (IEEE 802.11p), C-V2X (3GPP Release 14/15/16, 5G NR-V2X sidelink).
- **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs):**

$$ \text{End-to-End Latency} \; L_{e2e} = L_{tx} + L_{prop} + L_{proc} + L_{queue} $$

- **KTU value to remember:** 5G NR-V2X targets **1 ms** user-plane latency at **99.999%** reliability.

### **Layer 3 — Edge / Fog Computing Layer (The "Local Brain")**

This is the **focal layer** of the *PECIT867* course.

- **Responsibility:** Execute latency-critical inference, fusion, and decision logic **close to the vehicle** (RSU, MEC host, on-board unit).
- **Compute hierarchy:**

$$ \text{Vehicle OBU} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{RSU / MEC} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Fog Node Cluster} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Cloud} $$

- **Workload offloading decision** (foundational fog equation):

$$ T_{offload} = \frac{D_{size}}{R_{uplink}} + \frac{C_{workload}}{f_{edge}} \quad ; \quad T_{local} = \frac{C_{workload}}{f_{local}} $$

Offload is beneficial when $T_{offload} < T_{local}$ **AND** the energy model favours it.

- **Real-world use:** Tesla's "day of inference" runs at edge nodes near superchargers before syncing to the cloud.

### **Layer 4 — Middleware / Service Layer (The "Translator")**

- **Responsibility:** Decouple hardware from application using abstractions (publish/subscribe brokers, REST/gRPC, service registries, SDN controllers).
- **Functions:** Protocol translation, data normalisation, container orchestration (Kubernetes at the RSU), micro-service composition.
- **Standard:** ETSI MEC reference architecture, oneM2M service layer.

### **Layer 5 — Application Layer (The "What the User Sees")**

- **Responsibility:** End-user services such as autonomous driving, ADAS, infotainment, predictive maintenance, platooning.
- **Forms the SLO (Service Level Objective) the rest of the stack must honour.**

### **Layer 6 — Business / Management Layer (The "Strategy & Money")**

- **Responsibility:** SLA enforcement, billing, fleet analytics, regulatory compliance (GDPR, Indian MV Act 2019), carbon-credit accounting.
- **Decides** *which* services are deployed, *where*, and *for whom* — i.e. **orchestration policy**.

### **Layer 7 — Security & Trust Layer (Cross-Cutting "Bouncer")**

- **Responsibility:** Identity (PKI, blockchain), confidentiality (AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit), integrity, non-repudiation, intrusion detection, OTA firmware signing.
- **Why cross-cutting:** Every other layer *consumes* security primitives; it is **not** a vertical layer that can be tacked on at the top.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Print this table — it covers 80% of numerical/latency questions in Module-2 KTU exams.

| # | Concept | Formula / Parameter | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---------|---------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Shannon Channel Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bits/s |
| 2 | E2E Latency (5G URLLC) | $L_{e2e} = L_{tx}+L_{prop}+L_{proc}+L_{queue}$ | **1 ms** target |
| 3 | Offload Decision | $T_{offload} < T_{local}$ | seconds |
| 4 | Offload Time | $T_{offload} = \dfrac{D}{R_{up}} + \dfrac{W}{f_{edge}}$ | seconds |
| 5 | Local Compute Time | $T_{local} = \dfrac{W}{f_{local}}$ | seconds |
| 6 | Energy Saved (offload) | $E_{saved} = P_{local}\cdot T_{local} - (P_{tx}\cdot T_{tx}+P_{idle}\cdot T_{wait})$ | Joules |
| 7 | Reliability Bound | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | V2V Doppler Shift @ 5.9 GHz, 120 km/h | $\Delta f \approx 650$ Hz | Hz |
| 9 | Coverage Radius (RSU) | $r = \sqrt{h_{rsu}^{2} + 2R_{e}h_{rsu}} - R_{e}$ | metres |
| 10 | Trust Score (composite) | $T_{ij} = w_1 D_{ij} + w_2 R_{ij} + w_3 F_{ij}$ | $[0,1]$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive OEMs (Tata Motors, Tesla, Toyota):** Use Layer-3 fog nodes to run ADAS perception in < 10 ms.
- **Smart Cities (Kochi Smart City, Smart City Mission India):** Layer-2 + Layer-3 enable adaptive signal control using V2I.
- **Logistics (Flipkart, Delhivery fleets):** Layer-5 + Layer-6 enable dynamic routing and fuel-cost analytics.
- **Insurance (Acko, ICICI Lombard):** Layer-7 trust scores feed into usage-based insurance premiums.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Offloading Decision Threshold

We will derive the **latency-based offloading condition** used in fog-compute literature.

**Step 1 — Define the workload.**
Let a vehicle's task require $W$ CPU cycles to complete, and let the input data size be $D$ bits.

**Step 2 — Local execution time.**
If executed on the vehicle's On-Board Unit (OBU) of compute capacity $f_{local}$ cycles/s:

$$T_{local} = \frac{W}{f_{local}}$$

**Step 3 — Offload execution time.**
Uploading $D$ bits at uplink rate $R_{up}$ (bits/s) takes $\frac{D}{R_{up}}$ seconds. The edge node of capacity $f_{edge}$ needs $\frac{W}{f_{edge}}$ seconds to process. Total:

$$T_{offload} = \frac{D}{R_{up}} + \frac{W}{f_{edge}}$$

(We neglect downlink because the result $D'$ is tiny: e.g. brake command $\approx 64$ bits.)

**Step 4 — Offloading is beneficial iff:**

$$T_{offload} < T_{local}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the expressions:**

$$\frac{D}{R_{up}} + \frac{W}{f_{edge}} < \frac{W}{f_{local}}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to find the minimum required uplink rate:**

$$\frac{D}{R_{up}} < W\left(\frac{1}{f_{local}} - \frac{1}{f_{edge}}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $R_{up}$:**

$$R_{up} > \frac{D \cdot f_{local} \cdot f_{edge}}{W \cdot (f_{edge} - f_{local})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This inequality is the **designer's rule of thumb** for KTU problems: given a known task, compute the *minimum network* the system must guarantee for offloading to be worthwhile.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Composite Trust Score (Layer 7)

In vehicular networks, trust among vehicles is built from three signals: **direct observation**, **indirect reputation**, and **freshness**.

**Step 1 — Direct trust** $D_{ij}$ from vehicle $i$'s past interactions with $j$, normalised to $[0,1]$.

**Step 2 — Indirect trust** $R_{ij}$ aggregated from neighbouring vehicles' recommendations.

**Step 3 — Freshness** $F_{ij}$ is a decay function:

$$F_{ij}(t) = e^{-\lambda (t - t_{last})}$$

**Step 4 — Linear weighted combination:**

$$T_{ij} = w_1 \cdot D_{ij} + w_2 \cdot R_{ij} + w_3 \cdot F_{ij}$$

with $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$.

**Step 5 — Decision rule:** Vehicle $i$ accepts a message from $j$ **iff** $T_{ij} \geq \tau$, where $\tau$ is a threshold (e.g. 0.6).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Workload Placement Across Layers

| Question | Layer 1 | Layer 2 | Layer 3 (Fog) | Layer 4 | Layer 5 | Layer 6 | Layer 7 |
|----------|:-------:|:-------:|:-------------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|:-------:|
| Where does the LiDAR raw point cloud live? | ✅ | | | | | | |
| Where does the 5G sidelink packet header sit? | | ✅ | | | | | |
| Where is the object-detection CNN run? | | | ✅ | | | | |
| Where is gRPC service registration? | | | | ✅ | | | |
| Where is the infotainment UI rendered? | | | | | ✅ | | |
| Where is per-km billing computed? | | | | | | ✅ | |
| Where is the OTA signature verified? | | | | | | | ✅ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Seven-Layer Vertical Stack (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TB
    L7["LAYER 7 - Security and Trust"]
    L6["LAYER 6 - Business and Management"]
    L5["LAYER 5 - Application"]
    L4["LAYER 4 - Middleware and Service"]
    L3["LAYER 3 - Edge and Fog Computing"]
    L2["LAYER 2 - Network and Communication"]
    L1["LAYER 1 - Perception and Physical"]

    L7 --- L6
    L6 --- L5
    L5 --- L4
    L4 --- L3
    L3 --- L2
    L2 --- L1
```

## 4.2 Cross-Layer Interaction Map (V2X End-to-End Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VEH["VEHICLE DOMAIN"]
        V1["L1 Sensors LiDAR Radar Camera"]
        V2["L2 OBU Modem V2V V2I"]
        V3["L3 OBU Edge AI Inference"]
    end
    subgraph EDGE["EDGE FOG DOMAIN"]
        E1["L2 RSU 5G NR Sidelink"]
        E2["L3 MEC Server Container Cluster"]
        E3["L4 Service Bus and API Gateway"]
    end
    subgraph CLOUD["CLOUD BACKEND"]
        C1["L5 App Server ADAS Platoon Logic"]
        C2["L6 Billing and Fleet Manager"]
        C3["L7 PKI and Blockchain Ledger"]
    end

    V1 --> V2 --> V3
    V3 -->|Uplink| E1 --> E2 --> E3
    E3 --> C1 --> C2
    C3 -. secures .-> V3
    C3 -. secures .-> E2
    C3 -. secures .-> C1
```

## 4.3 Layer-Responsibility Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT Raw sensor data V2X packets User request"] --> B["LAYER 1 PERCEPTION"]
    B --> C["LAYER 2 NETWORK"]
    C --> D["LAYER 3 EDGE FOG"]
    D --> E["LAYER 4 MIDDLEWARE"]
    E --> F["LAYER 5 APPLICATION"]
    F --> G["LAYER 6 BUSINESS"]
    G --> H["OUTPUT SLA billing service decision"]

    S["LAYER 7 SECURITY TRUST Enforces all layers"] -.-> B
    S -.-> C
    S -.-> D
    S -.-> E
    S -.-> F
    S -.-> G
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram:** The vertical stack shows the *data plane flow* (bottom-up). The dashed lines from Layer 7 represent *control-plane / cross-cutting* enforcement — a classic KTU exam-point: *"Why is Security drawn as cross-layer?"*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3 Mark Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024 Model]**
*List any three characteristics that distinguish the IoV reference architecture from a generic 3-layer IoT model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Edge/Fog layer is explicit** — IoV is latency-sensitive (sub-10 ms), so compute must move to RSUs/MEC, unlike generic IoT which centralises at the cloud.
2. **Cross-cutting Security/Trust layer** — vehicular safety is life-critical, demanding PKI, blockchain-led trust, and intrusion detection as a first-class layer rather than an afterthought.
3. **High-mobility V2X communication** — uses IEEE 802.11p / C-V2X sidelink with handovers every few seconds, far more dynamic than stationary IoT links.
*(1 mark each)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*Define the Perception Layer and name two sensors commonly used in IoV-enabled vehicles.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Perception Layer is the **bottom-most physical plane** responsible for capturing raw data from the environment and the vehicle's own state using sensors such as **LiDAR** (light detection and ranging for 3-D mapping) and **Radar** (77 GHz radio detection for long-range object tracking). It also includes **cameras**, **ultrasonic sensors**, **GPS**, and **IMU**. *(1 mark definition + 2 marks examples)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Seven-Layered Reference Architecture of IoV**, clearly stating the responsibility of each layer. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vehicle task requires $W = 1 \times 10^9$ CPU cycles. The OBU compute capacity is $f_{local} = 1 \times 10^9$ cycles/s. The edge node has $f_{edge} = 5 \times 10^9$ cycles/s. The uplink rate is $R_{up} = 5 \times 10^6$ bits/s and the input data size is $D = 2.5 \times 10^6$ bits. Determine whether offloading is beneficial. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

Draw the vertical stack (similar to Section 4.1) and describe each layer:

1. **Layer 1 – Perception:** Collects data from LiDAR, radar, cameras, GPS, IMU, OBD-II. (1 mark)
2. **Layer 2 – Network:** V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N communication using DSRC / C-V2X. (1 mark)
3. **Layer 3 – Edge/Fog:** Local inference, data fusion, latency-critical decisions at RSUs/MEC. (1 mark)
4. **Layer 4 – Middleware:** Service abstraction, protocol translation, container orchestration. (1 mark)
5. **Layer 5 – Application:** ADAS, platooning, infotainment, predictive maintenance. (1 mark)
6. **Layer 6 – Business:** SLA, billing, fleet governance, regulatory compliance. (1 mark)
7. **Layer 7 – Security & Trust:** PKI, encryption, IDS, trust scoring. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $T_{local}$:**

$$T_{local} = \frac{W}{f_{local}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{9}}{1 \times 10^{9}} = 1\ \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute uplink time:**

$$T_{up} = \frac{D}{R_{up}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{6}}{5 \times 10^{6}} = 0.5\ \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute edge compute time:**

$$T_{edge} = \frac{W}{f_{edge}} = \frac{1 \times 10^{9}}{5 \times 10^{9}} = 0.2\ \text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Total offload time:**

$$T_{offload} = T_{up} + T_{edge} = 0.5 + 0.2 = 0.7\ \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Compare:**

$$T_{offload} = 0.7\ \text{s} \;<\; T_{local} = 1.0\ \text{s}$$

**Conclusion:** Offloading **is beneficial** — saves **0.3 s (30%)** of wall-clock time. *(7 marks: 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 3 for conclusion & interpretation)*

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model]`

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **role of the Edge/Fog Computing layer** in the IoV architecture. Why is it considered the "focal plane" for fog computing? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A vehicle moves at $v = 108\ \text{km/h}$ on a straight highway. A peer vehicle is approaching head-on at the same speed. The V2V communication uses $f_c = 5.9\ \text{GHz}$. Compute the **maximum Doppler shift** and comment on its impact on V2V channel coherence. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

- **Definition (2 marks):** The Edge/Fog layer is the distributed compute tier between the vehicle OBU and the remote cloud, typically hosted on **RSUs, MEC servers, or roadside micro-data-centres**.
- **Responsibilities (3 marks):**
  - Latency-critical inference (collision avoidance < 10 ms)
  - Local data fusion and pre-aggregation (reduces 4 TB/day to kB)
  - Workload offloading decisions
  - Privacy preservation (raw video never leaves the edge)
- **Why "focal plane" for fog computing (2 marks):** Because this is the layer that *embodies* the very definition of fog computing — *"compute where it is needed, close to the data source"*. Removing it collapses IoV to a cloud-only model that is unsafe for safety-critical vehicular workloads.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Step 1 — Convert velocity to m/s:**

$$v = 108\ \text{km/h} = 30\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Closing speed (head-on approach):**

$$v_{rel} = v_1 + v_2 = 30 + 30 = 60\ \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Doppler formula:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{v_{rel}}{c} \cdot f_c = \frac{60}{3 \times 10^{8}} \times 5.9 \times 10^{9}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$\Delta f = \frac{60 \times 5.9 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{354 \times 10^{9}}{3 \times 10^{8}} = 1180\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Comment (2 marks):** A Doppler shift of **1.18 kHz** at carrier **5.9 GHz** is significant. It causes fast channel fading (coherence time on the order of milliseconds), which is why IEEE 802.11p uses short OFDM symbols and C-V2X uses subframe-level reference signals to track the channel.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
> 1. **Drawing Security as the TOP layer only.** KTU expects it as a *cross-cutting* dashed line covering all layers. Wrong diagram = lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Always write *seconds*, *Hz*, *bits/s*. KTU examiners explicitly look for units.
> 3. **Confusing Edge and Fog.** Edge = single RSU/MEC. Fog = *cluster* of edge nodes working cooperatively. Marks are lost when these are used interchangeably.
> 4. **Skipping the conclusion in offload problems.** Writing only the numbers without stating *"offloading is beneficial because $T_{offload} < T_{local}$"* invites a 1-mark cut.
> 5. **Ignoring 5G NR-V2X sidelink terminology.** A bare mention of "4G" is outdated; Module-2 of the 2024 scheme emphasises 5G URLLC numerologies.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Seven layers:** Perception, Network, Edge/Fog, Middleware, Application, Business, Security (cross-cutting). ✅
- **V2X taxonomy:** V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2G, V2S. ✅
- **Edge ≠ Fog:** Edge is *one* RSU; Fog is a *cooperative cluster* of edges. ✅
- **Core offload equation:** $T_{offload} = \dfrac{D}{R_{up}} + \dfrac{W}{f_{edge}}$ vs. $T_{local} = \dfrac{W}{f_{local}}$. ✅
- **5G URLLC target:** **1 ms** latency, **99.999%** reliability. ✅
- **Security is cross-cutting**, not a vertical top layer. ✅
- **Sensors in Layer 1:** LiDAR, Radar, Camera, Ultrasonic, GPS, IMU, OBD-II. ✅
- **Trust Score formula:** $T_{ij} = w_1 D_{ij} + w_2 R_{ij} + w_3 F_{ij}$, with $\sum w_i = 1$. ✅
- **Doppler @ 5.9 GHz, 120 km/h closing:** ≈ **1.3 kHz**, demanding fast channel tracking. ✅
- **Real-world anchors to cite:** Tesla edge inference, Kochi Smart City V2I, Flipkart fleet tracking, ETSI MEC. ✅
- **One-line exam answer:**
  > *"IoV is a 7-layer cyber-physical system where Layer 3 (Edge/Fog) is the latency-critical brain, Layer 7 (Security) is the cross-cutting immune system, and Layers 1–2 are the senses and nerves."* ✅

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoV: Network Models, Challenges and Future Aspects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** IoV: Network Models, Challenges and Future Aspects # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Internet of Vehicles (IoV) — Formal Definition The **Internet of Vehicles (IoV)** is a distributed, heterogeneous network architecture that integrates vehicles, roadside infrastructure, pedestrians, cloud platforms, and edge/fog computing nodes into a unified cyber-physical system. It enables real-time data exchange, computational offloading, and intelligent decision-making using vehicular ad-hoc networks (VANETs), cellular networks, and short-range communication standards. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Exact):** IoV is defined as a dynamic, large-scale, multi-layer network that leverages **Vehicle-to-Everything (V2X)** communication, fog/edge computing, and AI to deliver safe, efficient, and comfortable intelligent transportation services. ## 1.2 The Five Pillars of V2X Communication | Pillar | Full Form | Communication With | |---|---|---| | **V2V** | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | Other cars/trucks/buses | | **V2I** | Vehicle-to-Infrastructure | Roadside Units (RSU), Traffic Lights | | **V2P** | Vehicle-to-Pedestrian | Smartphones of pedestrians/cyclists | | **V2N** | Vehicle-to-Network | Cellular base stations, Cloud | | **V2S** | Vehicle-to-Sensor | On-board IoT sensors, Lidar, Cameras | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Smartphones on Wheels" > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** Imagine your smartphone. It connects to Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, cellular towers, and other phones. Now, scale that to a vehicle moving at **120 km/h** on a highway, and replace the human with hundreds of embedded sensors (LIDAR, radar, GPS, cameras). That is IoV — a vehicle is essentially a **moving, high-velocity data center** that must talk to other vehicles, traffic signals, and the cloud within **milliseconds** to prevent accidents. ## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Highlighted for KTU) - **End-to-End Latency:** Target **< 10 ms** for safety-critical messages (5G NR-V2X). - **High Mobility:** Supports speeds up to **500 km/h** (high-speed trains). - **Communication Range:** Short-range (DSRC ~300m) vs Long-range (Cellular ~km). - **Message Frequency:** Safety beacons broadcast at **10 Hz** (every 100 ms). ## 1.5 Network Models — High-Level Taxonomy IoV network models are broadly classified as: 1. **Ad-hoc / Short-range Models** — WAVE, DSRC, IEEE 802.11p 2. **Cellular Models** — LTE-V2X (3GPP Release 14), 5G NR-V2X (3GPP Release 16/17) 3. **Hybrid Models** — Combining short-range + cellular (e.g., LTE + DSRC) 4. **Heterogeneous Models** — Integrating 5G, Wi-Fi 6, Satellite, and Edge/Fog 5. **Software-Defined Models** — SDVN (Software-Defined Vehicular Networks) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layered Architecture of IoV The IoV architecture is organized into **four logical layers**: ### Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer - Collects data via onboard units (OBU): **GPS, LIDAR, RADAR, cameras, OBD-II sensors**. - Generates **structured + unstructured data** (e.g., 25 GB/hour from a single autonomous vehicle). ### Layer 2 — Network / Communication Layer - Handles **V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N** communication. - Uses protocols: **IEEE 802.11p (DSRC), C-V2X, 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6**. - Performs routing, addressing, and message forwarding. ### Layer 3 — Fog/Edge Computing Layer (KTU Focus) - Deployed on **Roadside Units (RSUs)**, **MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing) servers**, and **in-vehicle fog nodes**. - Performs **localized processing** to reduce latency. - Acts as a **bridge** between vehicles and the cloud. ### Layer 4 — Cloud / Application Layer - Hosts **big data analytics, ML models, digital twins, and global traffic optimization**. - Stores historical data, performs fleet management, OTA updates. ## 2.2 Detailed Comparison of IoV Network Models > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** KTU frequently asks comparison questions between DSRC and C-V2X. Memorize the differences in **latency, range, deployment cost, and scalability**. | Feature | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | LTE-V2X (Rel. 14) | 5G NR-V2X (Rel. 16/17) | |---|---|---|---| | **Spectrum** | 5.9 GHz (Dedicated) | 5.9 GHz + Cellular | 5.9 GHz + mmWave | | **Latency** | ~4 ms | ~20-50 ms | **< 5 ms** (URLLC) | | **Range** | ~300 m | ~1 km | ~1-2 km | | **Data Rate** | 6-27 Mbps | 150 Mbps | **1 Gbps+** | | **Mobility Support** | Up to 200 km/h | Up to 350 km/h | **Up to 500 km/h** | | **Deployment Cost** | High (RSU density) | Medium (uses towers) | High (mmWave small cells) | | **Scalability** | Poor (CSMA/CA congestion) | Better (scheduled) | **Excellent (slicing + AI)** | | **Standardization** | Mature (US, EU legacy) | Mature (C-V2X) | Emerging (5GAA) | | **Why / How** | CSMA/CA carrier sensing | eNodeB-scheduled resources | URLLC + numerology flexibility | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Equations) | Symbol | Formula / Concept | Description | |---|---|---| | **Throughput** | $T = \frac{P \cdot L}{T_f}$ | $P$ = payload, $L$ = links, $T_f$ = frame time | | **End-to-End Latency** | $t_{e2e} = t_{tx} + t_{prop} + t_{queue} + t_{proc}$ | Sum of all delays | | **Propagation Delay** | $t_{prop} = \frac{d}{c}$ | $d$ = distance, $c$ = speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s) | | **Channel Capacity (Shannon)** | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $B$ = bandwidth, SNR = signal-to-noise ratio | | **Doppler Shift (V2V)** | $f_d = \frac{v \cdot f_c}{c} \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | $v$ = vehicle velocity, $f_c$ = carrier frequency | | **Packet Delivery Ratio (PDR)** | $\text{PDR} = \frac{P_{recv}}{P_{sent}} \times 100\%$ | Reliability metric | | **SINR (Signal to Interference + Noise)** | $\text{SINR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{interference} + P_{noise}}$ | Quality of V2X link | | **Vehicle Density (per km)** | $\rho = \frac{N}{L}$ | $N$ = vehicles, $L$ = road length (km) | | **Number of Messages/sec** | $\lambda = \rho \cdot f_b$ | $\rho$ = density, $f_b$ = beacon frequency | | **Fog Offloading Latency** | $t_{fog} = t_{uplink} + t_{compute} + t_{downlink}$ | Edge processing delay | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Trap:** Students often confuse $t_{prop}$ (propagation delay) with $t_{tx}$ (transmission delay). $t_{tx} = \frac{L_{packet}}{R_{bitrate}}$, while $t_{prop} = \frac{d}{c}$. They are fundamentally different. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Safety:** Collision avoidance via cooperative awareness messages (CAM) at 10 Hz. - **Efficiency:** Platooning reduces fuel consumption by **10-15%**. - **Autonomy:** L4/L5 self-driving cars depend on V2X for **sensor fusion** and **non-line-of-sight awareness**. - **Smart Cities:** IoV feeds real-time traffic data to municipal control centers. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Latency in a 5G NR-V2X Scenario **Problem Context:** A vehicle traveling at 120 km/h transmits a safety message to a nearby RSU. Compute the end-to-end latency. ### Given: - Distance $d = 200$ m - Packet size $L_p = 190$ bytes (typical CAM) - Bitrate $R = 27$ Mbps (DSRC reference for comparison) - Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - Processing delay $t_{proc} = 1$ ms - Queuing delay $t_{queue} = 0.5$ ms ### Step 1 — Compute Transmission Delay ($t_{tx}$) The transmission delay is the time to push the packet onto the medium. $$ t_{tx} = \frac{L_p}{R} $$ Substituting the values (convert units consistently): $$ t_{tx} = \frac{190 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{27 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} $$ Conversion logic: 1 byte = 8 bits, so we multiply by 8. Mbps = $10^6$ bits/s. $$ t_{tx} = \frac{1520 \text{ bits}}{27 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}} = 5.63 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} $$ $$ t_{tx} \approx 0.0563 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Propagation Delay ($t_{prop}$) The propagation delay is the time for the signal to travel through the medium. $$ t_{prop} = \frac{d}{c} $$ Substituting: $$ t_{prop} = \frac{200 \text{ m}}{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} = 6.67 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s} $$ $$ t_{prop} \approx 0.000667 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 3 — Sum All Delay Components $$ t_{e2e} = t_{tx} + t_{prop} + t_{queue} + t_{proc} $$ $$ t_{e2e} = 0.0563 + 0.000667 + 0.5 + 1.0 $$ $$ t_{e2e} = 1.557 \text{ ms} $$ ### Step 4 — Verdict The total end-to-end latency of **~1.56 ms** comfortably meets the **3GPP URLLC** requirement of **< 5 ms** for safety-critical V2X, confirming 5G NR-V2X suitability. ## 3.2 Derivation: Vehicle Density and Message Load **Problem Context:** On a 4-lane highway with average inter-vehicle gap of 30 m, compute the number of safety messages generated per second per km. ### Given: - Gap between vehicles $g = 30$ m - Beacon frequency $f_b = 10$ Hz - Number of lanes = 4 (assuming 1 direction, 4 lanes) ### Step 1 — Compute Vehicle Density per Lane $$ \rho = \frac{1 \text{ vehicle}}{30 \text{ m}} = \frac{1}{0.03 \text{ km}} = 33.33 \text{ vehicles/km} $$ ### Step 2 — Total Density Across 4 Lanes $$ \rho_{total} = 33.33 \times 4 = 133.33 \text{ vehicles/km} $$ ### Step 3 — Message Generation Rate $$ \lambda = \rho_{total} \times f_b $$ $$ \lambda = 133.33 \times 10 = 1333.33 \text{ messages/second/km} $$ ### Step 4 — Analytical Insight At this density, approximately **1333 CAM messages per second per km** flood the channel. This causes **congestion**, which is why DSRC (CSMA/CA based) struggles and **5G NR-V2X with scheduled resources** is preferred for dense urban scenarios. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — DSRC vs. C-V2X | Parameter | DSRC (802.11p) | C-V2X (5G NR) | Winner | |---|---|---|---| | **Channel Access** | CSMA/CA (contention-based) | Scheduled (gNB-coordinated) | C-V2X | | **Latency @ High Density** | Degrades sharply | Stable | C-V2X | | **Handover (Vehicles moving fast)** | Not applicable (ad-hoc) | Seamless via cellular | C-V2X | | **Security** | PKI-based (IEEE 1609.2) | SIM-based + PKI | Tie | | **Cost of Deployment** | Many RSUs needed | Reuses cellular infra | C-V2X | | **Maturity** | 20+ years of research | ~5 years of 5G maturity | DSRC | | **Backward Compatibility** | None | LTE-V2X compatible | C-V2X | | **KTU Recommended Answer** | Mention for legacy | **Emphasize as future** | C-V2X | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: IoV Layered Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB L4["LAYER 4: CLOUD AND APPLICATION LAYER
Big Data Analytics, Digital Twin, AI, OTA Updates"] L3["LAYER 3: FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING LAYER
RSU, MEC Servers, In-Vehicle Fog Nodes"] L2["LAYER 2: NETWORK AND COMMUNICATION LAYER
DSRC, C-V2X, 5G NR, Wi-Fi 6, LTE"] L1["LAYER 1: PERCEPTION AND SENSING LAYER
GPS, LIDAR, RADAR, Cameras, OBD-II"] L1 --> L2 L2 --> L3 L3 --> L4 L4 -.feedback.-> L3 L3 -.control.-> L2 ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: V2X Communication Types ```mermaid flowchart LR V[VEHICLE] V2[ANOTHER VEHICLE] RSU[ROADSIDE UNIT RSU] PED[PEDESTRIAN SMARTPHONE] NET[5G BASE STATION CLOUD] SENS[ONBOARD SENSORS LIDAR CAMERA] V -->|V2V| V2 V -->|V2I| RSU V -->|V2P| PED V -->|V2N| NET V -->|V2S| SENS RSU -->|V2N| NET ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: IoV Network Model Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TB IoV[IoV NETWORK MODELS] SR[SHORT RANGE MODELS] CEL[CELLULAR MODELS] HET[HETEROGENEOUS MODELS] SDN[SOFTWARE DEFINED MODELS] SR --> SR1[DSRC IEEE 802.11p] SR --> SR2[WAVE IEEE 1609] CEL --> CEL1[LTE V2X 3GPP Rel 14] CEL --> CEL2[5G NR V2X Rel 16 17] HET --> HET1[5G plus Wi-Fi 6 plus Satellite] SDN --> SDN1[SDVN with OpenFlow] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Challenges and Future Aspects ```mermaid flowchart TB CH[IoV CHALLENGES] F1[5G URLLC] F2[Edge AI Inference] F3[Blockchain Security] F4[Digital Twin Cities] F5[6G Terahertz] CH --> C1[Ultra Low Latency under 5 ms] CH --> C2[High Mobility and Handover] CH --> C3[Scalability in Dense Traffic] CH --> C4[Security and Privacy] CH --> C5[Data Heterogeneity] C1 --> F1 C2 --> F5 C3 --> F2 C4 --> F3 C5 --> F4 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)** ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` **Define the Internet of Vehicles (IoV). List any four V2X communication modes with one-line descriptions.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** The Internet of Vehicles (IoV) is a dynamic, heterogeneous network integrating vehicles, road infrastructure, pedestrians, networks, and sensors to enable intelligent transportation services using V2X communication, fog/edge computing, and AI. **V2X Modes (0.375 each = 1.5 Marks):** 1. **V2V (Vehicle-to-Vehicle):** Direct communication between nearby vehicles for cooperative awareness. 2. **V2I (Vehicle-to-Infrastructure):** Communication with RSUs and traffic signals. 3. **V2P (Vehicle-to-Pedestrian):** Alerts between vehicles and pedestrian smartphones. 4. **V2N (Vehicle-to-Network):** Communication with cellular base stations and cloud. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper]` **Differentiate between DSRC and C-V2X in terms of latency, spectrum, and scalability.** **Model Answer (3 Marks — 1 mark per point):** | Parameter | DSRC | C-V2X | |---|---|---| | **Latency** | ~4 ms (degrades in dense traffic) | **< 5 ms** (URLLC guaranteed) | | **Spectrum** | 5.9 GHz dedicated (75 MHz) | 5.9 GHz + licensed cellular bands | | **Scalability** | Poor (CSMA/CA congestion at high density) | **Excellent** (scheduled, network-sliced) | **Conclusion:** C-V2X is preferred for next-generation IoV due to better scalability and 3GPP ecosystem integration. --- ## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice** > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 mandates internal choice between two questions. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Explain the four-layer architecture of IoV with a neat diagram. Describe the role of the Fog/Edge Computing layer in reducing latency.** **Model Answer:** **Architecture Layers (4 Marks):** 1. **Perception Layer:** Collects data via OBU sensors — GPS, LIDAR, RADAR, cameras, OBD-II. Outputs include speed, location, obstacle detection. 2. **Network Layer:** Handles V2V/V2I/V2N communication using DSRC, C-V2X, 5G NR, and LTE. Performs routing, addressing, and message forwarding. 3. **Fog/Edge Computing Layer:** Provides localized computation at RSUs and MEC servers. Reduces dependence on distant cloud. 4. **Cloud/Application Layer:** Hosts big-data analytics, ML models, digital twins, and global fleet management. **Role of Fog/Edge in Latency Reduction (3 Marks):** - **Local processing:** Safety-critical decisions are made at RSUs within **1-5 ms**, avoiding the **50-100 ms** round-trip to the cloud. - **Pre-filtering:** Only critical events are forwarded to the cloud, reducing **uplink bandwidth by 70%**. - **Predictive caching:** Pre-fetches navigation and map data based on route prediction. - **Vehicle clustering:** Fog nodes aggregate data from 50-100 nearby vehicles before cloud upload. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Compare DSRC and C-V2X network models across 6 parameters. Justify which is more suitable for future IoV deployments.** **Model Answer (Tabular comparison, 6 parameters × 1 mark = 6 Marks + Justification = 1 Mark):** | Parameter | DSRC (IEEE 802.11p) | C-V2X (5G NR) | |---|---|---| | **Latency** | 4 ms (degrades with density) | < 5 ms (URLLC stable) | | **Range** | ~300 m | ~1-2 km | | **Data Rate** | 6-27 Mbps | Up to 1 Gbps | | **Channel Access** | CSMA/CA (contention) | Scheduled (synchronous) | | **Deployment Cost** | High (dense RSU grid) | Medium (reuses cellular) | | **Future Roadmap** | Limited (no 5G evolution) | Continuous (3GPP Rel. 16/17/18) | **Justification (1 Mark):** **C-V2X is more suitable** because it leverages the **5G/6G cellular ecosystem**, supports **URLLC for safety**, offers **network slicing for differentiated services**, and reuses **existing cellular infrastructure**, reducing CAPEX for smart city deployments. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Discuss the major challenges in IoV deployment with respect to latency, mobility, scalability, security, and data heterogeneity. Propose a fog/edge-based solution for each challenge.** **Model Answer (5 challenges × 1.2 Marks each = 6 Marks + Conclusion = 1 Mark):** | Challenge | Description | Fog/Edge Solution | |---|---|---| | **1. Ultra-low Latency** | Safety messages must reach receivers in < 5 ms. Cloud round-trip (~50 ms) is too slow. | **MEC servers at RSUs** process safety data locally within 1-3 ms. | | **2. High Mobility** | Vehicles move at 120+ km/h causing frequent handovers and Doppler shifts. | **5G NR-V2X** with numerology adaptation + **predictive handover** using AI at edge. | | **3. Scalability** | Thousands of vehicles per km² flood the channel with CAM/DENM messages. | **Edge aggregation**: Fog nodes cluster vehicle data before forwarding to cloud. | | **4. Security & Privacy** | V2X messages can be spoofed; vehicle tracking violates privacy. | **Blockchain + PKI at fog** for tamper-proof authentication; **pseudonymous IDs**. | | **5. Data Heterogeneity** | Mix of structured (GPS) and unstructured (LIDAR point clouds, video) data. | **Edge AI** performs **federated learning** to extract features locally and share only models. | **Conclusion (1 Mark):** A **multi-tier fog/edge architecture** (vehicle → RSU → MEC → Cloud) is essential to address the latency, scalability, and security triad of IoV. #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Explain the future aspects of IoV: 5G/6G, Edge AI, Digital Twins, and Blockchain. How do they integrate to enable autonomous driving?** **Model Answer:** **1. 5G/6G Communication (2 Marks):** - **5G NR-V2X** provides **URLLC (< 5 ms)**, **eMBB (1 Gbps)**, and **mMTC** for massive vehicle density. - **6G (2030+)** will introduce **Terahertz (THz) communication**, **AI-native air interface**, and **sub-ms latency** for L5 autonomy. **2. Edge AI (2 Marks):** - AI inference (object detection, path planning) runs on **vehicle ECUs** and **RSU GPUs**. - **Federated learning** trains global models without sharing raw data, preserving privacy. **3. Digital Twins (1.5 Marks):** - A **virtual replica** of the city/traffic is maintained in the cloud. - Simulates "what-if" scenarios for autonomous vehicles before real-world execution. **4. Blockchain (1.5 Marks):** - Provides **immutable, decentralized trust** for V2X message authentication. - **Smart contracts** automate tolling, insurance, and accident claims. **Integration Diagram (Implicit):** A 6G-connected autonomous vehicle uses **Edge AI** for local decisions, **Digital Twin** for route simulation, and **Blockchain** to trust messages from other vehicles — all orchestrated by a **fog/edge computing layer**. --- ## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in IoV Questions:** > > 1. **Confusing V2X modes:** Students write "V2V" when they mean "V2I" — a **1-mark loss** per error. > 2. **No diagram in architecture questions:** Always draw a **labeled block diagram** of the 4 layers. No diagram = **2-3 marks deducted**. > 3. **Skipping units in formulas:** Writing $t_{prop} = d/c$ without specifying $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s loses **0.5 marks**. > 4. **Not justifying comparisons:** Merely tabulating DSRC vs C-V2X is incomplete. Always add a **1-line "Justification"** at the end. > 5. **Ignoring fog/edge layer:** The course is **PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING**. Every IoV answer must emphasize the **fog/edge layer's role** — otherwise 20-30% marks lost. --- ## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU Exam):** - ✅ **IoV Definition** = dynamic, heterogeneous, multi-layer vehicular network using V2X + fog/edge + AI. - ✅ **5 V2X Modes** = V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2S (V2S = onboard sensors). - ✅ **4 Architecture Layers** = Perception → Network → Fog/Edge → Cloud. - ✅ **DSRC vs C-V2X** = DSRC uses CSMA/CA at 5.9 GHz; C-V2X uses scheduled cellular with URLLC. - ✅ **5G NR-V2X latency** = < 5 ms (URLLC); **Data rate** = up to 1 Gbps. - ✅ **Key formulas** = $t_{e2e} = t_{tx} + t_{prop} + t_{proc} + t_{queue}$; $t_{prop} = d/c$; $C = B \log_2(1+\text{SNR})$. - ✅ **Beacon frequency** = 10 Hz (every 100 ms) for CAM messages. - ✅ **Fog benefits** = Local processing (1-3 ms), pre-filtering (70% bandwidth saved), predictive caching. - ✅ **5 Major Challenges** = Latency, Mobility, Scalability, Security, Data Heterogeneity. - ✅ **4 Future Aspects** = 5G/6G, Edge AI, Digital Twins, Blockchain. - ✅ **Standards bodies** = 3GPP (C-V2X), IEEE (802.11p/1609), ETSI (ITS-G5), 5GAA. - ✅ **Mandatory KTU emphasis** = Always link IoV to **fog/edge computing** (it's the course theme!).

topics.title: Middleware for Fog and Edge Computing: Design Issues, Need for Fog and Edge Computing Middleware
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Middleware for Fog and Edge Computing: Design Issues, Need for Fog and Edge Computing Middleware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Middleware in Fog/Edge Context

**Middleware** is a **distributed software layer** (or a set of cooperating software agents/services) that sits *abstractly* between the heterogeneous physical hardware (sensors, actuators, IoT devices) at the **Edge layer**, the **Fog layer** (intermediate compute nodes), and the centralized **Cloud layer**. Its primary role is to provide **uniform, abstracted, and transparent mechanisms** for application development, communication, resource management, data offloading, security, and context-awareness, while hiding the underlying complexity of diverse hardware, communication protocols, and network topologies.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** terminology for PECIT867, middleware is treated as the *"nervous system"* of the Fog–Edge continuum, enabling:
- **Hardware abstraction** (device-agnostic programming)
- **Protocol interoperability** (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP translation)
- **Context-awareness** (location, network state, battery, user preference)
- **QoS enforcement** (latency, jitter, reliability, throughput)
- **Dynamic orchestration** of compute, storage, and networking resources

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Airport Translator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: International Airport Translator**
> Imagine you arrive at a massive international airport (the **IoT/data network**). You speak Malayalam, the airline staff speak English, the ground crew speak Japanese, and the pilot speaks German. Without a common layer of communication, the airport collapses into chaos. The **airport translator desks (middleware)** sit at every gate and counter. They:
> - **Translate** (Protocol translation: MQTT ↔ CoAP ↔ HTTP)
> - **Route** you to the right gate (Task offloading: Edge → Fog → Cloud)
> - **Track** your luggage through scanners (Sensing/actuation abstraction)
> - **Prioritize** VIPs and emergency cases (QoS prioritization)
> - **Remember** your preferences (Context-awareness: e.g., veg meal, window seat)
>
> The middleware in Fog/Edge is **exactly this translator-and-coordinator** — it bridges the heterogeneity between the chaotic, diverse Edge devices and the structured Cloud backend.

### 1.3 Why Fog/Edge Middleware is Different from Classical Middleware

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (KTU High-Yield Point)**
> Classical middleware (CORBA, DCOM, Java RMI, Enterprise Service Bus) was designed for **stable, well-connected, homogeneous enterprise networks**. Fog/Edge middleware must additionally handle:
> - **Resource-constrained devices** (few KB RAM, mAh-level batteries)
> - **Intermittent, lossy connectivity** (LPWAN, Wi-Fi handoffs, 5G slicing)
> - **Mobility** (vehicular edge, drone swarms)
> - **Heterogeneous protocols** (BLE, Zigbee, LoRa, Wi-Fi, 5G, MQTT, CoAP)
> - **Real-time, latency-critical** workloads (autonomous driving, AR/VR, industrial control)
> - **Massive scale** (millions of geographically distributed nodes)
> - **Security in untrusted physical environments** (edge gateways in public spaces)

### 1.4 Formal Three-Layer Architectural Position

The middleware occupies the **abstraction plane** between:
- **Below it:** Physical/Virtual infrastructure (sensors, actuators, gateways, fog nodes)
- **Above it:** Application logic (analytics, ML inference, dashboards, control loops)
- **Lateral:** Peer middleware instances on neighboring fog/edge nodes (federation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard)**
> *Fog/Edge Middleware* is defined as a *"context-aware software fabric that abstracts heterogeneous distributed infrastructure (Edge–Fog–Cloud) and provides unified APIs, orchestration, and QoS guarantees to upper-layer applications, while handling heterogeneity, mobility, and resource constraints transparently."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Need for Fog and Edge Computing Middleware — The Six Pillars

The need for middleware arises because **raw, un-mediated Edge/Fog environments are too chaotic, heterogeneous, and unreliable** to support modern applications directly. The six core needs are:

| # | Need | Why It Cannot Be Solved Without Middleware | KTU Mark-Worthy Keywords |
|---|------|---------------------------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Heterogeneity Abstraction** | Edge devices run RTOS, Linux, Contiki, Zephyr, Android Things — with no common API. Middleware provides a **Device Abstraction Layer (DAL)**. | DAL, HAL, virtualization, containerization |
| 2 | **Interoperability** | Devices use MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, HTTP, DDS, OPC-UA. Apps need a *single* communication paradigm. Middleware acts as a **Protocol Gateway/Broker**. | Protocol translation, semantic mapping |
| 3 | **Context-Awareness** | Decisions (e.g., where to process a video frame) depend on location, network RTT, battery, user activity. Middleware **collects and reasons** on context. | Context acquisition, reasoning, propagation |
| 4 | **QoS & Real-Time Guarantees** | Hard real-time (control loops) vs. soft real-time (video) need differentiated handling. Middleware enforces **priority queues, schedulers, admission control**. | Latency budget, deadline scheduling |
| 5 | **Resource Orchestration & Offloading** | Where should this task run — sensor, gateway, fog node, or cloud? Middleware decides via **computational offloading algorithms**. | Task placement, VM/container migration |
| 6 | **Security, Privacy & Trust** | Edge nodes in public spaces are physically tamperable. Middleware enforces **authentication, encryption, sandboxing, attestation**. | TPM, secure boot, federated identity |

### 2.2 Functional Architecture of Fog/Edge Middleware (Layered View)

A canonical Fog/Edge middleware stacks in **five vertical layers** (bottom-up):

1. **Hardware/Network Layer** — physical sensors, actuators, radios (BLE, LoRa, Wi-Fi, 5G)
2. **Device Abstraction Layer (DAL)** — virtualizes sensors as "logical sensors" with uniform APIs
3. **Communication Layer** — protocol brokers, message routing, pub/sub semantics
4. **Management Layer** — context manager, QoS manager, security manager, resource manager
5. **Application Layer** — user-facing analytics, dashboards, ML inference services

### 2.3 Design Issues in Fog/Edge Middleware (KTU High-Yield)

The **design issues** are the most examination-relevant sub-topic. They are categorized into eight critical dimensions:

| Design Issue | Description | Trade-off / Challenge |
|--------------|-------------|------------------------|
