- ✅ Middleware = software layer between hardware and apps; provides abstraction, communication, context, security, orchestration.
- ✅ 12 design goals: Heterogeneity, Scalability, Real-time, Mobility, Location Awareness, Interoperability, Security, Resource Constraint, Fault Tolerance, Energy Efficiency, Context Awareness, Orchestration.
- ✅ Amdahl's Law: $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$ ; Max speedup = $\dfrac{1}{1-P}$.
- ✅ End-to-end latency: $L_{e2e} = L_{prop} + L_{trans} + L_{queue} + L_{proc}$.
- ✅ Availability: $A = \dfrac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$.
- ✅ OpenFog 8 Pillars: Security, Scalability, Openness, Autonomy, RAS, Agility, Hierarchical Organization, Programmability.
- ✅ IEEE 1934-2018 = OpenFog standard.

#### **State-of-the-Art Middleware — Quick Map**

- **Cisco IOx** → Industrial, IOS + Linux, Fog Director.
- **AWS Greengrass** → Lambda at edge, device shadows, mTLS.
- **Azure IoT Edge** → Docker containers, modules, Moby engine.
- **Eclipse Kura** → Java/OSGi gateway, Kura Wires.
- **Eclipse OM2M** → OneM2M implementation, hierarchical CSE.
- **Eclipse Hono** → Kafka-based telemetry, massive scale.
- **Eclipse Ditto** → Digital twin framework.
- **OpenFog Architecture** → IEEE 1934-2018, 8 pillars.
- **FogFlow** → NGSI-LD orchestration, smart city.
- **DOMINO** → In-network analytics, IIoT.

#### **Standard Metrics & Units (Always Quote in Answers)**

- **Latency:** **ms** (Fog: 1–10 ms; Cloud: 50–200 ms)
- **Jitter:** **ms**
- **Bandwidth:** **Mbps** / **Gbps**
- **Availability:** **%** or **nines** (5 nines = 99.999%)
- **MTBF:** **hours**
- **MTTR:** **minutes**
- **Power:** **Watts (W)**
- **Throughput:** **events/sec**

#### **Numericals to Practice (Must-Do Before Exam)**

1. Amdahl's Law with $P = 0.8, 0.9, 0.95$ for $N = 4, 8, 16, 32$.
2. End-to-end latency with given $d, R, \lambda, \mu$.
3. Availability in nines for given MTBF/MTTR.
4. Annual downtime calculation.

#### **High-Weightage Exam Topics**

- ⭐⭐⭐ Design goals of fog middleware (always asked).
- ⭐⭐⭐ OpenFog 8 Pillars (very frequently asked).
- ⭐⭐ AWS Greengrass / Azure IoT Edge architecture.
- ⭐⭐ Amdahl's Law numerical.
- ⭐ State-of-the-Art middleware comparison.

#### **Sample Sentence Bank (Use These Phrases in Answers)**

- *"The middleware abstracts the heterogeneity of underlying edge devices by providing a uniform API..."*
- *"Real-time awareness is achieved by placing time-critical workloads on fog nodes close to the data source..."*
- *"The OpenFog Reference Architecture, adopted as IEEE 1934-2018, prescribes 8 foundational pillars..."*
- *"AWS Greengrass extends the AWS cloud to the local network, enabling offline operation and reduced latency..."*
- *"Amdahl's Law reveals that even with infinite nodes, the maximum speedup is bounded by the sequential fraction..."*

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 2: Design Goals and State-of-the-Art Middleware Infrastructures**
> **Good Luck for your KTU Examinations! 🎓**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Fog and Edge Computing**, a **System Model** is a formal, abstract representation that defines the **hierarchical architecture**, **components**, **interconnections**, **resource capabilities**, and **operational assumptions** of the distributed computing continuum spanning from end-user IoT devices (Edge) through intermediate fog nodes up to the centralized Cloud data centers.

The KTU 2024 Scheme defines the System Model as a **three-tier (or multi-tier) computational paradigm** comprising:

- **Tier 1 – Edge Layer:** Resource-constrained end devices (sensors, actuators, smartphones, gateways, embedded systems) closest to the data source.
- **Tier 2 – Fog Layer:** Intermediate compute nodes (routers, switches, micro-data centers, fog servers) providing compute, storage, and networking between Edge and Cloud.
- **Tier 3 – Cloud Layer:** High-performance, centralized data centers offering virtually unlimited storage and computational power.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECIT867 Module 2):**
> The System Model topic establishes the **mathematical and architectural framework** used to analyze latency, energy consumption, bandwidth, task offloading decisions, and Quality of Service (QoS) across the Edge-Fog-Cloud continuum.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the Fog-Edge-Cloud hierarchy as the **human body**:

| Body Part | Computing Equivalent | Function |
|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| **Senses (eyes, ears, skin)** | IoT sensors / Edge devices | Detect raw data from the environment |
| **Spinal Cord & Reflexes** | Edge gateways / Local processors | Immediate, real-time response (sub-second) |
| **Brain (localized processing)** | Fog nodes | Quick decision-making, aggregation, filtering |
| **Long-term memory & deep analysis** | Cloud data centers | Heavy computation, archival storage, big-data analytics |

Just as a reflex (e.g., pulling your hand from a hot stove) doesn't wait for the brain to deliberate, **time-critical tasks at the edge do not wait for the cloud**. The system model formalizes this **latency-driven distribution of intelligence**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The System Model is *not* a physical blueprint — it is a **mathematical abstraction** that allows engineers to *predict performance* (latency, energy, throughput) and *optimize task placement* across the hierarchy.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in the System Model

- **End-to-End Latency (L):** Total time from data generation to response, typically measured in **milliseconds (ms)**.
- **Bandwidth (B):** Available network capacity, measured in **Mbps** or **Gbps**.
- **Computational Capacity (C):** Processing power in **FLOPS** or **CPU/GPU cycles per second**.
- **Storage (S):** Disk capacity in **GB** or **TB**.
- **Energy Consumption (E):** Power used per task, in **Joules (J)** or **Watts (W)**.

> [!TIP]
> Memorize these five metrics — every KTU numerical problem on the System Model involves optimizing one or more of them.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Architecture Decomposition

The System Model is decomposed into **four interacting sub-models**, each addressing a specific concern:

### 2.1.1 Network Model
Defines the **topology** and **connectivity** between tiers. The standard model is a **layered mesh** where:
- Each edge device connects to **one or more fog nodes**.
- Each fog node connects to the cloud and other fog nodes (peering).
- Communication can be **wired (Ethernet, fiber)** or **wireless (Wi-Fi, 5G, LTE, ZigBee, LoRaWAN)**.

### 2.1.2 Computational Model
Defines the **compute resources** available at each tier:

| Tier | CPU/GPU | Memory | Storage | Typical Hardware |
|------|---------|--------|---------|------------------|
| **Edge** | Low (e.g., ARM Cortex, 1-4 cores) | 256 MB – 4 GB | < 32 GB | Raspberry Pi, microcontrollers |
| **Fog** | Medium (e.g., x86 server, 8-32 cores) | 16 GB – 128 GB | 1-10 TB | Industrial PCs, MEC servers |
| **Cloud** | Very high (cluster of thousands of cores) | TBs of RAM | PBs | Hyperscale data centers |

### 2.1.3 Storage Model
Defines **where data is stored**:
- **Edge storage:** Transient, real-time data, time-to-live (TTL) in seconds/minutes.
- **Fog storage:** Aggregated, filtered data, TTL in hours/days.
- **Cloud storage:** Long-term archival, big-data lakes, historical analytics.

### 2.1.4 Task/Application Model
Each computational task $\mathcal{T}_i$ is characterized by the tuple:

$$\mathcal{T}_i = (c_i, \ d_i, \ \tau_i, \ \rho_i)$$

Where:
- $c_i$ = computational load (CPU cycles required)
- $d_i$ = data size to be processed (bits)
- $\tau_i$ = maximum tolerable latency (deadline)
- $\rho_i$ = priority level

## 2.2 The Three-Tier Latency Model (Step-by-Step Logic)

The end-to-end latency $L_{total}$ for a task processed at tier $k$ is:

$$L_{total} = L_{trans}^{up} + L_{queue} + L_{proc} + L_{trans}^{down}$$

**Step 1 — Uplink Transmission Latency** (from edge to processing tier):
$$L_{trans}^{up} = \frac{d_i}{R_{up}}$$
Where $R_{up}$ is the uplink data rate.

**Step 2 — Queuing Latency** (waiting for processing):
$$L_{queue} = \frac{\rho_{server}}{1 - \rho_{server}} \cdot \frac{1}{\mu}$$
Where $\rho_{server}$ is server utilization and $\mu$ is service rate (M/M/1 queue approximation).

**Step 3 — Processing Latency** (actual computation):
$$L_{proc} = \frac{c_i}{f_k}$$
Where $f_k$ is the CPU clock speed of tier $k$.

**Step 4 — Downlink Transmission Latency** (result back to user):
$$L_{trans}^{down} = \frac{d_{result}}{R_{down}}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $L_{trans} = \dfrac{d}{R}$ | Transmission latency | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $L_{proc} = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Processing latency | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $L_{prop} = \dfrac{s}{v}$ | Propagation latency ($s$=distance, $v$=signal speed) | seconds (s) |
| 4 | $E_{total} = E_{trans} + E_{proc} + E_{idle}$ | Total energy per task | Joules (J) |
| 5 | $E_{trans} = P_{tx} \cdot L_{trans}$ | Transmission energy | Joules (J) |
| 6 | $E_{proc} = P_{proc} \cdot L_{proc}$ | Computation energy | Joules (J) |
| 7 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{total}}$ | Energy efficiency ratio | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\rho_{server} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | Server utilization ($\lambda$ = arrival rate) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $L_{queue} = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)}$ | M/M/1 queuing latency | seconds (s) |
| 10 | $L_{total} = L_{trans} + L_{queue} + L_{proc}$ | Total task latency | seconds (s) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget the **propagation latency** term in long-distance cloud transmissions. For Cloud, propagation can be **50-100 ms** (transcontinental), which often exceeds the processing time itself!

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The System Model is the **bedrock of every fog/edge research paper and product**:

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Real-time object detection *must* be processed at the edge (latency budget ~10 ms). The system model determines which tasks go to onboard ECU vs. road-side unit (RSU/fog) vs. cloud (HD map updates).
- **Smart Healthcare (IoMT):** Wearable ECG monitors process arrhythmia detection at the edge and offload aggregated trends to the cloud.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance uses fog nodes to aggregate vibration/temperature data from hundreds of sensors and pushes only anomalies to the cloud.
- **Smart Cities:** Traffic management uses fog for intersection-level coordination and cloud for city-wide optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why:** The system model exists because **naïve cloud-only architectures fail** in latency-critical, bandwidth-limited, or energy-constrained scenarios. It quantifies the trade-offs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: End-to-End Latency for an Edge-Processed Task

**Problem Setup:** A surveillance camera captures a 5 MB image and needs intrusion detection. We want to compute the total latency if the task is processed **locally at the edge** versus **offloaded to a fog node**.

### Given Values
- Image size: $d = 5$ MB $= 5 \times 2^{20}$ bytes $= 5 \times 8 \times 10^6$ bits $= 4 \times 10^7$ bits
- Edge CPU clock: $f_{edge} = 1$ GHz $= 10^9$ cycles/s
- CPU cycles needed: $c = 10^9$ cycles (1 billion cycles for CNN inference)
- Fog node clock: $f_{fog} = 3$ GHz $= 3 \times 10^9$ cycles/s
- Uplink rate to fog: $R_{up} = 100$ Mbps $= 10^8$ bps
- Downlink rate: $R_{down} = 100$ Mbps

### Part A: Edge Processing (Local)

**Step 1:** Compute transmission latency. Since the task is local, no transmission is needed.

$$L_{trans}^{up} = 0 \ \text{s}, \quad L_{trans}^{down} = 0 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute processing latency at edge.

$$L_{proc}^{edge} = \frac{c}{f_{edge}} = \frac{10^9}{10^9} = 1 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Total latency.

$$L_{edge} = 0 + 0 + 1 + 0 = 1 \ \text{s}$$

### Part B: Fog Offloading

**Step 1:** Compute uplink transmission latency.

$$L_{trans}^{up} = \frac{d}{R_{up}} = \frac{4 \times 10^7}{10^8} = 0.4 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute processing latency at fog.

$$L_{proc}^{fog} = \frac{c}{f_{fog}} = \frac{10^9}{3 \times 10^9} = 0.333 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Compute downlink transmission latency (assume result is small, $d_{result} = 10$ KB $= 8 \times 10^4$ bits).

$$L_{trans}^{down} = \frac{8 \times 10^4}{10^8} = 8 \times 10^{-4} = 0.0008 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 4:** Total latency.

$$L_{fog} = 0.4 + 0.333 + 0.0008 = 0.7338 \ \text{s} \approx 0.734 \ \text{s}$$

### Comparison Table

| Metric | Edge Processing | Fog Offloading | Winner |
|--------|----------------|----------------|--------|
| **Total Latency** | 1.000 s | 0.734 s | **Fog** ✓ |
| **Bandwidth Used** | 0 bps | 40 MB | Edge |
| **Edge Energy** | High (full processing) | Low (only transmit) | Fog |
| **Privacy** | High (local) | Medium (transmitted) | Edge |

**Conclusion:** In this case, fog offloading wins on latency because the transmission time (0.4 s) is offset by the faster fog CPU (3x speedup).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy Consumption Model

**Problem:** Compare energy consumption of edge vs. fog for the same task.

### Given
- Edge transmit power: $P_{tx,edge} = 0.5$ W
- Edge processing power: $P_{proc,edge} = 2$ W
- Fog uplink transmit power: $P_{tx,fog} = 1$ W
- Fog node processing power: $P_{proc,fog} = 5$ W (server-grade, higher absolute power)

### Part A: Edge Energy (Process Locally)

**Step 1:** No transmission, so $E_{trans} = 0$.

**Step 2:** Processing energy.

$$E_{proc}^{edge} = P_{proc,edge} \cdot L_{proc}^{edge} = 2 \ \text{W} \times 1 \ \text{s} = 2 \ \text{J}$$

**Step 3:** Total edge energy.

$$E_{edge} = 0 + 2 = 2 \ \text{J}$$

### Part B: Fog Energy (From Edge Device's Perspective)

**Step 1:** Transmission energy (for uploading 5 MB).

$$E_{trans}^{fog} = P_{tx,fog} \cdot L_{trans}^{up} = 1 \ \text{W} \times 0.4 \ \text{s} = 0.4 \ \text{J}$$

**Step 2:** Edge idle energy during fog processing (assume edge sleeps).

$$E_{idle} = P_{sleep} \cdot L_{proc}^{fog} = 0.05 \ \text{W} \times 0.333 \ \text{s} = 0.0167 \ \text{J}$$

**Step 3:** Total fog path energy (from edge's perspective).

$$E_{fog} = 0.4 + 0.0167 + (\text{cloud-side not counted}) = 0.417 \ \text{J}$$

### Energy Comparison

$$E_{edge} = 2.00 \ \text{J}, \quad E_{fog} = 0.417 \ \text{J}$$

$$E_{edge} - E_{fog} = 1.583 \ \text{J} \quad \text{(edge consumes 4.8× more energy)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the central reason why **IoT devices offload heavy tasks** — the transmit power of a wireless radio is much less than the processing power of a CPU doing CNN inference.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Edge vs. Fog vs. Cloud

The decision rule is governed by the **latency deadline** $\tau_i$:

$$
\text{Choose tier } k^* = \arg\min_{k \in \{edge, fog, cloud\}} \left( L_k \right) \quad \text{subject to} \quad L_k \leq \tau_i
$$

| Scenario | Latency Deadline $\tau_i$ | Best Choice | Reason |
|----------|--------------------------|-------------|--------|
| Vehicle collision avoidance | ~10 ms | **Edge** | Cloud round-trip is ~100 ms; too slow |
| Smart home automation | ~100 ms | **Fog / Edge** | Acceptable, depends on data size |
| Daily weather aggregation | ~hours | **Cloud** | Latency irrelevant; compute-intensive |
| AR/VR rendering | ~20 ms | **Edge + Fog** | Tight deadline, heavy compute |
| Video analytics (batch) | ~minutes | **Fog + Cloud** | Bulk data, can be aggregated |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of Thumb:** If $\tau_i < 20$ ms, **must use edge**. If $20 \le \tau_i \le 500$ ms, **fog is optimal**. If $\tau_i > 500$ ms, **cloud is acceptable**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Tier System Model Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CLOUD["TIER 3 CLOUD LAYER"]
        C1[Cloud Data Center 1]
        C2[Cloud Data Center 2]
        C3[Big Data Analytics]
        C4[Long Term Storage]
    end

    subgraph FOG["TIER 2 FOG LAYER"]
        F1[Fog Node A MEC]
        F2[Fog Node B Gateway]
        F3[Fog Node C RSU]
        F4[Fog Orchestrator]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["TIER 1 EDGE LAYER"]
        E1[IoT Sensors]
        E2[Smartphone]
        E3[Smart Camera]
        E4[Vehicular ECU]
        E5[Embedded MCU]
    end

    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F2
    E3 --> F2
    E4 --> F3
    E5 --> F1

    F1 --> C1
    F2 --> C2
    F3 --> C1
    F4 --> C3
    F4 --> C4

    F1 --- F2
    F2 --- F3
    F3 --- F1

    C1 --- C2
```

## 4.2 Task Offloading Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Task Arrives at Edge Device] --> B{Check Latency Deadline Tau}
    B -->|Tau less than 20 ms| C[Process at EDGE]
    B -->|20 to 500 ms| D{Data Size Check}
    B -->|Tau more than 500 ms| E[Offload to CLOUD]

    D -->|Small Data| F[Process at FOG]
    D -->|Large Data| G{Energy Budget Check}

    G -->|Low Energy| H[Process at EDGE]
    G -->|Adequate Energy| F

    C --> I[Return Result to User]
    F --> I
    E --> I
    H --> I

    I --> J[Log Metrics for Optimization]
    J --> K[Update Offloading Policy]
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture of the System Model

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT DOMAIN"]
        I1[Data Acquisition]
        I2[Preprocessing]
        I3[Task Profiling]
    end

    subgraph DECISION["DECISION ENGINE"]
        D1[Latency Estimator]
        D2[Energy Estimator]
        D3[Network Monitor]
        D4[Policy Selector]
    end

    subgraph EXECUTION["EXECUTION TIERS"]
        EX1[Edge Processor]
        EX2[Fog Processor]
        EX3[Cloud Processor]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT DOMAIN"]
        O1[Real Time Response]
        O2[Aggregated Insights]
        O3[Long Term Analytics]
    end

    I1 --> I2 --> I3
    I3 --> D1
    I3 --> D2
    I3 --> D3
    D1 --> D4
    D2 --> D4
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> EX1
    D4 --> EX2
    D4 --> EX3
    EX1 --> O1
    EX2 --> O2
    EX3 --> O3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - **Figure 4.1** shows the static three-tier hierarchy and inter-tier links.
> - **Figure 4.2** shows the dynamic *task routing logic* based on deadlines and data size.
> - **Figure 4.3** shows the *functional pipeline* — data in, decision made, task executed, output delivered.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** Define the System Model in Fog and Edge Computing. List the three hierarchical tiers with one example device/node per tier.

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **System Model** in Fog and Edge Computing is a formal abstraction of the distributed computing continuum that defines the **components, connectivity, resources, and operational assumptions** for processing tasks across Edge, Fog, and Cloud tiers.

The three tiers are:

1. **Edge Tier** — Resource-constrained devices closest to data sources. *Example:* IoT temperature sensor, Raspberry Pi gateway.
2. **Fog Tier** — Intermediate compute nodes between edge and cloud. *Example:* Micro-data center at a cellular base station, MEC server.
3. **Cloud Tier** — High-capacity centralized data centers. *Example:* AWS EC2, Microsoft Azure data center.

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark for tiers listed + 1 mark for correct examples)*

---

### **Q2.** Differentiate between Edge computing and Fog computing in terms of latency, processing power, and connectivity.

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model Question]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Edge Computing | Fog Computing |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|
| **Latency** | Ultra-low (~1-10 ms) | Low (~10-100 ms) |
| **Processing Power** | Low (constrained devices) | Medium (server-class nodes) |
| **Connectivity** | Often intermittent, local | Persistent, networked to cloud |
| **Location** | At/near data source (device-side) | Between edge and cloud (LAN-level) |
| **Example Hardware** | Raspberry Pi, ECU | Base station server, router |

*(1 mark for latency + 1 mark for processing power + 1 mark for connectivity distinction)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions have **internal choice**. Two fully-solved alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the three-tier system model of Fog-Edge-Cloud architecture. List the characteristics of each tier. (7 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TIER 3: CLOUD                           │
│  - High compute, high storage            │
│  - Long-term analytics                   │
│  - High latency                          │
└──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
               │ (WAN / Internet)
┌──────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
│  TIER 2: FOG                            │
│  - Medium compute, medium storage        │
│  - Aggregation, filtering                │
│  - LAN-level latency                     │
└──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
               │ (LAN / Wi-Fi / 5G)
┌──────────────▼───────────────────────────┐
│  TIER 1: EDGE                           │
│  - Low compute, minimal storage          │
│  - Real-time processing                  │
│  - Sub-10ms latency                      │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Characteristics of Each Tier (5 marks for explanation, 2 marks for diagram):**

**Edge Tier Characteristics:**
- Proximity: Located at/near the data source (1-5 m distance).
- Resources: Constrained CPU, limited battery.
- Latency: Ultra-low (< 10 ms).
- Examples: Sensors, smartphones, embedded MCUs, vehicular ECUs.

**Fog Tier Characteristics:**
- Proximity: LAN-level (few meters to few kilometers).
- Resources: Server-class machines, moderate storage.
- Latency: Low (~10-100 ms).
- Examples: MEC servers, smart routers, micro data centers, RSU in VANETs.

**Cloud Tier Characteristics:**
- Proximity: Geographically distant (100s of km).
- Resources: Virtually unlimited (cluster computing).
- Latency: High (~100-500 ms).
- Examples: AWS, Azure, Google Cloud data centers.

---

#### **(b)** A surveillance camera generates 2 MB of data per frame. Each frame requires $2 \times 10^9$ CPU cycles for intrusion detection. Compute the total latency if the frame is processed (i) locally at the edge (CPU = 1 GHz) and (ii) offloaded to a fog node (CPU = 2.5 GHz, uplink rate = 50 Mbps). Assume result size is negligible. (7 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- Data size: $d = 2$ MB $= 2 \times 8 \times 10^6 = 1.6 \times 10^7$ bits
- CPU cycles: $c = 2 \times 10^9$ cycles
- Edge clock: $f_{edge} = 10^9$ Hz
- Fog clock: $f_{fog} = 2.5 \times 10^9$ Hz
- Uplink rate: $R_{up} = 50 \times 10^6$ bps

### Part (i): Local Edge Processing

**Step 1:** No transmission latency.
$$L_{trans} = 0$$

**Step 2:** Processing latency.
$$L_{proc}^{edge} = \frac{c}{f_{edge}} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{10^9} = 2 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Total latency.
$$L_{edge} = 0 + 2 = 2 \ \text{s}$$

### Part (ii): Fog Offloading

**Step 1:** Uplink transmission latency.
$$L_{trans}^{up} = \frac{d}{R_{up}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^7}{50 \times 10^6} = 0.32 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Processing latency at fog.
$$L_{proc}^{fog} = \frac{c}{f_{fog}} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{2.5 \times 10^9} = 0.8 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 3:** Downlink latency (negligible, given).
$$L_{trans}^{down} \approx 0$$

**Step 4:** Total latency.
$$L_{fog} = 0.32 + 0.8 + 0 = 1.12 \ \text{s}$$

### Comparison

| Method | Total Latency |
|--------|--------------|
| **Edge processing** | **2.00 s** |
| **Fog offloading** | **1.12 s** |

**Conclusion:** Fog offloading is **~44% faster** than local edge processing in this scenario, because the fog's 2.5× higher CPU clock speed outweighs the 0.32 s transmission overhead.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### **(a)** Explain the components of the System Model: Network Model, Computational Model, Storage Model, and Task Model. (7 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Solution:**

### 1. Network Model (2 marks)

Defines the **communication topology** and protocols used between tiers.
- **Topology:** Layered hierarchical (Edge → Fog → Cloud) with mesh peer connections within a tier.
- **Protocols:** Wi-Fi, 5G, LTE, Ethernet, ZigBee, MQTT, CoAP.
- **Key parameters:** Bandwidth, latency, packet loss, jitter.

### 2. Computational Model (2 marks)

Defines **compute resources** at each node.
- Edge: Low-power CPUs (e.g., ARM Cortex-A53, ~1-2 GHz).
- Fog: Server-grade CPUs (e.g., Intel Xeon, 2-4 GHz, multi-core).
- Cloud: Distributed clusters (thousands of cores, GPU/TPU acceleration).

Each node $k$ is characterized by CPU clock $f_k$, core count $n_k$, and memory $M_k$.

### 3. Storage Model (1.5 marks)

Defines **data storage hierarchy**:
- **Edge:** Volatile, transient (RAM/flash, GBs).
- **Fog:** Aggregated, short-term (HDD/SSD, TBs).
- **Cloud:** Long-term archival (object storage, data lakes, PBs).

### 4. Task/Application Model (1.5 marks)

Each task $\mathcal{T}_i$ is described by:
- $c_i$: Compute load (cycles)
- $d_i$: Input data size (bits)
- $\tau_i$: Latency deadline (s)
- $\rho_i$: Priority

Decision rule: task is assigned to tier $k^*$ where $L_{k^*} \le \tau_i$ and energy is minimized.

---

#### **(b)** An IoT sensor node has transmit power 0.8 W and processing power 1.5 W. The task requires $5 \times 10^8$ CPU cycles at 500 MHz. Offloading to a fog node via a 20 Mbps link requires transmitting 1 MB. Calculate: (i) energy if processed locally, (ii) energy if offloaded. Comment on which is better. (7 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P_{tx} = 0.8$ W, $P_{proc} = 1.5$ W
- $c = 5 \times 10^8$ cycles, $f = 5 \times 10^8$ Hz
- $R_{up} = 20$ Mbps $= 2 \times 10^7$ bps
- $d = 1$ MB $= 8 \times 10^6$ bits

### Part (i): Local Processing

**Step 1:** Processing time.
$$t_{proc} = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{5 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^8} = 1 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Processing energy.
$$E_{proc} = P_{proc} \cdot t_{proc} = 1.5 \times 1 = 1.5 \ \text{J}$$

**Step 3:** Total local energy (no transmission).
$$E_{local} = 0 + 1.5 = 1.5 \ \text{J}$$

### Part (ii): Fog Offloading

**Step 1:** Transmission time.
$$t_{tx} = \frac{d}{R_{up}} = \frac{8 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^7} = 0.4 \ \text{s}$$

**Step 2:** Transmission energy.
$$E_{tx} = P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} = 0.8 \times 0.4 = 0.32 \ \text{J}$$

**Step 3:** Idle energy during fog processing (assume $P_{sleep} = 0.05$ W, fog processing = 0.1 s).
$$E_{idle} = 0.05 \times 0.1 = 0.005 \ \text{J}$$

**Step 4:** Total offload energy.
$$E_{offload} = 0.32 + 0.005 = 0.325 \ \text{J}$$

### Comparison

| Mode | Energy |
|------|--------|
| **Local** | 1.500 J |
| **Offload** | 0.325 J |

**Conclusion:** **Offloading is ~4.6× more energy-efficient.** The local CPU's processing power (1.5 W) is much greater than the radio's transmit power (0.8 W) over the shorter transmission duration.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in the System Model Topic:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert MB to bits (multiply by $8 \times 10^6$). Off by 8× → full marks lost.
> 2. **Ignoring Propagation Delay:** Especially for cloud offloading, the ~50-100 ms propagation over WAN is often missed. The KTU key *does* include it for cloud problems.
> 3. **Confusing Transmission and Processing Energy:** $E_{tx} = P_{tx} \times t_{tx}$ and $E_{proc} = P_{proc} \times t_{proc}$ — students often swap the powers.
> 4. **Forgetting Downlink Latency:** When result data is non-trivial, $L_{trans}^{down}$ must be added.
> 5. **CPU Units:** Clock speed must be in Hz, not GHz, in formulas. Write $f = 2 \times 10^9$, not $f = 2$ GHz, in equations.
> 6. **Not Stating Assumptions:** Always state "result size negligible" or "propagation delay ignored" in numerical answers — the KTU evaluator awards 1 mark for assumptions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for System Model:**

- ✅ **System Model =** abstract representation of Edge-Fog-Cloud architecture with components, connectivity, and resources.
- ✅ **Three Tiers:** Edge (sensors/devices, <10 ms) → Fog (intermediate nodes, ~100 ms) → Cloud (data centers, ~500 ms).
- ✅ **Four Sub-Models:** Network, Computational, Storage, Task/Application.
- ✅ **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $L_{trans} = d / R$
  - $L_{proc} = c / f$
  - $L_{prop} = s / v$
  - $L_{total} = L_{trans} + L_{queue} + L_{proc} + L_{trans}^{down}$
  - $E = P \times t$
- ✅ **Decision Rule:** If latency deadline $\tau_i < 20$ ms → Edge; 20-500 ms → Fog; > 500 ms → Cloud.
- ✅ **Energy vs. Latency Trade-off:** Fog/Cloud offloading saves energy at the edge but adds transmission latency.
- ✅ **M/M/1 Queuing Model:** $L_{queue} = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu(1-\rho)}$ — used in fog node congestion analysis.
- ✅ **Propagation vs. Transmission:** Transmission = packet pushing time; Propagation = signal travel time (significant in cloud, negligible in fog/edge).
- ✅ **The 5 Metrics:** Latency (ms), Bandwidth (Mbps), Compute (FLOPS), Storage (GB/TB), Energy (Joules).
- ✅ **Block Diagrams:** Always draw the three layers in vertical order: Edge (bottom) → Fog (middle) → Cloud (top).
- ✅ **Assumptions to State:** Result size, propagation delay, queuing, multi-hop routing, protocol overhead.
- ✅ **Real-world Examples:** V2X (vehicular), IoMT (medical), IIoT (industry), smart city, AR/VR.

> **End of Module 2 — System Model Notes**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme, Course Code: PECIT867 — Fog and Edge Computing*

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Proposed Architecture (Fog-Edge-Cloud Paradigm):**
> The *Proposed Architecture* in fog and edge computing refers to a **hierarchical, distributed, and service-oriented reference model** that systematically integrates three computational tiers — the **Edge Tier**, the **Fog Tier**, and the **Cloud Tier** — to deliver low-latency, context-aware, and scalable computing services across the Internet of Things (IoT) continuum.

This architecture was formally proposed to overcome the limitations of purely centralized cloud computing (high latency, bandwidth saturation, single-point-of-failure) and purely distributed edge computing (limited resources, lack of global coordination). It establishes a **cooperative continuum** where data, computation, storage, and control are pushed closer to the data source (edge devices) while maintaining global orchestration and large-scale analytics at the cloud.

### Key Architectural Tenets
- **Hierarchy:** Edge → Fog → Cloud (with optional bilateral data flow).
- **Distribution:** Computation is decentralized across geographically dispersed nodes.
- **Heterogeneity:** Supports diverse hardware (sensors, gateways, servers, hypervisors).
- **Virtualization:** Fog nodes often use containerization or virtualization (e.g., Docker, VMs).
- **Orchestration:** Software-Defined Networking (SDN) and orchestration planes manage resources.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it like a three-tier emergency response system for a smart city:**

| Tier | Real-World Analogy | Role |
|------|-------------------|------|
| **Edge** | Local citizens with smartphones (sensors/cameras) reporting a traffic accident | Detects events, makes immediate, micro-decisions locally |
| **Fog** | Local police station / city traffic control room (in each district) | Aggregates reports from many citizens, makes district-wide decisions, deploys local resources within minutes |
| **Cloud** | National Command Center / Ministry of Transport | Analyzes nationwide patterns, predicts future accidents, updates policies, stores long-term records |

Just as you would not call the National Ministry for every pothole on your street (too slow), but also would not ask a single citizen to manage city traffic (no overview), the **Proposed Architecture** intelligently distributes responsibility across these three layers. The *edge* reacts in **milliseconds**, the *fog* coordinates in **seconds**, and the *cloud* learns and optimizes in **minutes-to-hours**.

---

## 1.3 Core Components Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standardized Terminology (Aligned with KTU 2024 PECIT867 Syllabus):**
> - **Edge Device / End Device:** The data-producing/sensing node (e.g., temperature sensor, smartphone, smart camera). Limited compute; closest to the physical world.
> - **Edge Node / Smart Edge:** A slightly more capable device (e.g., Raspberry Pi, smart gateway) that performs light preprocessing and filtering.
> - **Fog Node:** A networked device with compute, storage, and network connectivity between the edge and the cloud (e.g., industrial router, switch, micro-server, gateway). Performs aggregation, analytics, and local decision-making.
> - **Cloud Data Center:** A large-scale, centralized facility housing thousands of servers for big-data analytics, long-term storage, and global coordination.
> - **Orchestrator:** A logical entity (often SDN-based) that dynamically allocates workloads across the continuum.
> - **Virtualization Layer:** Abstraction that allows multiple tenants/services to share fog/edge hardware.

---

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (Bolded)

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Metrics Used to Evaluate the Proposed Architecture:**
> - **Latency:** Measured in **milliseconds (ms)**. Target: Edge < 5 ms, Fog < 50 ms, Cloud < 200 ms.
> - **Bandwidth:** Measured in **Mbps** or **Gbps**. Aggregated at fog to reduce cloud-bound traffic by **~60-80%**.
> - **Jitter:** Variation in latency, measured in **ms**. Critical for real-time control.
> - **Energy Consumption:** Measured in **Joules (J)** or **Watts (W)**. Edge nodes target < **5 W**.
> - **Service Deployment Time:** Measured in **seconds (s)**. Target: < 30 s via orchestration.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of the Proposed Architecture

The architecture is decomposed into **five functional planes** that cut across the three physical tiers:

### A. The Three Physical Tiers
1. **Edge Tier (End / Perception Layer)**
   - Contains **sensors, actuators, and end-user devices**.
   - Functions: Data acquisition, signal conditioning, elementary filtering, emergency response (< 1 ms loop).
   - Protocol stack: Lightweight (MQTT-SN, CoAP, BLE, ZigBee, LoRaWAN).
   - **Why?** Placed at the physical boundary; cannot be abstracted further without losing real-time guarantees.

2. **Fog Tier (Intermediate / Network Layer)**
   - Contains **fog nodes** (gateways, switches, micro-data centers) geographically distributed.
   - Functions: Stream processing, local analytics (e.g., anomaly detection), caching, temporary storage, device management.
   - Protocol stack: IP-based (TCP/UDP, HTTP, MQTT, AMQP).
   - **How?** Receives aggregated data from many edge devices, performs 80-90% of the processing locally, and forwards only summarized insights to the cloud.

3. **Cloud Tier (Global / Application Layer)**
   - Contains **high-performance servers and data lakes**.
   - Functions: Big-data analytics, deep learning model training, long-term archival storage, business intelligence.
   - Protocol stack: REST/GraphQL APIs, gRPC.
   - **Why?** Provides the global view that fog/edge tiers lack; central source of truth.

### B. The Five Cross-Cutting Planes
| Plane | Responsibility |
|-------|----------------|
| **Compute Plane** | Distributes processing tasks across tiers |
| **Communication Plane** | Manages wired/wireless links (Wi-Fi, 5G, Ethernet) |
| **Storage Plane** | Hierarchical data placement (hot/warm/cold) |
| **Management/Orchestration Plane** | Resource allocation, fault tolerance, monitoring |
| **Security Plane** | Authentication, encryption, access control across all tiers |

---

## 2.2 Why Each Layer Exists — The "Why" Behind the Design

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Driving Problems Solved by the Proposed Architecture:**
> 1. **Latency Sensitivity:** Time-critical applications (autonomous driving, robotic surgery, industrial control loops) cannot tolerate round-trip-to-cloud delays of 50-200 ms. Fog/edge reduces this to 1-10 ms.
> 2. **Bandwidth Saturation:** Raw IoT data streams (e.g., HD video from 10,000 cameras) would overwhelm cloud uplinks. Fog performs **in-network aggregation** and **compression**.
> 3. **Reliability & Autonomy:** Network failures must not cripple local operations. The architecture provides **graceful degradation** — edge continues operating even if fog/cloud links fail.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for the Proposed Architecture (Exam-Relevant):**

| Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|---------|-------------|-------|
| **Round-Trip Time (RTT) Estimate** | $RTT_{total} = 2 \cdot \left( T_{prop} + \dfrac{L}{R} + T_{proc} \right)$ | Total latency for a request-response cycle | **ms** |
| **Propagation Delay** | $T_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v_{medium}}$ | Distance / signal propagation speed | **s** |
| **Transmission Delay** | $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | Packet length / link rate | **s** |
| **Bandwidth Aggregation Gain** | $G_{bw} = 1 - \dfrac{D_{cloud}}{D_{edge}}$ | Fraction of data filtered at fog | dimensionless |
| **Service Availability (Series)** | $A_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} A_i$ | Availability of n cascaded tiers | dimensionless (0-1) |
| **End-to-End Latency Budget** | $L_{budget} = L_{edge} + L_{fog} + L_{cloud}$ | Sum of per-tier latencies | **ms** |
| **Effective Throughput** | $\theta = \dfrac{W \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})}{N_{users}}$ | Shannon-based per-user throughput | **bps** |
| **Data Reduction Ratio** | $\eta_{red} = \dfrac{D_{raw} - D_{processed}}{D_{raw}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of data filtered at fog | **%** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The most frequently tested formulas are **$T_{prop}$**, **$T_{trans}$**, and **$L_{budget}$**. Always state the **unit** explicitly in your answer script.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Proposed Architecture |
|--------|--------------------------------------|
| **Smart Healthcare** | Wearable ECG monitors → patient-side smartphone (edge) → hospital fog node (real-time arrhythmia detection) → cloud (epidemiological analytics) |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Factory sensors → PLCs (edge) → on-premise fog server (predictive maintenance ML) → enterprise cloud (ERP integration) |
| **Smart Transportation** | Autonomous vehicle LiDAR (edge) → roadside unit (fog, V2X coordination) → traffic management cloud (city-wide flow optimization) |
| **Smart Grid** | Smart meters (edge) → neighborhood substation fog (demand forecasting) → national grid cloud (load balancing) |
| **AR/VR & Gaming** | Head-mounted display (edge rendering) → MEC server (fog) → cloud (multiplayer state sync) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — End-to-End Latency Budget

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** A temperature sensor at the edge sends data to a fog node, which processes and forwards to the cloud. The application requires a **hard real-time deadline of 100 ms**.

### Step 1: Define Latency Components
The total latency $L_{total}$ is the sum of delays incurred at each tier plus the transmission delays between tiers.

$$
L_{total} = L_{edge} + L_{fog} + L_{cloud} + T_{trans, e \to f} + T_{trans, f \to c}
$$

where:
- $L_{edge}$ = processing delay at edge device
- $L_{fog}$ = processing delay at fog node
- $L_{cloud}$ = processing delay at cloud
- $T_{trans, e \to f}$ = transmission delay from edge to fog
- $T_{trans, f \to c}$ = transmission delay from fog to cloud

### Step 2: Substitute Transmission Delay Formula
Recall the transmission delay is $T_{trans} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ where $L$ = packet size (bits) and $R$ = link rate (bps).

$$
L_{total} = L_{edge} + L_{fog} + L_{cloud} + \frac{L_1}{R_{e \to f}} + \frac{L_2}{R_{f \to c}}
$$

### Step 3: Insert Numerical Values
Assume the following engineering parameters:

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|-----------|-------|------|
| $L_{edge}$ | 2 | ms |
| $L_{fog}$ | 15 | ms |
| $L_{cloud}$ | 80 | ms |
| $L_1$ (edge→fog packet) | 1024 | bits |
| $R_{e \to f}$ (Wi-Fi link) | $1 \times 10^{6}$ | bps |
| $L_2$ (fog→cloud packet) | 8192 | bits |
| $R_{f \to c}$ (Fiber link) | $1 \times 10^{9}$ | bps |

### Step 4: Compute Transmission Delays
Compute the edge-to-fog transmission delay:

$$
T_{trans, e \to f} = \frac{1024}{1 \times 10^6} \text{ s} = 1.024 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.024 \text{ ms}
$$

Compute the fog-to-cloud transmission delay:

$$
T_{trans, f \to c} = \frac{8192}{1 \times 10^9} \text{ s} = 8.192 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} \approx 0.0082 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 5: Sum All Components

$$
L_{total} = 2 + 15 + 80 + 1.024 + 0.0082
$$

$$
L_{total} = 98.0322 \text{ ms}
$$

### Step 6: Compare to Deadline
Since $L_{total} = 98.03 \text{ ms} < 100 \text{ ms}$, the architecture **meets** the real-time deadline with a **safety margin of 1.97 ms**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The bottleneck is **$L_{cloud} = 80$ ms**. The proposed architecture mitigates this by ensuring that for time-critical decisions, the cloud is **bypassed** and the response is generated at the fog layer (e.g., $L_{edge} + L_{fog} + T_{trans, e \to f} = 18.024$ ms).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bandwidth Aggregation Gain at Fog

> [!NOTE]
> **Scenario:** 100 edge sensors each generate 1 kB of raw data per second. The fog node filters and aggregates them into 1 consolidated 10 kB report per second to the cloud.

### Step 1: Compute Raw Data Volume at Edge

$$
D_{edge} = N \cdot S_{raw} = 100 \times 1 \text{ kB/s} = 100 \text{ kB/s}
$$

### Step 2: Compute Data Forwarded to Cloud After Fog Aggregation

$$
D_{cloud} = 10 \text{ kB/s}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Bandwidth Aggregation Gain

$$
G_{bw} = 1 - \frac{D_{cloud}}{D_{edge}} = 1 - \frac{10}{100} = 0.90 = 90\%
$$

### Step 4: Compute Data Reduction Ratio

$$
\eta_{red} = \frac{100 - 10}{100} \times 100\% = 90\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 90% reduction means the cloud uplink is freed by **9×** its original load, allowing it to serve **9× more edge devices** without infrastructure upgrade.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Tier?

| Application Requirement | Best Tier | Justification |
|------------------------|-----------|---------------|
| Latency < 10 ms (e.g., robotic control) | **Edge** | Physical proximity; no network hop |
| Latency 10-100 ms, local analytics | **Fog** | Close to source; sufficient compute |
| Latency > 100 ms OK, heavy ML training | **Cloud** | Massive compute, GPU/TPU pools |
| Privacy-sensitive (e.g., patient data) | **Edge / Fog** | Data never leaves local domain (compliance with HIPAA/GDPR) |
| Intermittent connectivity | **Edge** | Continues functioning offline |
| Long-term archival (years) | **Cloud** | Cheap cold storage, compliance retention |
| Real-time video analytics (CCTV) | **Fog** | Reduces bandwidth; local ML inference |

---

## 3.4 Hierarchical Data Placement Policy

Define the **Storage Temperature** at each tier based on access frequency:

| Tier | Storage Class | Retention | Access Pattern |
|------|--------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Edge** | Hot | Seconds to minutes | Real-time, high frequency |
| **Fog** | Warm | Hours to days | Aggregated, moderate frequency |
| **Cloud** | Cold (default) / Hot (recent) | Months to years | Batch analytics, archival |

The data movement follows a **bottom-up promotion for analytics** and a **top-down policy distribution** cycle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
