<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3: Introduction to Resource Management, Task Scheduling & Application Deployment in Fog/Edge Computing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 Focus Statement**
> *This module introduces the foundational concepts of **Resource Management**, **Task Scheduling and Offloading**, and **Application Deployment** in Fog and Edge Computing paradigms. It establishes the computational, networking, and orchestration primitives required to efficiently distribute workloads across the Cloud–Fog–Edge continuum.*

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Resource Management at the Edge** refers to the systematic discovery, allocation, monitoring, and release of heterogeneous compute, storage, and network resources distributed across geographically dispersed edge nodes, fog nodes, and the centralized cloud. The objective is to satisfy Quality of Service (QoS) constraints — typically **latency (ms)**, **jitter**, **bandwidth (Mbps)**, **energy (Joules)**, and **availability (%)** — for latency-sensitive IoT and 5G/6G applications.

The three core pillars introduced in this module are:

1. **Task Scheduling & Computation Offloading** — Deciding *where* (which node) and *when* (priority/sequence) a computational task should be executed.
2. **Resource Allocation & Virtualization** — Distributing finite physical resources among competing tenants, applications, and virtual functions.
3. **Application Deployment & Orchestration** — Packaging, distributing, and managing the lifecycle of microservices across the distributed continuum.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Smart City Traffic Control" Analogy**
> Imagine a smart city with thousands of traffic intersections, each generating video feeds. Sending *every single video frame* to a central data center (the cloud) thousands of kilometers away would be too slow (high latency) and too expensive (huge bandwidth). 
> 
> Instead, the city installs **local micro-controllers at every intersection (Edge)**, **district-level servers every few kilometers (Fog)**, and a **central command center (Cloud)**. A person jumping a red light is detected *locally* (Edge), a pattern of accidents across a district is detected *regionally* (Fog), and long-term city planning analytics is done *centrally* (Cloud).
> 
> **Edge = "react instantly"**, **Fog = "coordinate locally"**, **Cloud = "think globally"** — Resource Management is the *traffic cop* that decides which frame goes where.

---

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU 2024 Standard Vocabulary)

| Metric | Standard Unit | Typical Target (Edge Use Case) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **End-to-End Latency** | **milliseconds (ms)** | **1–20 ms** (URLLC, autonomous driving) |
| **Bandwidth** | **Mbps / Gbps** | **≥ 100 Mbps** (per edge node uplink) |
| **Energy Consumption** | **Joules (J) / Watts (W)** | **≤ 10 W** (battery-powered edge) |
| **Service Availability** | **Percentage (%)** | **99.999 % (five-nines)** |
| **Jitter** | **ms** | **≤ 1 ms** |
| **CPU Utilization** | **%** | **60–80 %** (avoid saturation) |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** Always quote a numerical benchmark when defining an edge metric in your exam answer. Saying "low latency" is worth **0 marks**; saying "latency under **20 ms** as per 3GPP URLLC specifications" is worth **full marks**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Layer Operational Logic

The introduction to Module 3 follows a strict three-layer logical progression. Mastering this sequence is essential for answering KTU 14-mark analytical questions.

### Layer 1 — **Discovery & Profiling** (Where are the resources?)
- **Node Discovery:** Identification of all available edge, fog, and cloud nodes through directory services (e.g., DNS-SD, Consul).
- **Resource Profiling:** Continuous monitoring of CPU load, memory usage, network RTT, residual battery, and link bandwidth.
- **Topology Mapping:** Construction of a logical graph $G = (V, E)$ where $V$ is the set of nodes and $E$ is the set of communication links.

### Layer 2 — **Decision Engine** (Who gets what?)
- **Task Characterization:** Each incoming IoT workload is described by a task vector — input size, computational intensity, deadline, priority.
- **Offloading Decision:** A binary/multi-valued decision is made: execute *locally* (on the device) or *offload* to a fog/cloud node.
- **Resource Binding:** Selected physical/virtual resources are reserved for the task via a scheduler.

### Layer 3 — **Execution & Release** (What happens after?)
- **Container/VM Deployment:** The task is executed inside a sandboxed environment (Docker container, unikernel, or micro-VM).
- **Result Transmission:** Output is sent back to the requesting device or forwarded to a sink node.
- **Resource Release:** Compute slots, memory pages, and network sockets are returned to the available pool.

---

## 2.2 The Canonical Offloading Decision Model

The foundational mathematical model for computation offloading (foundational to Module 3) treats a task $T_i$ with the following parameters:

- **Input Data Size:** $D_i$ (in **bits** or **KB**)
- **Computational Workload:** $W_i$ (in **CPU cycles** or **MIPS**)
- **Maximum Tolerable Latency:** $T_i^{max}$ (in **seconds**)

The decision criterion is binary:

$$
X_i = 
\begin{cases}
0 & \text{if } T_i^{local} \leq T_i^{max} \quad \text{(execute locally)} \\
1 & \text{if } T_i^{offload} < T_i^{local} \quad \text{(offload to edge/fog/cloud)}
\end{cases}
$$

Where the **local execution time** is:

$$
T_i^{local} = \frac{W_i}{f_{device}}
$$

with $f_{device}$ being the device's CPU frequency in **cycles/second**, and the **offloading time** is:

$$
T_i^{offload} = T_i^{tx} + T_i^{queue} + T_i^{exec} + T_i^{rx}
$$

These are the four sequential costs of offloading — transmission, queuing, execution, and reception.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for Module 3 Introduction**
> Memorize every entry below. These appear in **≥ 70 %** of KTU Module 3 numerical or analytical questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Local execution time | $T^{local} = W / f$ | **seconds** | Baseline for offloading gain |
| 2 | Transmission time | $T^{tx} = D / R$ | **seconds** | Uplink bandwidth budget |
| 3 | Total offloading time | $T^{off} = T^{tx} + T^{queue} + T^{exec} + T^{rx}$ | **seconds** | End-to-end latency budget |
| 4 | Offloading gain | $G = T^{local} - T^{off}$ | **seconds** | Whether offloading is beneficial |
| 5 | Energy for transmission | $E^{tx} = P_{tx} \cdot (D / R)$ | **Joules** | Battery-aware offloading |
| 6 | Effective bandwidth | $R = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | **bps** | Shannon capacity limit |
| 7 | Queueing latency (M/M/1) | $T^{queue} = 1 / (\mu - \lambda)$ | **seconds** | Edge server load modeling |
| 8 | Total cost function | $C = \alpha \cdot T + \beta \cdot E$ | weighted | Multi-objective optimization |
| 9 | Node utilization | $U = \lambda / \mu$ | **dimensionless** | Edge server saturation check |
| 10 | Service availability | $A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | **%** | Reliability calculation |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Typo Trap:** In your answer sheet, never write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value inside a table — write it as $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\text{abs}(x)$. KTU examiners read handwritten scans and the pipe symbol is frequently misread as the numeral 1 or the letter l.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles introduced in this module directly drive engineering decisions in:

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Real-time split-second offloading decisions between in-car ECU, roadside edge, and cloud HD-map servers.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Latency-critical robotic control loops on factory-floor fog nodes.
- **Telemedicine & Remote Surgery:** Sub-10 ms haptic feedback requires fog-level resource reservation.
- **AR/VR & Metaverse:** Edge-rendered frames to reduce motion-to-photon latency below **20 ms**.
- **Smart Grids:** Distributed energy resource optimization across millions of edge meters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Offloading Gain Function

Below is the complete, line-by-line derivation of the **Offloading Gain** $G$, which is the most frequently tested analytical expression in Module 3. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly.

### Step 1 — Define the Task Parameters
We start with a single computational task arriving at an edge device. We formally define:

$$
D_i = \text{input data size of task } i \quad \text{(in bits)}
$$
$$
W_i = \text{required CPU cycles for task } i \quad \text{(in cycles)}
$$
$$
R_{ul} = \text{uplink data rate from device to edge node} \quad \text{(in bps)}
$$
$$
R_{dl} = \text{downlink data rate from edge to device} \quad \text{(in bps)}
$$
$$
f_{dev} = \text{device CPU clock frequency} \quad \text{(in cycles/second)}
$$
$$
f_{edge} = \text{edge server CPU clock frequency} \quad \text{(in cycles/second)}
$$

> **Logic note:** The parameters are split into device-side and edge-side because offloading is fundamentally a *two-system* operation; combining them would hide the latency source.

### Step 2 — Compute Local Execution Time
If the task runs entirely on the device, the time taken is workload divided by device speed:

$$
T_i^{local} = \frac{W_i}{f_{dev}}
$$

This is a direct application of the *time = work / rate* relationship, where work is in CPU cycles and rate is in cycles per second, yielding seconds.

### Step 3 — Compute Uplink Transmission Time
The input data must travel from device to edge node over the wireless uplink:

$$
T_i^{tx} = \frac{D_i}{R_{ul}}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Edge Execution Time
The edge server processes the data at its own CPU speed:

$$
T_i^{exec} = \frac{W_i}{f_{edge}}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Downlink Reception Time
The result (typically much smaller than the input) travels back:

$$
T_i^{rx} = \frac{D_i^{result}}{R_{dl}}
$$

For analytical simplicity in KTU problems, the result size is often assumed equal to the input size $D_i$ in the worst case, giving:

$$
T_i^{rx} \approx \frac{D_i}{R_{dl}}
$$

### Step 6 — Sum the Offloading Time
Adding the four sequential stages:

$$
T_i^{off} = T_i^{tx} + T_i^{exec} + T_i^{rx}
$$

(Queueing delay $T_i^{queue}$ is added in advanced problems using the M/M/1 model.)

### Step 7 — Define the Offloading Gain
The offloading gain is the *time saved* by offloading versus local execution:

$$
G_i = T_i^{local} - T_i^{off}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Substituted Form
Substituting all prior expressions:

$$
G_i = \frac{W_i}{f_{dev}} - \left( \frac{D_i}{R_{ul}} + \frac{W_i}{f_{edge}} + \frac{D_i}{R_{dl}} \right)
$$

> **Logic note:** A *positive* $G_i$ means offloading is beneficial; a *negative* $G_i$ means local execution is faster. The crossover point $G_i = 0$ defines the offloading threshold.

### Step 9 — Energy-Aware Extension (Frequently Asked)
If the device is battery-powered, we also compute transmission energy:

$$
E_i^{tx} = P_{tx} \cdot T_i^{tx} = P_{tx} \cdot \frac{D_i}{R_{ul}}
$$

where $P_{tx}$ is the transmit power in **Watts**. The joint time–energy cost becomes:

$$
C_i = \alpha \cdot T_i^{off} + \beta \cdot E_i^{tx}
$$

with weights $\alpha + \beta = 1$, $\alpha, \beta \in [0,1]$.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Illustration (Model Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample KTU-Style Numerical**
> A wearable health-monitoring device must process an ECG beat-classification task. Compute the offloading gain.
> 
> - $W = 1 \times 10^9$ cycles
> - $D = 2 \times 10^6$ bits
> - $f_{dev} = 0.5 \times 10^9$ Hz
> - $f_{edge} = 4 \times 10^9$ Hz
> - $R_{ul} = R_{dl} = 10 \times 10^6$ bps

**Solution:**

$$
T^{local} = \frac{1 \times 10^9}{0.5 \times 10^9} = 2.000 \ \text{seconds}
$$

$$
T^{tx} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^6} = 0.200 \ \text{seconds}
$$

$$
T^{exec} = \frac{1 \times 10^9}{4 \times 10^9} = 0.250 \ \text{seconds}
$$

$$
T^{rx} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^6} = 0.200 \ \text{seconds}
$$

$$
T^{off} = 0.200 + 0.250 + 0.200 = 0.650 \ \text{seconds}
$$

$$
G = 2.000 - 0.650 = 1.350 \ \text{seconds}
$$

**Conclusion:** Offloading to the edge saves **1.35 seconds** — a **67.5 %** latency reduction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Cloud–Fog–Edge Continuum (Module 3 Foundational Architecture)

The diagram below maps the *physical and logical layering* introduced in Module 3. It shows where resources are located, what types of tasks belong at each layer, and the latency tier they occupy.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cloudTier["CLOUD TIER - Global Analytics Layer"]
        cloudA["Hyperscale Data Center"]
        cloudB["Big Data and ML Training"]
        cloudC["Long Term Storage"]
    end
    subgraph fogTier["FOG TIER - Regional Coordination Layer"]
        fogA["District Fog Server"]
        fogB["Gateway Aggregator"]
        fogC["Local Network Switch"]
    end
    subgraph edgeTier["EDGE TIER - Device Proximity Layer"]
        edgeA["Roadside Edge Node"]
        edgeB["Smart Camera"]
        edgeC["Wearable Device"]
        edgeD["Industrial Sensor"]
    end
    subgraph userTier["END DEVICE TIER - Sensing and Actuation"]
        userA["Smartphone"]
        userB["Vehicle ECU"]
        userC["Robot Arm"]
    end
    cloudA --- cloudB
    cloudB --- cloudC
    fogA --- fogB
    fogB --- fogC
    edgeA --- edgeB
    edgeB --- edgeC
    edgeC --- edgeD
    userA --- userB
    userB --- userC
    edgeTier --- fogTier
    fogTier --- cloudTier
    userTier --- edgeTier
    userTier -->|Latency under 1 ms| edgeTier
    userTier -->|Latency 1 to 20 ms| fogTier
    userTier -->|Latency above 100 ms| cloudTier
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows on the right indicate the *latency tier* each workload type is matched to. A student should be able to draw a *similar layered triangle* from memory in the exam.

---

## 4.2 Resource Management Decision Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

This block diagram represents the **internal flow of a Resource Manager** — the software component that performs the offloading decisions analyzed in Section 3.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Task Arrival Queue] -->|Push event| blockB[Task Profiler]
    blockB -->|Task vector| blockC[Decision Engine]
    blockD[Resource Monitor] -->|Live telemetry| blockC
    blockE[Policy Repository] -->|Offloading rules| blockC
    blockC -->|X equals 0| blockF[Local Executor]
    blockC -->|X equals 1| blockG[Offload Scheduler]
    blockG -->|Reserve slot| blockH[Edge or Fog Node]
    blockH -->|Result payload| blockI[Result Aggregator]
    blockF --> blockI
    blockI -->|Feedback| blockD
    style blockA fill:#e8f4ff
    style blockC fill:#fff4d6
    style blockG fill:#ffe0e0
    style blockH fill:#d6f5d6
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The `Decision Engine` is the *brain*; the `Resource Monitor` is the *eye*; the `Policy Repository` is the *rulebook*. The binary output $X_i \in \{0, 1\}$ is the offloading decision introduced in Section 2.2.

---

## 4.3 Offloading Gain vs. Data Size — Conceptual Graph

This schematic represents the **decision boundary** between local and offloaded execution. KTU examiners frequently expect students to *describe* such a curve even if they cannot draw it precisely.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    grA["X axis: Input Data Size D in KB"] --> grB["Y axis: Execution Time in seconds"]
    grC["Local line: T local equals W over f dev - Constant slope zero, intercept W divided by f dev"]
    grD["Offload line: T off equals D over R plus W over f edge plus D over R - Linear in D with slope 2 over R"]
    grE["Intersection point D star - The offloading threshold"]
    grF["Region left of D star: Local is faster"]
    grG["Region right of D star: Offload is faster"]
    grA --- grC
    grA --- grD
    grC --- grE
    grD --- grE
    grE --- grF
    grE --- grG
```

> **Engineering insight:** The crossover point $D^*$ is found by setting $T^{local} = T^{off}$ and solving for $D$. Tasks with $D < D^*$ run locally; tasks with $D > D^*$ are offloaded.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the three-tier architecture of Fog Computing and state **two distinguishing features** of each tier.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three-tier architecture consists of:

1. **Edge Tier** — Devices such as sensors, smartphones, and wearables generate data. **Distinguishing features:** (i) proximity to data source giving **1–5 ms** latency, (ii) constrained resources (CPU, battery, memory).

2. **Fog Tier** — Intermediate nodes like gateways and micro-data-centers. **Distinguishing features:** (i) provides hierarchical aggregation and local analytics, (ii) supports mobility and geo-distribution.

3. **Cloud Tier** — Centralized hyperscale data centers. **Distinguishing features:** (i) virtually unlimited compute and storage, (ii) used for heavy batch analytics and long-term archival.

*(1 mark per tier + 1 mark for sketch = 3 marks total)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*Define **computation offloading** and list **four parameters** that influence the offloading decision in edge computing.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Computation offloading is the process of migrating a computational task from a resource-constrained end device to a more powerful remote server (edge, fog, or cloud) to improve performance, save energy, or meet latency deadlines.

**Four Influencing Parameters (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
1. **Available bandwidth** ($R$) of the wireless link.
2. **Server load** ($\lambda / \mu$) at the candidate edge node.
3. **Task deadline** ($T^{max}$) imposed by the application.
4. **Residual battery level** of the device (energy budget).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Resource Management System** in a Fog–Edge environment. Describe the role of each functional block in **7 marks**.

**(b)** A wearable ECG monitor must run a heart-beat classification task. Compute the **offloading gain** given: $W = 1.5 \times 10^9$ cycles, $D = 1.5 \times 10^6$ bits, $f_{dev} = 0.6 \times 10^9$ Hz, $f_{edge} = 5 \times 10^9$ Hz, $R_{ul} = R_{dl} = 5 \times 10^6$ bps. State whether offloading is beneficial. **7 marks**.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

> **Block Diagram (3 marks):** A labeled block diagram must show: *Task Queue → Profiler → Decision Engine ← Resource Monitor ← Policy DB → Local Executor / Offload Scheduler → Edge Node → Result Aggregator.*

**Functional Roles (4 marks):**

- **Task Queue:** Buffers incoming IoT tasks; preserves arrival order.
- **Profiler:** Extracts $D_i$, $W_i$, deadline $T_i^{max}$, and priority.
- **Decision Engine:** Computes $G_i = T^{local} - T^{off}$ and selects $X_i \in \{0,1\}$.
- **Resource Monitor:** Continuously polls CPU, memory, bandwidth, and energy.
- **Policy Repository:** Stores user-defined SLA, cost, and energy rules.
- **Offload Scheduler:** Reserves a slot on the chosen node and dispatches the task.
- **Result Aggregator:** Collects outputs and feeds performance metrics back to the Monitor for closed-loop adaptation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Local time (1 mark):**

$$
T^{local} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^9}{0.6 \times 10^9} = 2.500 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission time (1 mark):**

$$
T^{tx} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.300 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Edge execution time (1 mark):**

$$
T^{exec} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^9}{5 \times 10^9} = 0.300 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Reception time (1 mark):**

$$
T^{rx} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.300 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Total offloading time (1 mark):**

$$
T^{off} = 0.300 + 0.300 + 0.300 = 0.900 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Offloading gain (1 mark):**

$$
G = 2.500 - 0.900 = 1.600 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion (1 mark):** Since $G = +1.600$ s is **positive and large**, offloading to the edge node is **highly beneficial**, reducing latency by **64 %**.

---

### ⭐ Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Alternative) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Explain the **three-tier Cloud–Fog–Edge architecture** with a neat diagram. Discuss **two real-world applications** that benefit from each tier. **7 marks**.

**(b)** A smart camera performs on-device object detection. Evaluate the **total offloading time** when offloaded to a fog node, and compute the **cost function** $C = 0.7 \cdot T + 0.3 \cdot E$ using the data below. State if offloading is preferred over local execution. **7 marks**.
- $W = 2 \times 10^9$ cycles, $D = 4 \times 10^6$ bits
- $f_{dev} = 1 \times 10^9$ Hz, $f_{fog} = 8 \times 10^9$ Hz
- $R_{ul} = R_{dl} = 20 \times 10^6$ bps
- $P_{tx} = 0.5$ W

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Architecture (3 marks):** *Three layers — Edge (device-level, sub-5 ms latency), Fog (gateway/micro-DC, 5–50 ms latency), Cloud (hyperscale DC, > 100 ms latency).*

**Edge Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Autonomous vehicle collision avoidance** — needs **< 10 ms** reaction.
2. **AR/VR head-mounted displays** — needs **< 20 ms** motion-to-photon latency.

**Fog Applications (1 mark):** Smart-grid substation load balancing across hundreds of meters.

**Cloud Applications (1 mark):** Genome-wide association studies on petabytes of biomedical data.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Local time (1 mark):**

$$
T^{local} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{1 \times 10^9} = 2.000 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission time (1 mark):**

$$
T^{tx} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{20 \times 10^6} = 0.200 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Fog execution time (1 mark):**

$$
T^{exec} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{8 \times 10^9} = 0.250 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Reception time + total offload (1 mark):**

$$
T^{rx} = \frac{4 \times 10^6}{20 \times 10^6} = 0.200 \ \text{s}
$$

$$
T^{off} = 0.200 + 0.250 + 0.200 = 0.650 \ \text{s}
$$

**Step 5 — Transmission energy and cost (2 marks):**

$$
E^{tx} = 0.5 \times 0.200 = 0.100 \ \text{J}
$$

$$
C = 0.7 \times 0.650 + 0.3 \times 0.100 = 0.455 + 0.030 = 0.485
$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion (1 mark):** Local time is **2.000 s** versus offload time **0.650 s**; with cost $C = 0.485$, **offloading is strongly preferred**, yielding a **67.5 %** time reduction.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 3 Introduction**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting units (–1 mark):** Always write "**seconds**" or "**ms**" beside any time value. A bare "0.65" loses a mark.
> 
> 2. **Wrong formula for $T^{off}$ (–2 marks):** Many students write only $T^{tx} + T^{exec}$ and forget $T^{rx}$. The full expression is **always** $T^{tx} + T^{queue} + T^{exec} + T^{rx}$.
> 
> 3. **No sign convention for $G$ (–1 mark):** Always state explicitly: *"Since $G > 0$, offloading is beneficial."*
> 
> 4. **Vague tier descriptions (–1 mark):** Saying "Cloud is powerful" is vague. Say *"Cloud offers virtually unlimited elastic resources and is suited for batch analytics exceeding petabyte scale."*
> 
> 5. **Skipping the diagram (–2 marks):** A 14-mark analytical question *always* carries 2–3 marks for the diagram. Even a hand-drawn triangle with labels scores those marks.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3 Introduction**
> 
> - **Three tiers:** Cloud (analytics), Fog (aggregation), Edge (reaction). Know one real application for each.
> - **Core decision variable:** $X_i \in \{0, 1\}$ — local or offload.
> - **Local time formula:** $T^{local} = W / f_{dev}$.
> - **Offload time formula:** $T^{off} = T^{tx} + T^{queue} + T^{exec} + T^{rx}$.
> - **Offloading gain:** $G = T^{local} - T^{off}$. Positive $G$ ⇒ beneficial.
> - **Transmission energy:** $E^{tx} = P_{tx} \cdot D / R$.
> - **Cost function:** $C = \alpha T + \beta E$ with $\alpha + \beta = 1$.
> - **Queueing latency (M/M/1):** $T^{queue} = 1 / (\mu - \lambda)$.
> - **Shannon capacity:** $R = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$.
> - **Resource Manager blocks:** Profiler, Monitor, Decision Engine, Scheduler, Aggregator.
> - **Key metrics to memorize:** Latency **1–20 ms**, Availability **99.999 %**, Jitter **≤ 1 ms**.
> - **Always include:** Units, sign of $G$, conclusion sentence, and a diagram.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methodology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Methodology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fog and Edge Computing Methodology** refers to the systematic, structured set of procedures, principles, models, and engineering practices used to **design, develop, deploy, manage, and evaluate** distributed computing systems that extend cloud capabilities to the network edge. It encompasses the complete engineering lifecycle from requirements elicitation, architectural partitioning, resource modeling, and workload offloading, to runtime orchestration, performance benchmarking, and security integration.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, methodology is not just an abstract concept — it is the **engineering blueprint** that dictates *how* a fog/edge system is built. The methodology bridges the gap between theoretical models (latency, bandwidth, energy) and real-world deployment in heterogeneous environments involving IoT, 5G/6G, vehicular networks, and industrial IoT (IIoT).

### 1.1.1 Key Methodological Pillars

| Pillar | Description |
|---|---|
| **Architectural Methodology** | Defines the layered/tiered structure (Device → Edge → Fog → Cloud) |
| **Computational Offloading Methodology** | Decides *what* to compute locally vs. remotely |
| **Resource Allocation Methodology** | Manages CPU, memory, network, and energy across nodes |
| **Orchestration Methodology** | Coordinates services, containers, and VMs dynamically |
| **Evaluation Methodology** | Benchmarks QoS metrics: **latency**, **jitter**, **throughput**, **energy** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Methodology like the Recipe Book of a Master Chef** 🍳
>
> Imagine you are a chef running a massive kitchen with helpers stationed at the **dining tables** (Edge devices), **waiters roaming the floor** (Fog nodes), and a **central head chef in the back kitchen** (Cloud). The **Methodology** is the master recipe book that tells:
> - Which dishes (tasks) each helper must prepare at the table (local processing).
> - Which dishes need to go to the waiter (fog processing) for partial preparation.
> - Which complex dishes must be sent to the head chef (cloud) for full execution.
> - **How fast** each dish must reach the customer (latency targets).
> - **How much fuel** each station uses (energy budget).
>
> Without a methodology, the kitchen descends into chaos — late orders, cold food, and wasted gas. With a clear methodology, the operation becomes **predictable, efficient, and scalable**.

## 1.3 Why Methodology Matters in KTU Context

KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)**. The methodology topic directly supports the following Course Outcomes:

- **CO3**: Design fog/edge architectures using appropriate methodologies.
- **CO4**: Evaluate trade-offs in computational offloading, resource allocation, and orchestration.
- **CO5**: Apply systematic evaluation techniques for QoS-aware systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Recalled:**
> - **Latency** (ms): Time from request to response.
> - **Jitter** (ms): Variation in latency.
> - **Bandwidth** (Mbps): Data transfer capacity.
> - **Energy Efficiency** (Joules/bit): Power consumed per unit data.
> - **Service Availability** (%): Uptime of fog services.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fog/Edge Computing Engineering Methodology — Step-by-Step

A standard methodology for fog/edge systems follows a **six-phase structured engineering lifecycle**:

### Phase 1 — Requirements Elicitation & Use-Case Modeling
- Identify application domain: healthcare, smart city, autonomous vehicles, IIoT.
- Capture **QoS constraints** (latency < 10 ms for V2X, > 99.999% availability for medical).
- Map data sources, sinks, and flow rates.

### Phase 2 — System Architecture Design
- Choose tier model: **3-tier (Edge-Fog-Cloud)** or **4-tier (Device-Edge-Fog-Cloud)**.
- Define node capabilities: Raspberry Pi class (edge) vs. server-class (fog).
- Establish communication protocols: **MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/2, gRPC, DDS**.

### Phase 3 — Workload Partitioning & Offloading Strategy
- Decide task placement policy: **static, dynamic, or hybrid**.
- Apply decision variables: computation cost $C_c$, transmission cost $C_t$, deadline $D$.
- **Offload if** $C_{remote} + C_{transmission} < C_{local}$ **and** $T_{remote} < D$.

### Phase 4 — Resource Allocation & Scheduling
- Allocate CPU, memory, storage, and radio resources.
- Use schedulers: **First-Come-First-Serve (FCFS)**, **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**, **Weighted Fair Queuing (WFQ)**, or **AI-based reinforcement learning schedulers**.

### Phase 5 — Orchestration & Service Management
- Deploy containers (Docker) and orchestrators (**Kubernetes, K3s, KubeEdge**).
- Enable service migration via live migration or container handoff.
- Implement service mesh (Istio/Linkerd) for traffic management.

### Phase 6 — Performance Evaluation & Optimization
- Benchmark using tools: **iPerf, TC, Chaos Monkey, CloudSim, EdgeCloudSim, iFogSim**.
- Measure: latency, throughput, energy, packet loss, SLA violations.
- Iterate back to Phase 3 for re-optimization.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and form the analytical backbone of KTU 2024 Scheme examination questions on this topic.

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{total} = T_{compute} + T_{transmit} + T_{queue}$ | Total task completion time | seconds (s) |
| 2 | $T_{transmit} = \dfrac{D_{size}}{R_{bandwidth}}$ | Transmission delay = Data size / Rate | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $E_{total} = P_{compute} \cdot T_{compute} + P_{tx} \cdot T_{transmit}$ | Total energy consumption | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $C_{offload} = w_c \cdot C_{compute} + w_t \cdot T_{transmit}$ | Weighted offloading cost | normalized |
| 5 | $U_{util} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{load}_i}{N \cdot \text{capacity}_i}$ | Node utilization | % (0–100) |
| 6 | $S_{speedup} = \dfrac{T_{local}}{T_{offload}}$ | Speedup from offloading | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{SLA Violation} = \dfrac{\text{Missed Tasks}}{\text{Total Tasks}} \cdot 100\%$ | Service Level Agreement breach | % |
| 8 | $L_{jitter} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(l_i - \bar{l})^2}$ | Standard deviation of latency | ms |
| 9 | $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \cdot 100\%$ | System availability metric | % |
| 10 | $R_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} R_i$ (parallel) | Aggregated bandwidth in parallel paths | Mbps |
| 11 | $T_{makespan} = \max_{i}(T_{i}^{complete})$ | Scheduling makespan | seconds (s) |
| 12 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot P_{static} + (1-\alpha) \cdot P_{adaptive}$ | Dynamic power model (α ∈ [0,1]) | Watts (W) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:** $D_{size}$ = data size in bits, $R_{bandwidth}$ = link rate in bps, $P_{compute}$ = compute power in Watts, $P_{tx}$ = transmit power, $w_c, w_t$ = weighting factors, MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures, MTTR = Mean Time To Repair.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Smart Healthcare**: Methodology ensures ECG data processed at the edge within **100 ms** for arrhythmia detection.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: V2X offloading methodology targets **sub-10 ms** latency for collision avoidance.
- **Smart Grid**: Energy-aware methodology balances load between edge controllers and fog aggregators.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Predictive maintenance methodologies schedule ML inference at fog nodes.
- **AR/VR**: Methodologies for **MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing)** achieve **< 20 ms** motion-to-photon latency.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Offloading Decision Criterion (Energy–Latency Trade-off)

We derive the condition under which a mobile/edge device should offload a task to a fog node.

### Step 1: Define Local Execution Cost
Local computation time on a device with CPU frequency $f_l$ (cycles/sec) for a task of workload $W$ (cycles):

$$
T_{local} = \frac{W}{f_l}
$$

### Step 2: Define Local Energy Consumption
Local energy with power $P_l$ (Watts):

$$
E_{local} = P_l \cdot T_{local} = P_l \cdot \frac{W}{f_l}
$$

### Step 3: Define Remote (Offload) Execution Time
Remote execution time = transmission time + remote compute time + result return time:

$$
T_{remote} = \frac{D_{size}}{R_{uplink}} + \frac{W}{f_r} + \frac{D_{result}}{R_{downlink}}
$$

> Since $D_{result} \ll D_{size}$ (output is small), the return term is often approximated as negligible:

$$
T_{remote} \approx \frac{D_{size}}{R_{uplink}} + \frac{W}{f_r}
$$

### Step 4: Define Remote Energy Consumption
Energy spent in transmission (transmit power $P_{tx}$):

$$
E_{remote} = P_{tx} \cdot \frac{D_{size}}{R_{uplink}}
$$

> Note: Fog node's energy is *not* counted in the device's battery budget.

### Step 5: Offloading Decision Rule
Offload if both **time and energy** improve (or meet constraints):

$$
\text{Offload iff:} \quad T_{remote} < T_{local} \quad \text{AND} \quad E_{remote} < E_{local}
$$

### Step 6: Combine into Single Cost Function
Using a weighted objective (λ ∈ [0,1]):

$$
C_{total} = \lambda \cdot T + (1-\lambda) \cdot E
$$

$$
C_{local} = \lambda \cdot \frac{W}{f_l} + (1-\lambda) \cdot P_l \cdot \frac{W}{f_l}
$$

$$
C_{remote} = \lambda \cdot \left(\frac{D_{size}}{R_{uplink}} + \frac{W}{f_r}\right) + (1-\lambda) \cdot P_{tx} \cdot \frac{D_{size}}{R_{uplink}}
$$

$$
\text{Decision: Offload if } C_{remote} < C_{local}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Makespan for Parallel Task Scheduling in Fog Cluster

Consider $N$ tasks assigned to $k$ fog nodes with capacities $f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k$.

### Step 1: Workload Distribution
Total workload:

$$
W_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i
$$

### Step 2: Workload per Node
Assuming optimal load balancing:

$$
W_j = \frac{W_{total}}{k} \quad \text{(uniform)} \quad \text{or} \quad W_j = \sum_{i \in S_j} w_i \quad \text{(partitioned)}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Time per Node

$$
T_j = \frac{W_j}{f_j}
$$

### Step 4: Makespan Definition

$$
T_{makespan} = \max_{j=1}^{k} T_j
$$

### Step 5: Speedup Metric

$$
S_{speedup} = \frac{T_{makespan}^{single}}{T_{makespan}^{cluster}} = \frac{W_{total} / f_{max}}{T_{makespan}}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Methodology to Choose?

| Application Type | Recommended Methodology | Latency Target | Energy Priority | Offloading Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Autonomous Driving | V2X + MEC | < 10 ms | Low | **Mandatory offload** |
| Smart Healthcare | Edge AI | < 100 ms | High | **Hybrid (selective)** |
| Video Surveillance | Fog Analytics | < 500 ms | Medium | **Bulk offload** |
| Smart Agriculture | LPWAN + Edge | seconds | Very High | **Local-first** |
| Industrial Robotics | Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN) | < 1 ms | Medium | **Deterministic local** |
| AR/VR Gaming | MEC + 5G | < 20 ms | Medium | **Predictive offload** |
| Smart Metering | Event-driven Edge | minutes | Very High | **Local aggregation** |

> [!NOTE]
> This decision matrix is a **high-yield KTU 2024 exam tool** — examiners often pose a scenario question asking the student to justify a methodology choice.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fog/Edge Computing Methodology — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    reqStart["PHASE 1: Requirements Elicitation"]:::phase1
    archDesign["PHASE 2: Architecture Design"]:::phase2
    partition["PHASE 3: Workload Partitioning"]:::phase3
    resource["PHASE 4: Resource Allocation"]:::phase4
    orchestrate["PHASE 5: Orchestration"]:::phase5
    evaluate["PHASE 6: Performance Evaluation"]:::phase6
    feedback["Feedback Loop to Phase 3"]:::feedback

    reqStart --> archDesign
    archDesign --> partition
    partition --> resource
    resource --> orchestrate
    orchestrate --> evaluate
    evaluate --> feedback
    feedback --> partition

    subgraph deviceLayer["DEVICE LAYER"]
        d1["IoT Sensors"]
        d2["Cameras"]
        d3["Mobile Devices"]
        d4["Vehicles"]
    end

    subgraph edgeLayer["EDGE LAYER"]
        e1["Edge Gateways"]
        e2["Micro Data Centers"]
        e3["Base Stations"]
    end

    subgraph fogLayer["FOG LAYER"]
        f1["Fog Nodes"]
        f2["Local Servers"]
        f3["Network Switches"]
    end

    subgraph cloudLayer["CLOUD LAYER"]
        c1["Data Center"]
        c2["Analytics Engine"]
        c3["Long Term Storage"]
    end

    d1 --> e1
    d2 --> e1
    d3 --> e2
    d4 --> e3
    e1 --> f1
    e2 --> f2
    e3 --> f3
    f1 --> c1
    f2 --> c2
    f3 --> c3

    classDef phase1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef phase2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef phase3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef phase4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef phase5 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef phase6 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Offloading Decision Methodology — Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["Task Arrives at Edge Device"]):::start
    checkDeadline{"Deadline Check: \n Time Budget T_budget < D?"}:::decision
    checkEnergy{"Energy Check: \n E_local > E_threshold?"}:::decision
    computeLocal["Execute Locally on Device"]:::local
    checkNetwork{"Network Check: \n R_uplink > R_min?"}:::decision
    offloadFog["Offload to Fog Node"]:::fog
    failback["Queue or Drop Task"]:::fail
    computeRemote["Execute on Fog Node"]:::fog
    returnResult["Return Result to Device"]:::return
    complete(["Task Complete"]):::end

    start --> checkDeadline
    checkDeadline -->|No, sufficient time| checkEnergy
    checkDeadline -->|Yes, time critical| checkNetwork
    checkEnergy -->|No, energy OK| computeLocal
    checkEnergy -->|Yes, low battery| checkNetwork
    checkNetwork -->|Yes, network good| offloadFog
    checkNetwork -->|No, network poor| failback
    offloadFog --> computeRemote
    computeRemote --> returnResult
    returnResult --> complete
    computeLocal --> complete

    classDef start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef end fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef local fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
    classDef fog fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef fail fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Q: Define Fog and Edge Computing methodology. List any three key phases of the methodology lifecycle.**

**Model Answer:**
Fog and Edge Computing methodology is a systematic engineering approach used to design, deploy, and manage distributed computing systems that extend cloud capabilities to the network edge. It defines structured procedures for architectural design, workload partitioning, resource allocation, orchestration, and performance evaluation.

**Three key phases:**
1. **Requirements Elicitation** — capturing QoS constraints (latency, energy, availability).
2. **Workload Partitioning & Offloading** — deciding what runs locally vs. on fog.
3. **Performance Evaluation** — benchmarking against SLA targets using tools like iFogSim or EdgeCloudSim.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Q: Differentiate between static and dynamic offloading methodologies in fog computing.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Offloading | Dynamic Offloading |
|---|---|---|
| **Decision Time** | At compile/deployment time | At runtime |
| **Input Awareness** | Fixed task profile | Real-time context (network, battery) |
| **Adaptability** | Low — does not adapt to changes | High — adapts to channel/load variation |
| **Complexity** | Low | High (requires monitoring infrastructure) |
| **Use Case** | Predictable industrial control | Mobile/vehicular scenarios |
| **Example** | Pre-assigned fog node for sensor X | RL-based task migration in V2X |

> Static offloading is simpler but rigid; dynamic offloading is flexible but adds computational and communication overhead.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** write vague statements like *"fog is better than cloud"* — always back claims with **quantitative metrics** (latency, energy, cost).
> - **Do NOT** skip the **derivation** in offloading decision questions — KTU examiners award marks for the **decision criterion formula** explicitly.
> - **Always** mention **assumptions** (e.g., "assuming negligible result-return delay") when deriving formulas.
> - For 14-mark questions, **sub-parts (a) and (b) each carry 7 marks** — ensure balanced depth.
> - **Common loss of marks**: Students forget to include **units** in the final numerical answer. Always annotate units.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — *OFFICIAL KTU CHOICE A*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the six-phase methodology lifecycle for designing a fog computing system, with a real-world example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the offloading decision criterion considering both **time and energy** as objectives, and apply it to a numerical scenario. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The six-phase methodology lifecycle:

**Phase 1 — Requirements Elicitation:**
Identify the application's functional and non-functional requirements. For example, in a **smart traffic management system** in a KTU campus city like Kochi, requirements include: detect vehicles, count them, predict congestion, and alert drivers — all within **< 200 ms**.

**Phase 2 — Architecture Design:**
Choose a **3-tier architecture**: Cameras (device layer) → Edge gateway (Raspberry Pi class) → Fog server (in the traffic control room). Cloud is used only for historical analytics.

**Phase 3 — Workload Partitioning:**
- Local (edge): Image preprocessing, noise filtering.
- Fog: Vehicle detection using YOLO model, classification.
- Cloud: Long-term trend analysis, ML model retraining.

**Phase 4 — Resource Allocation:**
Allocate CPU shares to containerized microservices (e.g., Docker containers running detection, classification, and storage tasks). Use **cgroup limits** and **Kubernetes QoS classes**.

**Phase 5 — Orchestration:**
Deploy **K3s (lightweight Kubernetes)** on fog node. Enable horizontal pod autoscaling based on traffic density detected by the system itself.

**Phase 6 — Performance Evaluation:**
Benchmark using **EdgeCloudSim** with metrics: end-to-end latency, fog node CPU utilization, network bandwidth consumed, and detection accuracy. Use **Chaos Engineering** to test failure resilience.

**Real-World Mapping:** Kerala's Smart City Mission (Kochi) uses similar methodology — edge cameras send frames to fog nodes that run object detection; results trigger local traffic signal control in real time.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivation of Energy–Latency Aware Offloading Decision:**

Let:
- $W$ = workload (cycles) = $500 \times 10^6$ cycles
- $f_l$ = local CPU freq = $1 \text{ GHz}$
- $f_r$ = fog CPU freq = $3 \text{ GHz}$
- $D_{size}$ = data to upload = $2 \text{ MB} = 16 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}$
- $R_{uplink}$ = uplink rate = $10 \text{ Mbps}$
- $P_l$ = local compute power = $0.8 \text{ W}$
- $P_{tx}$ = transmit power = $1.2 \text{ W}$
- $\lambda$ = weight for time = $0.5$

**Step 1: Local time and energy**

$$
T_{local} = \frac{W}{f_l} = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^9} = 0.5 \text{ s}
$$

$$
E_{local} = P_l \cdot T_{local} = 0.8 \times 0.5 = 0.4 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 2: Remote time and energy**

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{D_{size}}{R_{uplink}} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{10 \times 10^6} = 1.6 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{compute\_remote} = \frac{W}{f_r} = \frac{500 \times 10^6}{3 \times 10^9} \approx 0.1667 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T_{remote} = 1.6 + 0.1667 = 1.7667 \text{ s}
$$

$$
E_{remote} = P_{tx} \cdot T_{tx} = 1.2 \times 1.6 = 1.92 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3: Weighted cost function**

$$
C_{local} = 0.5 \times 0.5 + 0.5 \times 0.4 = 0.25 + 0.20 = 0.45
$$

$$
C_{remote} = 0.5 \times 1.7667 + 0.5 \times 1.92 = 0.8833 + 0.96 = 1.8433
$$

**Step 4: Decision**

$$
C_{local} = 0.45 \ll C_{remote} = 1.8433
$$

**Conclusion:** **Do NOT offload**. Execute locally because the uplink bandwidth is too low, making transmission the dominant cost.

> If $R_{uplink}$ were $100 \text{ Mbps}$, then $T_{tx} = 0.16 \text{ s}$, $C_{remote} \approx 0.563$, and the decision would still be local-first, but the gap would narrow. If $R_{uplink} = 1 \text{ Gbps}$, then $T_{tx} = 0.016 \text{ s}$ and offloading becomes attractive.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *OFFICIAL KTU CHOICE B*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q: (a)** Discuss the key performance metrics used in the evaluation methodology of fog/edge systems. Explain how **latency, energy, and availability** are measured. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fog cluster has 4 nodes with capacities $f_1=2$, $f_2=4$, $f_3=4$, $f_4=8$ GHz. If 10 tasks arrive with workloads $[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 2, 3, 1, 4, 5]$ (in $10^9$ cycles), compute the **makespan** using **longest-processing-time-first (LPT)** scheduling. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Key Performance Metrics in Fog/Edge Evaluation Methodology:**

**1. Latency ($L$):**
- Measured as: $L = T_{response} - T_{request}$ in milliseconds.
- Tools: `ping`, `tcpdump`, custom timestamps, Wireshark.
- KTU-relevant thresholds: V2X < 10 ms, healthcare < 100 ms, gaming < 20 ms.
- **Jitter** = $\sigma(L)$ measures latency stability.

**2. Energy ($E$):**
- Measured using hardware power meters (e.g., Watts Up Pro), or software estimators like `powerstat` (Linux), or model-based: $E = \int P(t) \, dt$.
- Energy efficiency metric: **EPI** (Energy per Instruction) = $\dfrac{E}{\text{Total cycles executed}}$.
- Critical for battery-powered edge devices (smartphones, drones, wearables).

**3. Availability ($A$):**
$$
A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \cdot 100\%
$$
- MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures.
- MTTR = Mean Time To Repair.
- Fog systems target **five-9s (99.999%)** for critical infrastructure.

**Other metrics:**
- **Throughput**: tasks/sec or Mbps.
- **SLA Violation Rate**: % of deadline misses.
- **Cost Efficiency**: \$ per million operations.
- **Scalability**: performance under node addition/removal.

**Methodology Note:** Evaluation is performed using **simulation tools (iFogSim, EdgeCloudSim, CloudSim)** before live deployment, then validated on testbeds (small Raspberry Pi clusters).

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- 4 fog nodes: $f_1 = 2$ GHz, $f_2 = 4$ GHz, $f_3 = 4$ GHz, $f_4 = 8$ GHz.
- 10 tasks (workload in $10^9$ cycles): $[1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 2, 3, 1, 4, 5]$.

**Step 1: Sort tasks in descending order (LPT rule)**

$$
\text{Sorted workloads: } [5, 5, 4, 4, 3, 3, 2, 2, 1, 1] \quad (\text{in } 10^9 \text{ cycles})
$$

**Step 2: Compute execution time for each task on each node**

$$
T_{ij} = \frac{w_i}{f_j}
$$

| Task $w_i$ ($10^9$ cycles) | $T$ on $f_1$=2 GHz | $T$ on $f_2$=4 GHz | $T$ on $f_3$=4 GHz | $T$ on $f_4$=8 GHz |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | 2.5 s | 1.25 s | 1.25 s | 0.625 s |
| 5 | 2.5 s | 1.25 s | 1.25 s | 0.625 s |
| 4 | 2.0 s | 1.0 s | 1.0 s | 0.5 s |
| 4 | 2.0 s | 1.0 s | 1.0 s | 0.5 s |
| 3 | 1.5 s | 0.75 s | 0.75 s | 0.375 s |
| 3 | 1.5 s | 0.75 s | 0.75 s | 0.375 s |
| 2 | 1.0 s | 0.5 s | 0.5 s | 0.25 s |
| 2 | 1.0 s | 0.5 s | 0.5 s | 0.25 s |
| 1 | 0.5 s | 0.25 s | 0.25 s | 0.125 s |
| 1 | 0.5 s | 0.25 s | 0.25 s | 0.125 s |

**Step 3: LPT Scheduling — Assign largest task to node with earliest finish time**

Initialize all node loads = 0.

- Task $w=5$: Earliest finish → assign to $f_4$. Node 4 load = $0.625$.
- Task $w=5$: Next earliest → assign to $f_2$ or $f_3$ (tie at $1.25$). Assign to $f_2$. Node 2 load = $1.25$.
- Task $w=4$: Earliest finish is $f_3 = 1.0$. Assign to $f_3$. Node 3 load = $1.0$.
- Task $w=4$: Earliest finish is $f_4$ (currently $0.625$, add $0.5$ → $1.125$). Or $f_2$ (currently $1.25$, add $1.0$ → $2.25$). So $f_4$ is earliest at $1.125$. Assign to $f_4$. Node 4 load = $1.125$.
- Task $w=3$: Earliest finish — $f_3$ has $1.0 + 0.75 = 1.75$, $f_1$ has $0 + 1.5 = 1.5$. So $f_1$ is earliest. Assign to $f_1$. Node 1 load = $1.5$.
- Task $w=3$: Earliest finish — $f_2$ has $1.25 + 0.75 = 2.0$, $f_3$ has $1.0 + 0.75 = 1.75$, $f_1$ has $1.5 + 1.5 = 3.0$. So $f_3$ is earliest. Assign to $f_3$. Node 3 load = $1.75$.
- Task $w=2$: Earliest finish — $f_1 = 1.5 + 1.0 = 2.5$, $f_2 = 1.25 + 0.5 = 1.75$. So $f_2$ is earliest. Assign to $f_2$. Node 2 load = $1.75$.
- Task $w=2$: Earliest finish — $f_1 = 1.5 + 1.0 = 2.5$, $f_3 = 1.75 + 0.5 = 2.25$, $f_4 = 1.125 + 0.25 = 1.375$. So $f_4$ is earliest. Assign to $f_4$. Node 4 load = $1.375$.
- Task $w=1$: Earliest finish — $f_1 = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2.0$, $f_2 = 1.75 + 0.25 = 2.0$, $f_3 = 1.75 + 0.25 = 2.0$, $f_4 = 1.375 + 0.125 = 1.5$. So $f_4$ is earliest. Assign to $f_4$. Node 4 load = $1.5$.
- Task $w=1$: Earliest finish — $f_1 = 1.5 + 0.5 = 2.0$, $f_2 = 1.75 + 0.25 = 2.0$, $f_3 = 1.75 + 0.25 = 2.0$, $f_4 = 1.5 + 0.125 = 1.625$. So $f_1$ tied with $f_2$ and $f_3$ at $2.0$. Assign to $f_1$. Node 1 load = $2.0$.

**Step 4: Final Node Loads**

| Node | Load (s) |
|---|---|
| $f_1$ (2 GHz) | **2.0** |
| $f_2$ (4 GHz) | **1.75** |
| $f_3$ (4 GHz) | **1.75** |
| $f_4$ (8 GHz) | **1.5** |

**Step 5: Makespan**

$$
T_{makespan} = \max(2.0, 1.75, 1.75, 1.5) = \mathbf{2.0 \text{ seconds}}
$$

**Step 6: Speedup vs. single fastest node**

If all tasks ran on $f_4 = 8$ GHz alone:

$$
T_{single} = \frac{\sum w_i}{f_4} = \frac{30 \times 10^9}{8 \times 10^9} = 3.75 \text{ s}
$$

$$
S_{speedup} = \frac{3.75}{2.0} = 1.875
$$

> **Conclusion:** LPT scheduling achieves a makespan of **2.0 s** with a speedup of **1.875×** over a single-node execution. The bottleneck is $f_1$ (the slowest node). A balanced methodology would either upgrade $f_1$ or migrate its tasks dynamically.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Methodology**

- ✅ **Definition**: Methodology = structured engineering approach (design → deploy → evaluate).
- ✅ **Six Phases**: Requirements → Architecture → Partitioning → Resource Allocation → Orchestration → Evaluation.
- ✅ **Offloading Decision**: Offload iff $C_{remote} < C_{local}$ (weighted time + energy).
- ✅ **Static vs. Dynamic Offloading**: Static = pre-defined; Dynamic = runtime adaptive.
- ✅ **Three Core Metrics**: **Latency** (ms), **Energy** (Joules), **Availability** (%).
- ✅ **Formula to Memorize**:
  - $T_{transmit} = D_{size} / R_{bandwidth}$
  - $E_{total} = P_{compute} \cdot T_{compute} + P_{tx} \cdot T_{transmit}$
  - $T_{makespan} = \max_j(T_j)$ — bottleneck node time
  - $A = MTBF / (MTF + MTTR)$
- ✅ **Tools to Know**: iFogSim, EdgeCloudSim, CloudSim, K3s/KubeEdge, Docker, MQTT, CoAP.
- ✅ **Architecture Tiers**: Device → Edge → Fog → Cloud (4-tier model preferred for clarity).
- ✅ **LPT Scheduling Rule**: Sort tasks in descending order; assign each to the node with the **earliest current finish time**.
- ✅ **KTU Pitfall**: Always **justify your methodology choice** with quantitative metrics, not vague qualitative claims.
- ✅ **Real-World Mapping**: Kerala Smart Cities (Kochi), V2X pilot projects, IIoT in Cochin Shipyard — all use this methodology.
- ✅ **Bloom's Level**: This topic is **Apply / Analyze** (L3–L4) — focus on **why** and **how**, not just definitions.
- ✅ **Marks Distribution**: 3-mark questions = definition/comparison; 14-mark questions = lifecycle + derivation/numerical.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrated C2F2T Literature by Modeling Technique
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Integrated C2F2T Literature by Modeling Technique

# Integrated C2F2T Literature by Modeling Technique

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **C2F2T (Cloud–Fog–Fog–Thing)** continuum refers to the **hierarchical, distributed computing architecture** that integrates three computational tiers — **Cloud (C)**, **Fog (F)**, and **Things (T)** — to provide seamless, low-latency, and scalable services across the IoT–Edge–Cloud stack. The "F2" duplication denotes the **multi-layer fog intermediate tier**, which can be geographically and logically stratified (e.g., near-fog, far-fog, edge-fog).

When studied **by modeling technique**, this involves the systematic **survey, classification, and synthesis of existing research literature** based on the *analytical, simulation, optimization, or learning-based models* employed to characterize, optimize, or evaluate the C2F2T continuum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Interpretation:** "Integrated C2F2T Literature by Modeling Technique" refers to the *taxonomic study* of how researchers have mathematically or computationally modeled the Cloud–Fog–Thing pipeline to solve problems such as **task offloading, latency minimization, energy optimization, resource allocation, and QoS provisioning**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **city's water distribution system**:
- The **Things (T)** are individual households (sensors, IoT devices).
- The **Fog (F)** layers are the **local water tanks and pumping stations** in each neighborhood (edge servers, gateways, micro-data centers).
- The **Cloud (C)** is the **central reservoir and treatment plant** (high-capacity data centers).

Water (data) flows from households up to local tanks, and only critical/summarized data reaches the central reservoir. **Modeling techniques** are like the **engineering blueprints** (hydraulic equations, simulation tools) used by civil engineers to design and optimize the system. Just as engineers use different models for pressure analysis vs. flow optimization, C2F2T researchers use different modeling paradigms to analyze latency vs. energy vs. reliability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Modeling is Crucial:** Pure experimental deployment of fog infrastructure is **cost-prohibitive** at scale. Modeling allows researchers to *predict, optimize, and benchmark* C2F2T behavior *in silico* before physical implementation.

### 1.3 Key Tier Characteristics (Quick Reference)

| Tier | Proximity to Data Source | Latency | Compute Power | Storage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cloud (C)** | Far (Internet-scale) | **High (50–200 ms)** | **Virtually Unlimited** | **Petabyte-scale** |
| **Fog (F)** | Near (LAN/5G) | **Low (1–10 ms)** | **Moderate** | **Terabyte-scale** |
| **Thing (T)** | At source | **Negligible (<1 ms)** | **Constrained** | **Kilobyte–Megabyte** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Modeling Techniques in C2F2T Literature

Researchers have integrated the C2F2T literature along **five dominant modeling paradigms**:

#### A. Analytical / Mathematical Modeling
- **Queueing Theory** (M/M/1, M/M/c, M/G/1 queues) for task arrival and service analysis.
- **Markov Decision Processes (MDP)** for dynamic task offloading.
- **Stochastic Petri Nets (SPN)** for state-transition modeling of fog nodes.

#### B. Optimization-Based Modeling
- **Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP)** for joint task placement and resource allocation.
- **Convex Optimization** for energy-latency trade-offs.
- **Game-Theoretic Models** (non-cooperative, coalition games) for multi-fog resource competition.

#### C. Simulation-Based Modeling
- **Discrete-Event Simulation (DES)** using tools like CloudSim, iFogSim, EdgeCloudSim.
- **Network Simulators** (NS-3, OMNeT++) for communication-layer modeling.

#### D. Machine Learning / AI-Based Modeling
- **Reinforcement Learning (RL/DRL)** for adaptive offloading decisions.
- **Supervised Learning** (DNN, LSTM) for workload prediction in fog nodes.
- **Federated Learning** for distributed training across fog tiers.

#### E. Graph & Network-Theoretic Modeling
- **Task DAGs (Directed Acyclic Graphs)** for dependency-aware scheduling.
- **Graph Neural Networks (GNN)** for topology-aware fog orchestration.
- **Hypergraph Models** for multi-tier task relationships.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Modeling Choice

- **Why Analytical?** → Provides *closed-form bounds*, tractable proofs, and asymptotic guarantees. Best for theoretical guarantees in latency/energy.
- **Why Optimization?** → Captures *multi-objective trade-offs* (latency vs. energy vs. cost) with rigorous constraint handling.
- **Why Simulation?** → Enables *empirical validation* with realistic workloads, mobility models, and network dynamics.
- **Why ML/AI?** → Handles *uncertainty, non-stationarity, and scale* in real-world IoT traffic patterns.
- **Why Graph?** → Naturally represents *multi-tier hierarchies and task dependencies*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are standard, mathematically verified foundations of C2F2T modeling literature.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Total Latency (C2F2T)** | $L_{total} = L_{comm}^{T \rightarrow F} + L_{proc}^{F} + L_{comm}^{F \rightarrow C} + L_{proc}^{C}$ | $L_{comm}$: communication delay; $L_{proc}$: processing delay | **milliseconds (ms)** |
| 2 | **Communication Latency** | $L_{comm} = \frac{D}{R} + \tau_{prop}$ | $D$: data size; $R$: bandwidth; $\tau_{prop}$: propagation delay | ms |
| 3 | **Processing Latency (Fog)** | $L_{proc}^{F} = \frac{\lambda_{task}}{f_{fog}}$ | $\lambda_{task}$: task complexity (CPU cycles); $f_{fog}$: fog CPU freq | ms |
| 4 | **Energy Consumption (Thing)** | $E_{thing} = P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx} + P_{idle} \cdot t_{idle}$ | $P_{tx}$: transmit power; $t_{tx}$: transmit time | **Joules (J)** |
| 5 | **Energy (Fog/Cloud Computing)** | $E_{comp} = \kappa \cdot f^{2} \cdot \lambda_{task}$ | $\kappa$: switching capacitance; $f$: CPU frequency | J |
| 6 | **M/M/1 Queue Response Time** | $T_{queue} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | $\mu$: service rate; $\lambda$: arrival rate | s |
| 7 | **M/M/c Queue Response Time** | $T_{queue}^{c} = \frac{P_{wait}}{\mu c} + \frac{1}{\mu}$ | $c$: number of servers; $P_{wait}$: wait probability | s |
| 8 | **Offloading Decision Utility** | $U_{offload} = \alpha \cdot L_{gain} - \beta \cdot E_{gain} - \gamma \cdot C_{cost}$ | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$: weight coefficients | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Throughput (Fog Layer)** | $\Theta_{fog} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{fog}} \mu_i \cdot \rho_i$ | $N_{fog}$: # fog nodes; $\rho_i$: utilization of node $i$ | tasks/s |
| 10 | **Cost Function (Multi-tier)** | $C_{total} = w_C \cdot C_{cloud} + w_F \cdot C_{fog} + w_T \cdot C_{thing}$ | $w$: tier-specific weights | \$ or units |
| 11 | **Utilization Ratio** | $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\rho < 1$ for stability | dimensionless |
| 12 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = R \cdot RTT$ | $RTT$: round-trip time | bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** In the table above, the **vertical bar symbol `|`** has been replaced with `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. Students writing answers should similarly avoid raw `|` in markdown tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Dominant Modeling Technique Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Healthcare** | Wearable ECG analysis with privacy-preserving fog processing | MDP + Federated Learning |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | V2X real-time obstacle detection | Queueing Theory + RL |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Predictive maintenance on factory floor | Digital Twin + ML |
| **Smart Grid** | Real-time load balancing | MILP + Game Theory |
| **AR/VR Gaming** | Low-latency rendering offload | Convex Optimization + Simulation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: End-to-End C2F2T Latency Model

**Objective:** Derive the total latency for a task flowing from a Thing → Fog → Cloud → Fog → Thing (full round-trip).

**Step 1: Define task variables.**

Let the IoT task have:
- Input data size: $D_i$ (in bits)
- Required CPU cycles: $\lambda_{task}$ (in cycles)
- Output data size: $D_o$ (in bits)

**Step 2: Uplink transmission (Thing → Fog).**

The uplink communication latency depends on the wireless bandwidth $R_{up}$ and propagation delay $\tau_{prop}^{up}$:

$$
L_{up}^{T \rightarrow F} = \frac{D_i}{R_{up}} + \tau_{prop}^{up}
$$

**Step 3: Fog processing latency.**

If the fog node has CPU frequency $f_{fog}$ (cycles/second):

$$
L_{proc}^{F} = \frac{\lambda_{task}}{f_{fog}}
$$

If a queue exists at the fog node (M/M/1 model with arrival rate $\lambda_{arr}$ and service rate $\mu_{fog} = f_{fog} / \overline{\lambda}_{task}$):

$$
L_{queue}^{F} = \frac{1}{\mu_{fog} - \lambda_{arr}}
$$

Total fog latency: $L_{total}^{F} = L_{proc}^{F} + L_{queue}^{F}$

**Step 4: Fog → Cloud transmission.**

$$
L_{trans}^{F \rightarrow C} = \frac{D_i}{R_{core}} + \tau_{prop}^{core}
$$

where $R_{core}$ is the core network bandwidth.

**Step 5: Cloud processing latency.**

Cloud has virtually unlimited resources, but introduces queueing:

$$
L_{proc}^{C} = \frac{\lambda_{task}}{f_{cloud}} + L_{queue}^{C}
$$

**Step 6: Cloud → Fog → Thing return path.**

$$
L_{down}^{C \rightarrow F} = \frac{D_o}{R_{core}} + \tau_{prop}^{core}
$$

$$
L_{down}^{F \rightarrow T} = \frac{D_o}{R_{down}} + \tau_{prop}^{down}
$$

**Step 7: Summation of round-trip latency.**

$$
L_{round-trip} = L_{up}^{T \rightarrow F} + L_{proc}^{F} + L_{trans}^{F \rightarrow C} + L_{proc}^{C} + L_{down}^{C \rightarrow F} + L_{down}^{F \rightarrow T}
$$

This can be compactly expressed as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{C2F2T} &= \left( \frac{D_i}{R_{up}} + \tau_{prop}^{up} \right) + \left( \frac{\lambda_{task}}{f_{fog}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{fog} - \lambda_{arr}} \right) \\
&\quad + \left( \frac{D_i}{R_{core}} + \tau_{prop}^{core} \right) + \left( \frac{\lambda_{task}}{f_{cloud}} + L_{queue}^{C} \right) \\
&\quad + \left( \frac{D_o}{R_{core}} + \tau_{prop}^{core} \right) + \left( \frac{D_o}{R_{down}} + \tau_{prop}^{down} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy-Latency Trade-off Objective Function

**Step 1:** Define decision variable $x \in [0, 1]$ where $x$ = fraction of task offloaded to fog.

**Step 2:** Local execution energy on Thing:

$$
E_{local} = \kappa \cdot f_{T}^{2} \cdot \lambda_{task}
$$

**Step 3:** Offload energy (transmit + fog compute share):

$$
E_{offload} = P_{tx} \cdot \frac{D_i}{R_{up}} + \kappa \cdot f_{fog}^{2} \cdot \lambda_{task}
$$

**Step 4:** Define trade-off objective $J(x)$:

$$
J(x) = (1-x) \cdot E_{local} + x \cdot E_{offload} + \mu \cdot L_{total}(x)
$$

where $\mu$ is the **Lagrange multiplier** balancing energy vs. latency.

**Step 5:** Optimal $x^{*}$ by setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial J}{\partial x} = -E_{local} + E_{offload} + \mu \cdot \frac{\partial L_{total}}{\partial x} = 0
$$

$$
x^{*} = \frac{E_{offload} - E_{local} + \mu \cdot L'_{total}(x^{*})}{2 \mu \cdot L''_{total}(x^{*})}
$$

This derivative forms the basis of **optimal offloading policies** in C2F2T modeling literature.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Modeling Techniques

| Criterion | Analytical | Optimization | Simulation | ML/AI | Graph-Based |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Solution Accuracy** | Medium (bounds) | High (exact/near-optimal) | High (empirical) | Very High (data-driven) | Medium-High |
| **Scalability** | Low–Medium | Medium | High | Very High | Medium |
| **Real-time Capability** | High | Low | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Theoretical Guarantees** | ✓✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ |
| **Handling Uncertainty** | Low | Medium | Medium | ✓✓✓ | Low |
| **Implementation Cost** | Low | High | Medium | High | Medium |
| **Data Requirement** | None | None | Workload traces | Large datasets | Topology |
| **KTU Exam Weightage** | **High** | **High** | **Medium** | **Medium** | **Low** |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
**Define the C2F2T continuum. List the three tiers and their primary characteristic difference.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **C2F2T (Cloud–Fog–Fog–Thing)** continuum is a hierarchical distributed computing architecture that integrates three tiers for IoT service delivery:

1. **Thing (T) Tier:** IoT endpoints (sensors, actuators, wearables). Characterized by **resource-constrained** hardware, minimal compute, but **negligible communication latency** to data source.

2. **Fog (F) Tier:** Intermediate compute layer deployed at network edges (gateways, micro data centers). Provides **moderate compute** with **low latency (1–10 ms)**, bridging things and cloud.

3. **Cloud (C) Tier:** Centralized, virtually unlimited compute and storage with **high latency (50–200 ms)** but massive analytical capability.

**Primary characteristic difference:** *Proximity to data source* vs. *computational capacity* — a fundamental trade-off between latency and processing power. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Name any three modeling techniques used in C2F2T literature. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Queueing Theory (Analytical):** Advantage — provides *closed-form latency bounds* with strong theoretical guarantees. **(1 mark)**

2. **Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (Optimization):** Advantage — captures *multi-objective trade-offs* (energy vs. latency vs. cost) under constraints. **(1 mark)**

3. **Reinforcement Learning / DRL (ML/AI):** Advantage — handles *dynamic, non-stationary* IoT workloads and learns optimal policies without explicit system models. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Confusing *Fog Computing* with *Edge Computing*. Edge = at data source; Fog = one level up. **(−1 mark)**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Writing C2F2T as "Cloud-Fog-Things" without explaining the *F2* (multi-fog layering). **(−1 mark)**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Listing modeling techniques without showing *how* they apply to the C2F2T continuum. **(−2 marks)**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Skipping units in latency/energy formulas. Always mention **ms** or **J**. **(−0.5 mark)**
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Forgetting to state assumptions (e.g., M/M/1 assumes Poisson arrivals). **(−1 mark)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the taxonomy of modeling techniques used in the integrated C2F2T literature. Classify them into **five categories** with one example application each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **end-to-end latency model** $L_{C2F2T}$ for a task offloaded from a Thing to Fog and then to the Cloud. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

The integrated C2F2T literature employs five primary modeling techniques:

| # | Technique | Description | Example Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Analytical Modeling** | Uses mathematical frameworks like queueing theory, MDPs, stochastic Petri nets to derive closed-form performance bounds. | M/M/1 queueing at fog nodes for latency analysis. |
| 2 | **Optimization-Based** | Formulates MILP, convex, or game-theoretic models to optimize resource allocation, task placement, and energy-latency trade-offs. | Joint task offloading + bandwidth allocation via MILP. |
| 3 | **Simulation-Driven** | Uses discrete-event simulators (iFogSim, CloudSim) to emulate realistic C2F2T deployments under variable workloads. | Smart city surveillance workload simulation. |
| 4 | **ML / AI-Based** | Applies DRL, federated learning, or supervised models to learn adaptive offloading policies under uncertainty. | DRL-based V2X offloading in vehicular fog. |
| 5 | **Graph / Network-Theoretic** | Models the C2F2T hierarchy as DAGs, hypergraphs, or GNN-compatible structures for dependency-aware scheduling. | Task DAG scheduling in healthcare IoT. |

**Award 1 mark per correctly described category with example = 5 marks. Additional 2 marks for the introductory sentence and overall synthesis. (7 marks)**

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Assumptions:**
1. Single task flows Thing → Fog → Cloud.
2. Wireless uplink bandwidth $R_{up}$, core bandwidth $R_{core}$.
3. Fog node CPU frequency $f_{fog}$, cloud frequency $f_{cloud}$.
4. M/M/1 queue at fog with arrival rate $\lambda_{arr}$, service rate $\mu_{fog}$.
5. Task requires $\lambda_{task}$ CPU cycles.

**Step 1: Uplink Thing → Fog latency.** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
L_{up}^{T \rightarrow F} = \frac{D_i}{R_{up}} + \tau_{prop}^{up}
$$

**Step 2: Fog processing + queueing latency.** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
L_{proc}^{F} = \frac{\lambda_{task}}{f_{fog}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{fog} - \lambda_{arr}}
$$

**Step 3: Fog → Cloud transmission latency.** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
L_{trans}^{F \rightarrow C} = \frac{D_i}{R_{core}} + \tau_{prop}^{core}
$$

**Step 4: Cloud processing latency.** **(1.5 marks)**

$$
L_{proc}^{C} = \frac{\lambda_{task}}{f_{cloud}}
$$

**Step 5: Total end-to-end C2F2T latency.** **(1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{C2F2T} &= L_{up}^{T \rightarrow F} + L_{proc}^{F} + L_{trans}^{F \rightarrow C} + L_{proc}^{C} \\
&= \frac{D_i}{R_{up}} + \tau_{prop}^{up} + \frac{\lambda_{task}}{f_{fog}} + \frac{1}{\mu_{fog} - \lambda_{arr}} + \frac{D_i}{R_{core}} + \tau_{prop}^{core} + \frac{\lambda_{task}}{f_{cloud}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(Total: 7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the **role of Queueing Theory and Markov Decision Processes (MDP)** as analytical modeling techniques in C2F2T literature. Compare their suitability for fog-tier modeling. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how **Reinforcement Learning (DRL)** is applied to the C2F2T offloading problem. State the **state, action, and reward** formulation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Queueing Theory in C2F2T (3.5 marks):**
- Models task arrival and service at fog nodes as stochastic processes (M/M/1, M/M/c, M/G/1).
- Provides **mean response time**: $T = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ (M/M/1).
- Used to compute **waiting time, utilization ($\rho = \lambda / \mu$), and throughput**.
- **Suitability for Fog Tier:** Excellent for fog because fog nodes have *bounded resources* and *Poisson-like IoT task arrivals*. Provides *closed-form latency bounds* essential for **real-time guarantees**.

**MDP in C2F2T (3.5 marks):**
- Models the dynamic decision-making of an IoT device choosing between local, fog, or cloud execution.
- Components: **State** (network conditions, queue length, energy), **Action** (offload decision), **Transition Probability** (P(s'|s,a)), **Reward** (−latency, −energy).
- Solved via **Bellman equation**: $V(s) = \max_a \left[ R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \mid s,a) V(s') \right]$
- **Suitability:** Best for *sequential decision-making under uncertainty*. Captures time-varying workloads better than static queueing models.

**Comparative Verdict:** Queueing theory provides **steady-state analytical bounds**; MDP captures **dynamic optimality**. Both complement each other in C2F2T modeling.

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Example Scenario:** Autonomous vehicle deciding whether to process object detection **locally**, on a **roadside fog unit**, or in the **cloud**.

**DRL Formulation for C2F2T Offloading (7 marks):**

| Component | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Agent** | The IoT device / orchestrator | Autonomous vehicle ECU |
| **State ($s_t$)** | System snapshot at time $t$ | (queue length at fog, vehicle speed, network bandwidth, remaining battery) |
| **Action ($a_t$)** | Offloading decision | $a_t \in \{\text{Local}, \text{Fog}, \text{Cloud}\}$ |
| **Reward ($r_t$)** | Negative cost (to maximize) | $r_t = -(\alpha \cdot L_{total} + \beta \cdot E_{consumed})$ |
| **Policy ($\pi$)** | Mapping state → action | $\pi(a_t \mid s_t)$ |
| **Value Function** | Expected cumulative reward | $V^{\pi}(s) = \mathbb{E}\left[ \sum_{t=0}^{\infty} \gamma^t r_t \mid s_0 = s \right]$ |

**Algorithm Used:** Deep Q-Network (DQN) or Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) for continuous action spaces.

**Why DRL for C2F2T?**
- Handles **non-stationary** IoT traffic.
- **Model-free**: does not need exact system dynamics.
- **Adaptive**: learns from experience as the C2F2T environment evolves.
- Achieves **near-optimal offloading** with **sub-millisecond inference** at runtime.

**(Award full 7 marks for clear formulation with all five components + justification.)**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **C2F2T = Cloud – Fog (multi-layer) – Thing** continuum.
- ✅ **Three tiers:** Thing (sensors, constrained), Fog (edge, low latency), Cloud (centralized, high capacity).
- ✅ **Five modeling categories:** Analytical, Optimization, Simulation, ML/AI, Graph-Theoretic.
- ✅ **Queueing theory** (M/M/1, M/M/c) is the **most common analytical tool** for fog-tier latency modeling.
- ✅ **MILP** is the dominant optimization model for joint offloading + resource allocation.
- ✅ **iFogSim** is the most widely used simulator for fog C2F2T evaluation.
- ✅ **DRL (Deep Reinforcement Learning)** is the most popular AI technique for *adaptive* offloading.
- ✅ **Key trade-off in C2F2T:** **Latency vs. Energy vs. Cost** — captured in the multi-objective objective $J = \alpha L + \beta E + \gamma C$.
- ✅ **Latency formula:** $L_{C2F2T}$ = sum of all communication + processing + queueing delays across tiers.
- ✅ **M/M/1 response time:** $T = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$; stability requires $\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} < 1$.
- ✅ **Energy for transmission:** $E = P_{tx} \cdot t_{tx}$; for computation: $E = \kappa f^2 \lambda_{task}$.
- ✅ **Always state assumptions** in KTU answers — e.g., Poisson arrivals, single-task flow, time-invariance.
- ✅ **Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ instead of `|`** inside markdown tables to avoid syntax errors.
- ✅ **Differentiate Edge vs. Fog:** Edge = at device; Fog = between edge and cloud (network-level).
- ✅ **Examiners reward** clean diagrams (Mermaid/flowchart), proper units, and crisp definitions of state/action/reward in DRL-based answers.
