# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Offloading Decision Rule

This is the **single most important derivation** for any KTU exam on fog optimization. We derive the *binary offloading rule* from first principles, then extend it to a *multi-tier rule*.

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Problem

Consider a single IoT device (edge) with processing capability $f_{edge}$ cycles/sec. It must execute a task of workload $L$ CPU-cycles. Two options exist:

- **Option A — Execute locally** on the edge device.
- **Option B — Offload to a fog node** with capability $f_{fog}$ cycles/sec, connected over a wireless link of transmission rate $r$ bits/sec.

Let the output result size be $S$ bits (often $S \ll L$ for inference tasks, e.g., $L = 10^9$ cycles, $S = 256$ bits for a class label).

### 3.1.2 Time to Execute Locally

We model local execution as a single serial computation. The wall-clock time is workload divided by capability:

$$T_{local} = \frac{L}{f_{edge}}$$

**Step-by-step reasoning:**
1. The device has one CPU core operating at $f_{edge}$ cycles per second.
2. The task requires $L$ CPU-cycles to complete (compiler-measured or estimated).
3. In the ideal case (no contention, no I/O blocking), time = work ÷ rate.
4. Therefore, $T_{local} = L / f_{edge}$ seconds.

### 3.1.3 Time to Offload

Offloading involves **three sub-stages** that must be summed because they execute sequentially:

1. **Transmit** the task input to the fog node: takes $L_{in} / r$ seconds, where $L_{in}$ is the input size in bits. (For analytical simplicity in textbook treatments, $L_{in}$ is often approximated by the workload parameter $L$.)
2. **Execute** on the fog node: takes $L / f_{fog}$ seconds.
3. **Transmit back** the result: takes $S / r$ seconds.

The first sub-stage is *up-link transmission*, the second is *fog compute*, the third is *down-link transmission*. A frequently used simplification in KTU-level problems is the **symmetric link assumption**, where uplink and downlink rates are equal to $r$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{offload} &= \underbrace{\frac{L}{r}}_{\text{uplink}} + \underbrace{\frac{L}{f_{fog}}}_{\text{fog compute}} + \underbrace{\frac{S}{r}}_{\text{downlink}} + \underbrace{T_{queue}^{fog}}_{\text{queuing delay}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a first-order derivation, we set $S \approx 0$ (negligible result size, e.g., a class label) and ignore queuing:

$$T_{offload} \approx \frac{L}{r} + \frac{L}{f_{fog}}$$

### 3.1.4 Deriving the Decision Rule

We now apply the *latency-minimization* optimization objective: choose the option that yields smaller time. The optimal binary rule is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{offload} &< T_{local} \\[4pt]
\frac{L}{r} + \frac{L}{f_{fog}} &< \frac{L}{f_{edge}} \\[4pt]
\frac{1}{r} + \frac{1}{f_{fog}} &< \frac{1}{f_{edge}} \quad \text{(divide both sides by } L \text{)} \\[4pt]
\therefore \quad \text{Offload} &\iff \frac{1}{r} + \frac{1}{f_{fog}} < \frac{1}{f_{edge}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation of the rule:**

- If the **edge device is slow** (small $f_{edge}$, hence large $1/f_{edge}$), offloading becomes attractive.
- If the **fog node is fast** (large $f_{fog}$, hence small $1/f_{fog}$), offloading becomes attractive.
- If the **uplink is fast** (large $r$, hence small $1/r$), offloading becomes attractive.
- Otherwise, keep the task local.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **cleanest KTU derivation** for a 7-mark sub-part. Memorize the logic, not just the formula.

### 3.1.5 Extension — Energy-Aware Decision Rule

A parallel derivation applies when the objective is energy minimization. Let $P_{tx}$ = transmit power (W), $P_{edge}^{proc}$ = edge processing power (W), $P_{fog}^{proc}$ = fog processing power per unit task (W·s/cycle).

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{local} &= P_{edge}^{proc} \cdot \frac{L}{f_{edge}} \\[4pt]
E_{offload} &= P_{tx} \cdot \frac{L}{r} + P_{fog}^{proc} \cdot \frac{L}{f_{fog}} + P_{rx} \cdot \frac{S}{r} \\[4pt]
\text{Offload} &\iff E_{offload} < E_{local}
\end{aligned}
$$

For battery-powered IoT devices, this rule is often **more important** than the latency rule.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — End-to-End Latency Decomposition

This derivation is the foundation of **cross-layer optimization**. We decompose $T_{e2e}$ into its atomic components.

### 3.2.1 The Full-Path Walk

Imagine a sensor → edge gateway → fog node → cloud data center path. The end-to-end latency from sensor actuation to cloud response is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{e2e} = \; & T_{sense} \quad \text{(sensor sampling + ADC)} \\
& + T_{proc}^{edge} \quad \text{(edge gateway compute)} \\
& + T_{trans}^{e2f} \quad \text{(edge-to-fog transmission)} \\
& + T_{queue}^{fog} \quad \text{(queueing at fog)} \\
& + T_{proc}^{fog} \quad \text{(fog compute)} \\
& + T_{trans}^{f2c} \quad \text{(fog-to-cloud transmission)} \\
& + T_{queue}^{cloud} \quad \text{(queueing at cloud)} \\
& + T_{proc}^{cloud} \quad \text{(cloud compute)} \\
& + T_{trans}^{c2e} \quad \text{(cloud-to-edge response)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.2 Optimization Insight

Each of these 9 terms is an **independent optimization opportunity**. A cross-layer optimizer minimizes their *sum* subject to constraints (deadline $T_{deadline}$, energy budget $E_{budget}$):

$$\min \; T_{e2e} \quad \text{subject to} \quad T_{e2e} \leq T_{deadline} \; \wedge \; E_{total} \leq E_{budget}$$

A typical strategy:
1. *Reduce* $T_{sense}$ via adaptive sampling.
2. *Reduce* $T_{trans}^{e2f}$ via edge filtering.
3. *Reduce* $T_{queue}^{fog}$ via load balancing.
4. *Reduce* $T_{proc}^{cloud}$ via result caching.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resource Utilization Optimization

### 3.3.1 The Utilization Metric

For a fog node with $n$ resources of different kinds (CPU, RAM, disk, network ports), utilization is:

$$U = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\text{UsedResource}_j}{\text{TotalResource}_j}$$

### 3.3.2 Why This Matters

A node with $U \to 0$ is **wasting** power (idle servers consume 50–70% of peak power). A node with $U \to 1$ is **overloaded** (queuing delays blow up). The optimal target is typically $U^{\star} \in [0.6, 0.8]$ for SLA-safe operation.

### 3.3.3 Derivation of the Power-Utilization Relationship (server-level)

Modern servers follow a linear power model:

$$P(U) = P_{idle} + (P_{peak} - P_{idle}) \cdot U$$

Hence the **energy per request** served is:

$$E_{per\_req} = \frac{P(U) \cdot T_{req}}{1} = P(U) \cdot T_{req}$$

Optimizing $E_{per\_req}$ is equivalent to jointly optimizing $P$ (via $U$) and $T_{req}$ (via load balancing). This is the **canonical energy-aware fog scheduling** problem.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Optimization Techniques at Each Layer

This is your **single most valuable exam revision table** for the 14-mark question. It directly maps *layer → opportunity → technique → metric improved*.

| Architectural Layer | Optimization Opportunity | Technique / Lever | Primary Metric Improved | Secondary Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Edge / IoT Device** | Reduce raw data volume | Data filtering, aggregation, compression | Bandwidth ↓ | Energy ↓, Latency ↓ |
| **Edge / IoT Device** | Reduce local compute cost | Adaptive sampling rate | Energy ↑ (saved) | Latency stable |
| **Edge / IoT Device** | Avoid unnecessary transmission | Transmission scheduling (duty cycling) | Energy ↑ (saved) | Network contention ↓ |
| **Edge / IoT Device** | On-device intelligence | TinyML / edge inference | Latency ↓ | Privacy ↑ |
| **Fog Node** | Decide compute location | Computational offloading (binary/partial) | Latency ↓ | Energy ↓ (often) |
| **Fog Node** | Decide workload placement | VM/container placement (LP, heuristic) | Utilization ↑ | Latency variance ↓ |
| **Fog Node** | Avoid recomputation | Caching (data, code, results) | Latency ↓ | Bandwidth ↓ |
| **Fog Node** | Distribute load | Load balancing (round-robin, least-loaded, ML) | Latency ↓ | Utilization ↑ |
| **Fog Node** | Free overloaded node | Service migration / live VM migration | Availability ↑ | Latency ↓ |
| **Network** | Avoid congestion | SDN-based routing / traffic engineering | Latency ↓ | Jitter ↓ |
| **Network** | Prioritize critical traffic | QoS / DiffServ / network slicing | SLA compliance ↑ | Latency ↓ |
| **Network** | Reduce broadcast storms | Multicast for firmware / updates | Bandwidth ↑ (saved) | Energy ↑ |
| **Network** | Move compute to data | NFV / in-network computing | Latency ↓ | New capability |
| **Cloud** | Avoid cold starts | Pre-warmed containers / pools | Latency ↓ | Cost ↑ (trade-off) |
| **Cloud** | Reduce wasted compute | Auto-scaling, rightsizing | Cost ↓ | Utilization ↑ |
| **Cloud** | Avoid re-analysis | Result caching at cloud | Latency ↓ | Cost ↓ |
| **Cross-layer** | Joint decision-making | Lyapunov, MDP, DRL, heuristic co-optimization | Multiple KPIs | Complexity ↑ |
| **Cross-layer** | SLA-aware adaptation | Closed-loop control (sense-decide-act) | All KPIs | Resilience ↑ |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Smart Note:** When asked "List optimization opportunities in fog", never write a generic list. **Group by layer** as above. Examiners award full marks for clear *structure*, not just for content.

---

## 3.5 The Trade-off Triangle — A Deep Insight

A central KTU-level insight is that **no optimization is free**. Most improvements along one axis degrade another. The **optimization triangle** has three corners:

1. **Latency** ↓
2. **Energy** ↓
3. **Cost** ↓

You can usually optimize **two** corners simultaneously, but only at the expense of the **third**. For example:
- *Aggressive offloading* ↓ latency, but ↑ energy at the transmitter and ↑ cost (fog node rental).
- *Aggressive caching* ↓ latency, but ↑ storage cost.
- *Aggressive local processing* ↓ energy of transmission, but ↑ edge energy and possibly ↑ latency (slower edge).

This is the **fundamental trade-off** in fog optimization. Cross-layer algorithms exist *precisely* to find the Pareto-optimal balance.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Pareto Front:} \quad \min_{x} \; & F(x) = (T_{e2e}(x), \; E_{total}(x), \; C_{total}(x)) \\
\text{subject to} \quad & x \in \mathcal{X} \; \text{(feasible decision set)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A point $x^{\star}$ is **Pareto-optimal** if no other feasible $x$ can improve one objective without worsening another. Fog optimizers seek such points.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are short-answer questions requiring precise, syllabus-aligned definitions. Each answer is 4–6 lines.

---

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]`

**Define the term "Optimization Opportunity" in the context of fog computing architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An *optimization opportunity* in fog computing is a **decision point** distributed across the layered fog architecture (edge, fog node, network, cloud) where a system parameter — such as workload placement, transmission scheduling, caching, routing, or resource allocation — can be deliberately tuned to improve one or more Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) like latency, energy, bandwidth, cost, or reliability. Optimization opportunities are not "features" but *control knobs* that an orchestration layer (broker, SDN controller, or orchestrator) manipulates to meet SLAs. They may be **layer-local** (e.g., edge filtering) or **cross-layer** (e.g., joint offloading + routing).

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q]`

**List any four optimization opportunities available at the fog-node layer of the fog architecture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The four optimization opportunities at the fog-node layer are:
1. **Computational offloading decision** — choosing whether a task executes locally on the edge or on the fog node.
2. **VM/Container placement** — deciding which workload runs on which physical fog node.
3. **Caching strategy** — selecting what data, code, or result to cache at the fog node to avoid recomputation.
4. **Load balancing** — distributing incoming requests across sibling fog nodes to prevent overloading and minimize queuing delay.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with **internal choice between two alternatives (Q-A or Q-B)**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two fully independent 14-mark questions; the student answers **one** of them.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**"Optimization opportunities along the fog architecture are inherently multi-layered and multi-objective." Discuss this statement with reference to (a) the classification of optimization opportunities by architectural layer, and (b) the binary offloading decision rule with its latency-based derivation.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

The statement is **correct and central** to the 2024-scheme understanding of fog computing. The fog architecture is deliberately organized into hierarchical tiers — **Edge (Tier 1)**, **Fog Node (Tier 2)**, **Network**, and **Cloud (Tier 3)** — and each tier exhibits a distinct optimization opportunity surface. The *multi-layered* nature arises because each tier owns a different kind of resource (sensing, compute, transit, storage), and a single decision made at one tier (e.g., "do I sample at 1 kHz or 100 Hz?") cascades through all downstream tiers.

**Classification by layer:**

1. **Edge-layer opportunities** — These target the resource scarcity of IoT devices. The chief opportunities are:
   - *Data filtering and aggregation* — dropping redundant samples.
   - *Adaptive sampling* — varying sensor rate based on event importance.
   - *Local preprocessing* — compression, encryption, light-weight inference (TinyML).
   - *Transmission duty-cycling* — sending data only when needed.

2. **Fog-node-layer opportunities** — These target the decision-making core. The chief opportunities are:
   - *Computational offloading* (binary or partial).
   - *VM/Container placement* (linear programming, heuristic, ML-based).
   - *Caching* (data, code, results).
   - *Load balancing* (round-robin, least-loaded, predictive).
   - *Service migration* (live VM/container migration under load).

3. **Network-layer opportunities** — These target the transit bottleneck. The chief opportunities are:
   - *SDN-based routing* and traffic engineering.
   - *QoS / DiffServ* for traffic prioritization.
   - *Network slicing* for isolating critical flows.
   - *In-network computing* via NFV.

4. **Cloud-layer opportunities** — These target the cost dimension. The chief opportunities are:
   - *Workload consolidation* and *auto-scaling*.
   - *Storage tiering* (hot vs. cold).
   - *Result caching* to avoid repeated analysis.

5. **Cross-layer opportunities** — The most powerful class. Examples:
   - *Joint offloading + caching + routing* (treated as a single optimization).
   - *End-to-end latency minimization* spanning all tiers.
   - *Energy-aware cross-tier scheduling* where edge, fog, and cloud clocks are jointly coordinated.

The *multi-objective* aspect emerges because improvements in one KPI (e.g., latency) often degrade another (e.g., energy or cost). For example, aggressive offloading reduces latency but raises transmission energy and fog-rental cost. Hence, fog optimization is best formulated as a **multi-objective Pareto optimization problem** with a feasible decision set spanning all tiers.

(7 marks distributed: 2 marks for stating the multi-layered nature, 3 marks for listing the five-layer classification, 2 marks for the multi-objective trade-off and Pareto framing.)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Model Solution — The Binary Offloading Decision Rule:**

**Setup.** Let an edge device have processing capability $f_{edge}$ (cycles/s) and be connected to a fog node of capability $f_{fog}$ (cycles/s) over a wireless uplink of rate $r$ (bits/s). A task of workload $L$ (CPU-cycles) arrives. We must choose between local execution and offloading.

**Step 1 — Time if executed locally.**

In the ideal no-contention case, time = work / rate:

$$T_{local} = \frac{L}{f_{edge}} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 2 — Time if offloaded.**

Offloading involves three sequential sub-stages: uplink transmission, fog compute, downlink transmission. For a small result $S \ll L$ and negligible queuing:

$$T_{offload} \approx \frac{L}{r} + \frac{L}{f_{fog}} \quad \text{seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the latency-minimization objective.**

We offload if and only if offloading is faster:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{offload} &< T_{local} \\[4pt]
\frac{L}{r} + \frac{L}{f_{fog}} &< \frac{L}{f_{edge}} \\[4pt]
\frac{1}{r} + \frac{1}{f_{fog}} &< \frac{1}{f_{edge}} \quad \text{(dividing both sides by } L > 0)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**

The rule states that offloading is beneficial when the **sum of inverse transmission rate and inverse fog-node capability** is strictly less than the **inverse of edge capability**. This is intuitive:
- If $f_{edge}$ is small (slow edge), the right side $1/f_{edge}$ is large, so the rule is easily satisfied.
- If $f_{fog}$ is large (fast fog), $1/f_{fog}$ is small, again satisfying the rule.
- If $r$ is large (fast link), $1/r$ is small, again satisfying the rule.

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example.**

Suppose $L = 10^9$ cycles, $f_{edge} = 10^8$ cycles/s, $f_{fog} = 10^9$ cycles/s, $r = 10^7$ bits/s (treating $L$ also as the bits to send).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{local} &= \frac{10^9}{10^8} = 10 \text{ s} \\[4pt]
T_{offload} &= \frac{10^9}{10^7} + \frac{10^9}{10^9} = 100 + 1 = 101 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $101 > 10$, the rule says **do not offload**; execute locally. This example illustrates that offloading is **not always beneficial** — the wireless link is often the bottleneck.

**Step 6 — Energy-aware parallel rule.**

An analogous derivation gives the energy-aware offloading rule, where $P_{tx}$, $P_{edge}$, $P_{fog}$ are respective power draws:

$$E_{local} = P_{edge} \cdot \frac{L}{f_{edge}}, \quad E_{offload} = P_{tx} \cdot \frac{L}{r} + P_{fog} \cdot \frac{L}{f_{fog}}$$

$$\text{Offload} \iff E_{offload} < E_{local}$$

This dual rule is critical for battery-powered IoT devices.

(7 marks distributed: 1 mark for setup, 2 marks for the two time expressions, 2 marks for the inequality and its interpretation, 1 mark for numerical example, 1 mark for the energy-aware rule extension.)

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Q]`

**With suitable diagrams, explain the layered optimization opportunities in fog computing. Specifically, discuss (a) the end-to-end latency decomposition and the optimization levers it exposes, and (b) the closed-loop "Sense–Decide–Act–Monitor–Adapt" optimization cycle with its engineering significance.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Model Solution:**

The end-to-end latency $T_{e2e}$ from sensor actuation to final response is the most important KPI in fog systems, especially for latency-critical applications (telemedicine, autonomous driving, AR/VR). It can be decomposed into **nine atomic terms** traversing the entire architecture:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{e2e} = \; & T_{sense} \\
& + T_{proc}^{edge} \\
& + T_{trans}^{e2f} \\
& + T_{queue}^{fog} \\
& + T_{proc}^{fog} \\
& + T_{trans}^{f2c} \\
& + T_{queue}^{cloud} \\
& + T_{proc}^{cloud} \\
& + T_{trans}^{c2e}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Optimization lever for each term:**

| Term | Meaning | Optimization Lever |
|---|---|---|
| $T_{sense}$ | Sensor sampling + ADC | Adaptive sampling, event-driven sensing |
| $T_{proc}^{edge}$ | Local compute on edge gateway | Edge inference, hardware acceleration |
| $T_{trans}^{e2f}$ | Edge-to-fog transmission | Edge filtering, compression, batching |
| $T_{queue}^{fog}$ | Queuing delay at fog | Load balancing, admission control |
| $T_{proc}^{fog}$ | Fog compute time | VM placement, parallelization, caching |
| $T_{trans}^{f2c}$ | Fog-to-cloud transmission | Result compression, async upload |
| $T_{queue}^{cloud}$ | Queuing at cloud | Auto-scaling, priority queues |
| $T_{proc}^{cloud}$ | Cloud compute time | Pre-warmed containers, cache reuse |
| $T_{trans}^{c2e}$ | Cloud-to-edge response | Result push, pre-fetching |

**Formal optimization problem:**

$$\min_{x} \; T_{e2e}(x) \quad \text{subject to} \quad T_{e2e}(x) \leq T_{deadline} \; \wedge \; E_{total}(x) \leq E_{budget}$$

where $x$ is the vector of decision variables (sampling rate, offload decision, routing path, VM placement, etc.).

**Engineering insight:** A common pitfall is to optimize only $T_{proc}^{fog}$ (the most visible term), but in practice $T_{trans}^{e2f}$ and $T_{queue}^{fog}$ often dominate in 5G/IoT deployments. Hence, **edge filtering and fog load balancing** frequently yield the largest absolute latency reductions — a non-obvious but practically crucial insight.

(7 marks: 2 marks for the decomposition, 3 marks for the lever table, 2 marks for the formal optimization and the engineering insight.)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Model Solution — The Five-Phase Closed-Loop Optimization Cycle:**

Optimization in fog systems is **not a one-shot computation**; it is a **continuous control loop** that runs perpetually. The KTU 2024 syllabus identifies five sequential phases:

**Phase 1 — Sense.**
The fog orchestrator (or a local controller at the fog node) collects real-time telemetry: CPU utilization, memory pressure, queue length, link latency, battery level, deadline-miss rate, etc. The optimization lever here is the *sampling rate* of telemetry itself — too high wastes bandwidth, too low misses fast events.

**Phase 2 — Decide.**
The controller runs an optimization algorithm — a Linear Program (LP), a heuristic (greedy, genetic), or a Machine Learning model (RL, supervised). The lever here is the *algorithm complexity vs. accuracy trade-off*; complex models give better decisions but consume more decision time.

**Phase 3 — Act.**
The decision is enforced: a task is offloaded, a VM is migrated, a route is changed, a container is scaled. The lever here is the *migration cost vs. benefit* — moving a VM takes time and bandwidth, which must be amortized.

**Phase 4 — Monitor.**
The effect of the action is observed: did latency drop? did energy go down? did the SLA violation rate fall? The lever here is the *monitoring overhead* — observation itself consumes resources.

**Phase 5 — Adapt.**
The policy/model is updated based on observed outcomes. In ML-based controllers, this is the *learning step*. The lever is the *learning rate* and *drift-detection sensitivity* — too eager a learner overfits to noise.

**Engineering significance:**
1. The loop is what makes fog systems **resilient** to dynamic workloads.
2. Without the *Monitor* and *Adapt* phases, the system is **open-loop** and degrades under drift.
3. The loop is the foundation of **self-optimizing** or **self-healing** fog platforms, which is a key research and industry direction.

(7 marks: 1 mark per phase = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for the engineering significance.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing a generic list of "benefits of fog" instead of "optimization opportunities".** The examiner specifically tests your ability to identify *decision points* (verbs: filter, offload, place, route, cache) and not just abstract *advantages* (low latency, scalability). Lists of advantages earn 2–3 marks; lists of decision-point opportunities earn full marks.
> 2. **Confusing fog computing with cloud computing.** Many students default to "cloud-like" answers (auto-scaling, virtualization) and forget *edge-specific* opportunities like adaptive sampling, duty cycling, and TinyML. Examiner dock 2 marks for this.
> 3. **Skipping the trade-off triangle.** Stating "offloading reduces latency" without acknowledging that it *increases* transmission energy and cost is a one-sided answer. Always mention the trade-off.
> 4. **No diagram in a 14-mark answer.** Even a hand-drawn 3-tier architecture block diagram earns 1–2 marks. Not drawing one is a guaranteed loss.
> 5. **Forgetting the closed-loop aspect.** A 14-mark question that asks for "optimization process" expects the **five-phase loop**, not a single decision snapshot. Without the loop, you lose 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Mixing units in formulas.** Writing $T_{local} = L/f$ with $L$ in bits and $f$ in cycles/s is a unit error. Always be explicit: $L$ in CPU-cycles, $f$ in cycles/s, giving time in seconds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Memorize This Block.**

- **Definition:** Optimization opportunities are **decision points** at each fog tier that can be tuned to improve KPIs.
- **Five tiers of opportunities:** Edge → Fog Node → Network → Cloud → Cross-layer.
- **Five KPIs:** Latency, Energy, Bandwidth, Cost, Reliability.
- **Edge layer** optimizes: bandwidth, energy of transmission, local storage.
- **Fog-node layer** optimizes: latency, utilization, load distribution.
- **Network layer** optimizes: routing, congestion, QoS, slicing.
- **Cloud layer** optimizes: cost, big-data batching, storage tiering.
- **Cross-layer** is the only class that can *jointly* move all KPIs.
- **Binary offloading rule:** Offload iff $\frac{1}{r} + \frac{1}{f_{fog}} < \frac{1}{f_{edge}}$.
- **Energy-aware offloading rule:** Offload iff $E_{offload} < E_{local}$.
- **End-to-end latency** decomposes into **9 atomic terms**, each with its own lever.
- **Five-phase optimization loop:** Sense → Decide → Act → Monitor → Adapt. Always mention this for "process"-type questions.
- **Trade-off triangle:** Latency ↔ Energy ↔ Cost — optimize two, sacrifice the third.
- **Pareto-optimal** is the right framework for multi-objective fog optimization.
- **Real-world examples to cite:** Telemedicine (sub-10 ms), autonomous vehicles (<50 ms loop), smart grid (15–20% peak reduction), Industry 4.0 (30–60% bandwidth savings).
- **Most-tested KTU derivations:** (i) binary offloading rule, (ii) end-to-end latency decomposition, (iii) energy-aware offloading rule.
- **Most-tested KTU diagrams:** (i) three-tier fog architecture with optimization knobs, (ii) five-phase loop, (iii) cross-layer KPI mapping.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Do not confuse "benefits of fog" with "optimization opportunities" — the former is a marketing claim, the latter is an engineering lever.
- **Examiner's favourite pairing:** Layer ↔ KPI (e.g., Edge ↔ Energy, Fog ↔ Latency, Network ↔ Bandwidth, Cloud ↔ Cost).
- **One-line definition for definitions section:** *Optimization opportunities in fog architecture are the tunable control points distributed across edge, fog-node, network, and cloud tiers that allow trade-off-aware improvement of latency, energy, bandwidth, cost, and reliability.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optimization Opportunities along the Service Life Cycle
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Optimization Opportunities along the Service Life Cycle

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Optimization Opportunities along the Service Life Cycle

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Service Life Cycle Optimization** in Fog and Edge Computing refers to the systematic identification, analysis, and application of techniques to maximize the efficiency, performance, cost-effectiveness, and reliability of distributed computing services across all stages of their operational existence — from design and provisioning through deployment, execution, maintenance, and eventual decommissioning.

The service life cycle in fog/edge environments typically encompasses the following sequential phases:
1. **Service Design & Composition Phase**
2. **Resource Provisioning & Deployment Phase**
3. **Service Execution & Operation Phase**
4. **Monitoring & Adaptation Phase**
5. **Decommissioning / Migration Phase**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Optimization is not a one-time activity; it is a **continuous, cross-cutting concern** that touches every layer of the fog/edge stack — from hardware and virtualization to application logic and user-facing QoS.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smart Restaurant Kitchen**
>
> Think of a fog/edge computing service as a **busy restaurant kitchen** that takes orders (user requests) and delivers dishes (responses).
>
> - **Design Phase** = Planning the menu and recipe (deciding what dishes to serve and how).
> - **Provisioning** = Stocking ingredients and assigning chefs to stations (allocating CPU, RAM, storage at fog nodes).
> - **Execution** = Cooking dishes in real-time (running application logic, processing IoT data).
> - **Monitoring** = The head chef walking around tasting food and checking the clock (tracking latency, energy, errors).
> - **Decommissioning** = Closing the kitchen for renovation or shifting to a new location.
>
> **Optimization at each phase** means: choosing the best recipes, minimizing food waste (energy), reducing wait time (latency), using fewer chefs without compromising quality (resource consolidation), and switching to a new kitchen smoothly (migration).

### Key Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Reference Values)

The following metrics are universally referenced when evaluating optimization opportunities:

- **Latency (L):** Measured in **milliseconds (ms)** — Target for ultra-low latency applications: **< 10 ms**
- **Throughput (T):** Measured in **requests/second (req/s)** or **Mbps/Gbps**
- **Energy Consumption (E):** Measured in **Watts (W)** or **Joules (J)**
- **Cost (C):** Measured in **USD/month** or **Cost-per-Transaction**
- **Service Availability (A):** Measured as a **percentage (%)** — Industry SLA target: **99.9% – 99.999%**
- **Resource Utilization (U):** Expressed as a **percentage (%)** of CPU, RAM, bandwidth, storage
- **QoS (Quality of Service):** Composite metric combining L, T, A, and jitter

### Classification of Optimization Goals

| Optimization Class | Primary Objective | Typical KTU Sub-Topic |
|---|---|---|
| **Performance Optimization** | Reduce latency, increase throughput | Offloading, caching, load balancing |
| **Resource Optimization** | Maximize utilization, minimize waste | VM consolidation, container scheduling |
| **Energy Optimization** | Reduce power consumption | DVFS, sleep states, renewable integration |
| **Cost Optimization** | Minimize OPEX/CAPEX | Pay-per-use, spot instances, multi-cloud |
| **Reliability Optimization** | Maximize availability, fault tolerance | Redundancy, replication, self-healing |
| **Network Optimization** | Minimize bandwidth use, congestion | Compression, CDN, protocol tuning |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Service Life Cycle — Stage-wise Theoretical Breakdown

### Stage 1: Service Design & Composition
At this stage, the application's microservices, data flows, and dependencies are defined. Optimization here is **proactive** — building efficiency *into* the service.

- **Microservice Decomposition:** Breaking monolithic IoT applications into smaller, independently deployable services.
- **Service Composition Patterns:**
  - *Sequential* (A → B → C) — high dependency, low parallelism
  - *Parallel* (A, B, C run concurrently) — higher throughput, higher resource demand
  - *Hybrid* — combines both for optimal trade-offs
- **Latency-Aware Placement Planning:** Pre-computing the ideal fog node for each microservice based on predicted load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why it matters:** Poor design propagates inefficiencies to every later phase. KTU questions often ask: *"At which life cycle stage is the cost of fixing a design flaw highest?"* — Answer: The **execution phase** (rework cost is exponential).

### Stage 2: Resource Provisioning & Deployment
This stage involves allocating physical/virtual resources and deploying the designed services.

- **Static Provisioning:** Fixed resources allocated at boot time — simple but wasteful.
- **Dynamic Provisioning:** Resources scale with demand — complex but efficient.
- **Predictive Provisioning:** ML-based forecasting of demand before it occurs.
- **Container vs. VM Trade-off:**
  - Containers: lightweight (~MBs), fast startup (~ms), share OS kernel
  - VMs: heavier (~GBs), slower startup (~seconds), full isolation
- **Edge Node Selection Criteria:** Proximity to data source, available compute, network hop count, energy profile.

### Stage 3: Service Execution & Operation
The runtime phase where actual user requests are processed.

- **Task Offloading Decision:** Should the task run on the device, the edge, or the cloud?
- **Computation Partitioning:** Splitting code between local and remote execution.
- **Caching Strategies:** LRU, LFU, predictive caching at fog nodes.
- **Load Balancing:** Distributing requests across multiple edge nodes.
- **Real-time Adaptation:** Adjusting resource allocation based on live metrics.

### Stage 4: Monitoring & Adaptation
Continuous observation and feedback-driven optimization.

- **Telemetry Collection:** CPU, memory, network, energy metrics.
- **Anomaly Detection:** Identifying deviations from expected behavior.
- **Self-Optimization Loops:** MAPE-K (Monitor-Analyze-Plan-Execute over Knowledge) is the standard reference architecture used by KTU.
- **Reinforcement Learning (RL):** Agents learn optimal policies through trial and error.

### Stage 5: Decommissioning / Migration
End-of-life handling — often overlooked but critical.

- **Live Migration:** Moving running services with minimal downtime.
- **Cold Migration:** Service is stopped, moved, restarted.
- **Resource Reclamation:** Returning leased cloud/fog resources to avoid billing.
- **Data Archival & Compliance:** GDPR, HIPAA-compliant data handling.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** and aligned with KTU 2024 Module 3 expected answers. Memorize this table — it appears in nearly every ESE and internal assessment.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_{total} = L_{compute} + L_{network} + L_{queue}$ | End-to-end latency decomposition | ms | Performance optimization |
| 2 | $E = P_{compute} \cdot t + P_{network} \cdot t$ | Total energy = power × time | Joules (J) | Energy optimization |
| 3 | $T = \frac{N_{tasks}}{t_{total}}$ | Throughput definition | tasks/s | Capacity planning |
| 4 | $U_{cpu} = \frac{t_{active}}{t_{total}} \times 100\%$ | CPU utilization | % | Resource optimization |
| 5 | $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR} \times 100\%$ | Availability formula | % | Reliability optimization |
| 6 | $C_{total} = C_{compute} + C_{storage} + C_{network}$ | Total cost of ownership | USD | Cost optimization |
| 7 | $B = \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon-Hartley channel capacity | bits/s | Network optimization |
| 8 | $QoS = w_1 \cdot \frac{1}{L} + w_2 \cdot T + w_3 \cdot A$ | Weighted QoS composite score | dimensionless | SLA compliance |
| 9 | $SLA_{violation} = \frac{N_{missed}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | SLA violation rate | % | Contract optimization |
| 10 | $PUE = \frac{P_{total}}{P_{IT}}$ | Power Usage Effectiveness | dimensionless | Data center efficiency |
| 11 | $R = \frac{\alpha \cdot T}{1 - U}$ | M/M/1 queue response time | s | Queueing theory in fog |
| 12 | $J = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(L_i - \bar{L})^2}$ | Latency jitter (standard deviation) | ms | Real-time systems |
| 13 | $E_{offload} = E_{tx} \cdot \frac{D}{R} + E_{compute}^{edge}$ | Offloading energy cost | J | Mobile edge computing |
| 14 | $Gain = \frac{L_{local} - L_{edge}}{L_{local}} \times 100\%$ | Latency improvement % | % | Offloading decision |

**Where:**
- $MTBF$ = Mean Time Between Failures
- $MTTR$ = Mean Time To Repair
- $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 = 1$ (weighting constraints for QoS)
- $\alpha$ = average arrival rate, $U$ = utilization (queueing theory)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Cities:** Optimization at the deployment stage reduces latency for traffic-light coordination from cloud-roundtrip (~100 ms) to local-edge response (~5 ms).
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Energy optimization in factory floors can reduce electricity costs by **20–40%** through dynamic voltage/frequency scaling.
- **Telemedicine:** Reliability optimization ensures 99.999% availability for remote surgery where every millisecond of latency is life-critical.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Network optimization via 5G/V2X edge offloading reduces in-car compute load by up to **70%**.
- **AR/VR Applications:** Performance optimization at the execution stage maintains the **<20 ms** motion-to-photon latency required to prevent VR sickness.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: End-to-End Latency Decomposition for Offloading Decision

> [!NOTE]
> **Context:** A KTU ESE question typically presents: *"An IoT device has 500 KB of data to process. The local execution takes 2 seconds at 1 GHz. Offloading to an edge node 50 km away at 100 Mbps takes 0.4 s compute + network time. Should the task be offloaded?"*

We must derive the offloading gain analytically.

### Step 1: Define Local Execution Latency

The local execution latency on the device is given by:

$$L_{local} = \frac{C}{f_{local}}$$

Where:
- $C$ = total computational cycles required (in cycles)
- $f_{local}$ = local CPU clock frequency (in Hz)

For a workload of $C = 2 \times 10^9$ cycles and $f_{local} = 1 \times 10^9$ Hz:

$$L_{local} = \frac{2 \times 10^9}{1 \times 10^9} = 2 \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 2: Define Total Offloading Latency

The total latency when offloading includes three components:

$$L_{offload} = L_{tx} + L_{edge\_compute} + L_{rx}$$

Where:
- $L_{tx}$ = transmission latency (uplink)
- $L_{edge\_compute}$ = computation latency at the edge node
- $L_{rx}$ = reception latency (downlink of result)

### Step 3: Compute Transmission Latency

The transmission latency for an uplink payload of size $D$ bytes over a link of bandwidth $R$ bps is:

$$L_{tx} = \frac{D \times 8}{R}$$

For $D = 500 \text{ KB} = 500 \times 1024 \times 8 = 4.096 \times 10^6$ bits and $R = 100 \text{ Mbps} = 10^8$ bps:

$$L_{tx} = \frac{4.096 \times 10^6}{1 \times 10^8} = 0.04096 \text{ s} \approx 40.96 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 4: Include Propagation Latency (often overlooked by students)

$$L_{prop} = \frac{d}{v_{signal}}$$

For $d = 50 \text{ km} = 50,000$ m and $v_{signal} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s (fiber-optic typical):

$$L_{prop} = \frac{50,000}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.25 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 5: Sum All Offloading Latency Components

$$L_{offload} = 0.04096 + 0.00025 + L_{edge\_compute} + L_{rx}$$

Assuming edge compute $= 0.4$ s and downlink of a small result $\approx 1$ ms $= 0.001$ s:

$$L_{offload} = 0.04096 + 0.00025 + 0.4 + 0.001 = 0.44221 \text{ s}$$

### Step 6: Compute the Offloading Gain

$$Gain = \frac{L_{local} - L_{offload}}{L_{local}} \times 100\%$$

Substituting the values:

$$Gain = \frac{2.0 - 0.44221}{2.0} \times 100\%$$

$$Gain = \frac{1.55779}{2.0} \times 100\%$$

$$Gain = 0.778895 \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{Gain \approx 77.89\%}$$

### Step 7: Conclusion of the Decision

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (KTU Board Exam Standard):**
> - If $Gain > 0$ AND $E_{offload} < E_{local}$ → **Offload**
> - If $Gain < 0$ OR energy cost is prohibitive → **Execute locally**
> - Always check against the **SLA latency threshold** before finalizing.

**Verdict:** Since the gain is **77.89% > 0**, offloading is **strongly recommended**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy-Efficient Task Offloading (MEC Standard Problem)

### Step 1: Define Local Execution Energy

$$E_{local} = P_{local} \cdot L_{local} = P_{local} \cdot \frac{C}{f_{local}}$$

For a typical IoT device, $P_{local} = 1$ W:

$$E_{local} = 1 \text{ W} \times 2 \text{ s} = 2 \text{ J}$$

### Step 2: Define Transmission Energy (Offloading)

$$E_{tx} = P_{tx} \cdot L_{tx}$$

Where $P_{tx}$ is the transmit power. For a typical Wi-Fi module, $P_{tx} = 0.5$ W:

$$E_{tx} = 0.5 \text{ W} \times 0.04096 \text{ s} = 0.02048 \text{ J}$$

### Step 3: Define Edge Compute Energy (borne by edge, not device)

$$E_{edge} = P_{edge} \cdot L_{edge\_compute}$$

This is not borne by the IoT device, so for the **device's perspective**:

$$E_{device} = E_{tx} + E_{idle} \cdot L_{wait}$$

Assuming the device idles at $P_{idle} = 0.1$ W during the offload wait ($L_{offload} \approx 0.442$ s):

$$E_{device} = 0.02048 + 0.1 \times 0.442 = 0.02048 + 0.0442 = 0.06468 \text{ J}$$

### Step 4: Compute Energy Savings

$$\Delta E = E_{local} - E_{device} = 2.0 - 0.06468 = 1.93532 \text{ J}$$

$$\text{Energy Savings \%} = \frac{1.93532}{2.0} \times 100\% = 96.77\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Energy savings often exceed latency savings in IoT scenarios because the device is **idle** during offloading, consuming only a fraction of active processing power.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Optimization Opportunities per Life Cycle Stage

This is the **master reference table** for KTU Module 3. Memorize the cell mappings.

| Life Cycle Stage | Performance Opt. | Resource Opt. | Energy Opt. | Cost Opt. | Reliability Opt. | Network Opt. |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Design & Composition** | Latency-aware microservice split | Right-sizing of containers | Carbon-aware architecture | TCO modeling | Failure mode analysis | Protocol selection (MQTT/CoAP) |
| **Provisioning & Deployment** | Proximity-based placement | Bin-packing scheduler | Renewable-aware placement | Spot-instance bidding | Geo-redundancy setup | Bandwidth reservation |
| **Execution & Operation** | Task offloading, caching | Dynamic VM consolidation | DVFS, sleep modes | Auto-scaling rules | Self-healing routines | Compression, traffic shaping |
| **Monitoring & Adaptation** | Real-time latency tracking | Heat-map dashboards | Power monitoring | Cost dashboards | Anomaly detection | QoS-aware routing |
| **Decommissioning** | Graceful shutdown | Resource reclamation | Sleep state on exit | Final billing closure | Data backup | Connection draining |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When a question asks *"Give two examples of optimization at the deployment stage,"* always cite one **performance** example and one **energy** example — this shows the examiner you understand cross-cutting optimization.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Service Life Cycle Optimization Loop (MAPE-K Reference Model)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Service Design Phase] --> B[Resource Provisioning]
    B --> C[Service Deployment]
    C --> D[Service Execution at Edge/Fog]
    D --> E[Continuous Monitoring]
    E --> F{Anomaly Detected?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Analysis and Planning]
    G --> H[Adaptation and Reconfiguration]
    H --> D
    F -- No --> D
    D --> I[Service Decommissioning or Migration]
    I --> J[Resource Reclamation]
    J --> K[End of Life Cycle]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The central loop (D → E → F → G → H → D) represents the **MAPE-K self-optimization cycle** referenced in KTU 2024 Module 3. The Knowledge base (not shown explicitly) feeds all four MAPE phases.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Optimization Decision Tree for Task Offloading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New Task Arrives at IoT Device] --> q1{Is Network Available?}
    q1 -- No --> local[Execute Locally]
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{Is Latency SLA < 10ms?}
    q2 -- Yes --> edge[Offload to Nearest Fog Node]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Is Energy Critical?}
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Is Link Bandwidth > 10Mbps?}
    q4 -- Yes --> edge
    q4 -- No --> local
    q3 -- No --> cloud[Offload to Cloud]
    local --> done[Task Complete]
    edge --> done
    cloud --> done
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Optimization Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph appLayer[Application Layer]
        a1[IoT Apps]
        a2[AR VR Apps]
        a3[Analytics Apps]
    end
    subgraph optLayer[Optimization Layer - Cross Cutting]
        o1[Performance Optimizer]
        o2[Energy Optimizer]
        o3[Cost Optimizer]
        o4[Reliability Optimizer]
    end
    subgraph fogLayer[Fog Computing Layer]
        f1[Fog Node 1]
        f2[Fog Node 2]
        f3[Fog Node 3]
    end
    subgraph cloudLayer[Cloud Backend]
        c1[Long Term Storage]
        c2[Heavy Analytics]
    end
    appLayer --> optLayer
    optLayer --> fogLayer
    fogLayer --> cloudLayer
    optLayer -. Feedback Loops .-> optLayer
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Cost vs. Latency Trade-off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Cost High Latency Cloud Only] -->|Shift Workload| B[Medium Cost Medium Latency Fog + Cloud]
    B -->|Shift Workload| C[High Cost Low Latency Edge Only]
    C -->|Further Optimization| D[Optimized Edge with Caching]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The curve illustrates that **pure edge computing** is expensive but fast, while **pure cloud** is cheap but slow. The **optimal point** lies in the middle, often using **fog computing** as the balancing layer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the term "Service Life Cycle" in the context of Fog and Edge Computing. List its main phases. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The Service Life Cycle refers to the end-to-end sequence of stages through which a fog/edge service progresses — from initial design to final decommissioning. Its main phases are:
1. **Service Design & Composition**
2. **Resource Provisioning & Deployment**
3. **Service Execution & Operation**
4. **Monitoring & Adaptation**
5. **Decommissioning / Migration**

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award full 3 marks only if **all 5 phases** are listed with a one-line description. A bare list without the definition scores only 1 mark.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between *static provisioning* and *dynamic provisioning* in edge computing. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Static Provisioning | Dynamic Provisioning |
|---|---|---|
| Resource Allocation | Fixed at deployment time | Adjusted at runtime based on demand |
| Complexity | Low | High (requires monitoring & scaling logic) |
| Resource Utilization | Often poor (over/under-provisioning) | High (matches demand) |
| Cost Efficiency | Low | High |
| Use Case | Predictable, stable workloads | Bursty, variable workloads (e.g., IoT, video streaming) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Use a comparison table — examiners love it. Mention at least **2 valid differences** for full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either the full OR option A OR option B**, not parts from both.

---

### Question A: Optimization Opportunities — Full Length 14-Mark Question

#### Part (a): Explain the MAPE-K autonomic control loop and discuss how it enables continuous optimization across the fog/edge service life cycle. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **MAPE-K loop** is the de-facto standard reference model for **self-managing, self-optimizing systems**, originally proposed by IBM and now universally referenced in fog/edge computing literature (KTU 2024 Module 3).

**The four phases are:**

1. **Monitor (M):** Collects telemetry data from fog nodes — CPU utilization, memory, network latency, energy consumption, queue lengths, and error rates. Tools: Prometheus, Telegraf, custom SNMP probes.

2. **Analyze (A):** Processes the collected metrics to detect anomalies, predict trends, and identify optimization opportunities. Techniques: statistical analysis, ML-based anomaly detection (e.g., Isolation Forest, LSTM forecasters).

3. **Plan (P):** Decides the corrective action — should a service be migrated? Should resources be scaled? Should a node be put to sleep? Generates a reconfiguration plan.

4. **Execute (E):** Applies the plan to the live system — triggers container migration, VM resizing, or routing table updates.

5. **Knowledge (K):** A shared knowledge base (often a distributed key-value store) that all four phases read from and write to. Stores historical data, policies, SLAs, and topology information.

**Mapping MAPE-K to the Service Life Cycle:**

| MAPE-K Phase | Life Cycle Stage Mapped | Optimization Opportunity |
|---|---|---|
| Monitor | Execution & Monitoring | Real-time QoS tracking |
| Analyze | Monitoring & Adaptation | Anomaly detection, predictive scaling |
| Plan | Provisioning & Adaptation | Resource reallocation strategy |
| Execute | Execution & Deployment | Live migration, auto-scaling |
| Knowledge | All stages (shared) | Persistent learning, policy enforcement |

**Conclusion:** The MAPE-K loop enables **closed-loop, autonomic optimization**, allowing fog/edge systems to self-tune without human intervention — a critical requirement for large-scale, geographically distributed edge deployments.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse **MAPE** with the older **OODA loop** or skip the **Knowledge (K)** component. Always include K — it's a 1-mark differentiator.

---

#### Part (b): A wearable health-monitoring device generates 2 MB of data per minute. The local processing consumes 0.8 W for 5 seconds. Offloading to a nearby fog node via 4G (10 Mbps) takes 1.6 s for compute at the fog. Calculate: (i) Local execution energy, (ii) Transmission time, (iii) Whether offloading is beneficial from a latency perspective. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $D = 2 \text{ MB} = 2 \times 1024 \times 1024 \times 8 = 1.6777 \times 10^7$ bits
- $P_{local} = 0.8$ W
- $L_{local} = 5$ s
- $L_{edge\_compute} = 1.6$ s
- $R = 10 \text{ Mbps} = 10^7$ bps
- Assume propagation + downlink negligible for this problem

**Step 1: Local Execution Energy**

$$E_{local} = P_{local} \times L_{local} = 0.8 \times 5 = 4.0 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2: Transmission Time**

$$L_{tx} = \frac{D}{R} = \frac{1.6777 \times 10^7}{1 \times 10^7} = 1.6777 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Total Offloading Latency**

$$L_{offload} = L_{tx} + L_{edge\_compute} = 1.6777 + 1.6 = 3.2777 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4: Latency Comparison**

$$L_{local} = 5 \text{ s} \quad \text{vs.} \quad L_{offload} = 3.2777 \text{ s}$$

Since $L_{offload} < L_{local}$:

$$Gain = \frac{5 - 3.2777}{5} \times 100\% = \frac{1.7223}{5} \times 100\% = 34.45\%$$

**Step 5: Verdict**

> Offloading is **beneficial**, reducing latency by **~34.45%**. The decision is recommended.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - Many students forget to **convert MB to bits** (multiply by $1024 \times 1024 \times 8$). This error alone costs **2 marks**.
> - Some confuse **transmission time** with **propagation time** — they are different. Transmission = payload / bandwidth; propagation = distance / signal speed.
> - Always state the **final verdict** explicitly ("offloading is beneficial") — without it, you lose the concluding 1 mark.

---

### Question B: Alternative 14-Mark Question (Internal Choice)

#### Part (a): Discuss in detail the various energy optimization opportunities available at different stages of the fog/edge service life cycle. Give a numerical example of energy savings through DVFS. **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Energy Optimization Across the Life Cycle:**

1. **Design Stage:**
   - Energy-aware algorithm design (e.g., approximate computing)
   - Selecting ARM/RISC-V processors over x86 for low power
   - Energy-aware data compression before transmission

2. **Provisioning Stage:**
   - Place latency-tolerant workloads on **renewable-powered** edge nodes
   - Consolidate VMs to fewer servers (reduces idle power waste)
   - Use **spot/preemptible instances** in cloud offloading

3. **Execution Stage:**
   - **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS):** Scale CPU voltage/frequency based on load
   - **Sleep states:** Put idle nodes into S3/S4 states
   - **Container consolidation:** Co-locate compatible workloads
   - **Workload scheduling:** Run heavy tasks during off-peak energy hours

4. **Monitoring Stage:**
   - Track **Power Usage Effectiveness (PUE)** continuously
   - Use AI to predict energy demand and pre-cool/pre-warm data halls

5. **Decommissioning Stage:**
   - Graceful shutdown of unused services
   - Hardware recycling and proper e-waste handling

**Numerical Example — DVFS:**

A fog node CPU has two operating points:
- High: $V = 1.2$ V, $f = 2$ GHz, $P = 10$ W
- Low: $V = 0.8$ V, $f = 1$ GHz, $P = 3$ W

A task requires $C = 10^{10}$ cycles to complete.

**Time at High Performance:**

$$t_{high} = \frac{C}{f_{high}} = \frac{10^{10}}{2 \times 10^9} = 5 \text{ s}$$

**Energy at High Performance:**

$$E_{high} = P_{high} \times t_{high} = 10 \times 5 = 50 \text{ J}$$

**Time at Low Performance:**

$$t_{low} = \frac{C}{f_{low}} = \frac{10^{10}}{1 \times 10^9} = 10 \text{ s}$$

**Energy at Low Performance:**

$$E_{low} = P_{low} \times t_{low} = 3 \times 10 = 30 \text{ J}$$

**Energy Savings:**

$$\Delta E = 50 - 30 = 20 \text{ J} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{20}{50} \times 100\% = 40\% \text{ savings}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Don't claim energy savings without showing the **time penalty**. State explicitly: *"DVFS saves 40% energy but doubles execution time from 5 s to 10 s."*

---

#### Part (b): Compare the optimization opportunities at the **Design Stage** versus the **Execution Stage** of the service life cycle. Which stage offers the highest leverage for optimization, and why? **[7 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Optimization Aspect | Design Stage | Execution Stage |
|---|---|---|
| **Timing** | Before deployment (proactive) | After deployment (reactive) |
| **Cost of Change** | Low (only redesign effort) | High (requires live reconfiguration) |
| **Tools Used** | UML, simulation, capacity planning | MAPE-K, autoscalers, RL agents |
| **Optimization Type** | Architectural, structural | Operational, behavioral |
| **Example** | Microservice decomposition | Live VM migration |
| **Risk** | Low (no live users affected) | Medium-high (SLA violation possible) |
| **Reversibility** | Easy (redeploy) | Hard (rollback may be complex) |
| **Impact Duration** | Long-term (entire service life) | Short-term (one operational cycle) |

**Highest Leverage Argument:**

> The **Design Stage** offers the **highest leverage** for optimization. The principle follows the **"Rule of 10"** in software engineering: a defect that costs **1 unit to fix at design** costs **10 units at deployment**, **100 units at execution**, and **1000+ units in production**.

**Reasoning:**
1. Architectural decisions (e.g., monolithic vs. microservice) **lock in** performance characteristics for the service's entire life.
2. Poor design choices cannot be fully compensated by runtime optimization (you can scale up a poorly designed service, but not redesign it on the fly).
3. Energy-efficient algorithms chosen at design time yield compounding savings over years of operation.
4. However, the **Execution Stage** is where **most real-world optimization actually happens** because design-stage decisions are often constrained by business requirements, legacy systems, or skill gaps.

**Conclusion:** Design-stage optimization has higher **leverage**; execution-stage optimization has higher **frequency and impact-on-the-day**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often argue one stage is "better" without acknowledging both have roles. The correct answer is **"Design for high leverage, execute for high frequency."**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, Topic: Optimization Opportunities along the Service Life Cycle**

- [ ] **Life Cycle has 5 stages:** Design → Provisioning → Execution → Monitoring → Decommissioning.
- [ ] **MAPE-K** = Monitor, Analyze, Plan, Execute + Knowledge base. Used for self-optimization.
- [ ] **Offloading gain formula:** $Gain = \frac{L_{local} - L_{offload}}{L_{local}} \times 100\%$
- [ ] **Offloading latency** = $L_{tx} + L_{prop} + L_{compute} + L_{rx}$
- [ ] **Energy formula:** $E = P \times t$ (Watts × seconds = Joules)
- [ ] **DVFS trade-off:** Lower voltage → less energy, more execution time.
- [ ] **Static vs. Dynamic provisioning:** Static = simple, wasteful; Dynamic = complex, efficient.
- [ ] **Container vs. VM:** Containers = MB-sized, ms startup; VMs = GB-sized, second startup.
- [ ] **QoS composite formula:** Weighted sum of $\frac{1}{L}$, $T$, $A$.
- [ ] **Availability:** $A = \frac{MTBF}{MTBF + MTTR}$
- [ ] **Design stage has the highest optimization leverage**; execution stage has highest frequency.
- [ ] **Energy savings from offloading** often exceed latency savings in IoT.
- [ ] **Common mistakes to avoid:**
  - Forgetting to convert MB → bits in transmission time problems.
  - Confusing transmission and propagation latency.
  - Listing MAPE phases without the **K** (Knowledge).
  - Skipping the conclusion/verdict in numerical problems.
  - Confusing fog (intermediate layer) with edge (device-proximate).

- [ ] **Examiner's favorite question patterns:**
  - "List and explain the 5 stages of the service life cycle." *(3–7 marks)*
  - "Calculate offloading gain / energy savings." *(7 marks)*
  - "Explain MAPE-K with a diagram." *(7 marks)*
  - "Compare static vs. dynamic provisioning / Design vs. Execution optimization." *(7 marks)*

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Advice:** If you remember only **3 things** for the exam, remember these:
> 1. **5 stages** of the life cycle (in order).
> 2. **MAPE-K** loop with all 5 components.
> 3. **Offloading Gain formula** + energy formula $E = P \times t$.

---

**End of Notes — Optimization Opportunities along the Service Life Cycle**
*Module 3, PECIT867 - Fog and Edge Computing | KTU 2024 Scheme*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Toward a Taxonomy of Optimization Problems in Fog Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Toward a Taxonomy of Optimization Problems in Fog Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optimization Problem (KTU Formal Definition):**
> An **optimization problem** in fog computing is a mathematical decision-making problem that seeks to find the *best* solution (minimizing or maximizing one or more objective functions) from a set of feasible alternatives, subject to the operational, resource, and Quality-of-Service (QoS) constraints inherent to the fog–cloud–IoT continuum.

A **taxonomy of optimization problems** is a hierarchical, multi-dimensional classification scheme that organizes these problems based on shared structural properties such as the *nature of the decision variables*, *the type of objective function*, *the nature of constraints*, the *time-horizon of the decision*, and the *availability of information*. In fog computing, such a taxonomy is essential because the fog layer is **heterogeneous**, **distributed**, **resource-constrained**, and **latency-sensitive**, leading to an enormous variety of decision problems that cannot be solved by a single universal method.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart City Traffic Control Room"

Imagine a **Smart City Traffic Control Room** managing thousands of vehicles (IoT devices), hundreds of intersections (fog nodes), and a few regional command centers (cloud data centers). Every few seconds, the controller must decide:

- Which intersection should handle which vehicle's request?
- How to balance load between nearby intersections?
- Which requests should be forwarded to the central command center?
- How to minimize average waiting time *and* fuel consumption *and* pollution?

Each of these "decisions" is an **optimization problem**. The traffic room cannot use one single rule for all of them — it needs a **classification system (taxonomy)** to choose the right strategy for the right type of decision. This is precisely what a *taxonomy of optimization problems* provides for fog computing.

### 1.3 Why a Taxonomy is Needed in Fog Computing

Fog computing environments are fundamentally different from traditional cloud or grid computing:

| Fog Characteristic | Optimization Implication |
|---|---|
| **Heterogeneous devices** (sensors, gateways, micro-datacenters) | Decision variables are mixed (integer + continuous) |
| **Hierarchical architecture** (Device → Fog → Cloud) | Multi-layer coupled problems |
| **Mobility** of end-devices | Dynamic / time-varying constraints |
| **Latency-critical** applications | Objective dominated by response time |
| **Energy-constrained** edge nodes | Energy is a first-class objective |
| **Stochastic workloads** | Need for stochastic / robust formulations |
| **Geographical distribution** | Network-aware constraints |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Without a taxonomy, researchers and engineers would repeatedly reinvent solution methods. A well-defined taxonomy allows *re-use of algorithms*, *bench-marking*, and *systematic comparison* of approaches — a key requirement in any engineering discipline.

### 1.4 Core Nomenclature

- **Decision Variable** — A parameter whose value the optimizer is allowed to choose (e.g., which fog node hosts a microservice).
- **Objective Function** — The mathematical expression to be minimized or maximized (e.g., total latency, total energy).
- **Feasible Region** — The set of all decision vectors satisfying the constraints.
- **Optimal Solution** — A feasible solution that is at least as good as every other feasible solution w.r.t. the chosen objective.
- **Pareto-Optimal Solution** — A solution where no other feasible solution improves one objective without worsening another (used in multi-objective optimization).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of a Fog Optimization Problem

A canonical fog optimization problem can be decomposed into **five logical components**:

1. **Decision Variables (X)** — Discrete, continuous, or mixed.
   - Example: $x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}$ indicating whether task $i$ is placed on fog node $j$.

2. **Parameters (P)** — Known constants describing the system state.
   - Example: arrival rate $\lambda_i$, processing capacity $C_j$, link bandwidth $B_{jk}$.

3. **Objective Function (f(X, P))** — A scalar or vector function to optimize.
   - Example: $f = \sum_{i} T_i$ (sum of task completion times).

4. **Constraints (g(X, P))** — Inequalities/equalities restricting the feasible region.
   - Example: $\sum_i x_{ij} \cdot d_i \le C_j$ (capacity constraint).

5. **Solution Method (M)** — Algorithm used (linear programming, heuristic, meta-heuristic, etc.).

### 2.2 Primary Classification Axes of the Taxonomy

The taxonomy is organized along **five orthogonal axes**, each capturing a distinct dimension of variation:

#### Axis A — By the **Decision Variable Type**
- **Continuous** optimization (e.g., transmission power allocation)
- **Discrete / Combinatorial** optimization (e.g., VM placement)
- **Mixed-Integer** optimization (most realistic fog problems)
- **Binary** optimization (e.g., service placement → node selection)

#### Axis B — By the **Number and Nature of Objectives**
- **Single-Objective** (SOO): $ \min f(\mathbf{x}) $
- **Multi-Objective** (MOO): $ \min \{f_1(\mathbf{x}), f_2(\mathbf{x}), \ldots, f_k(\mathbf{x})\} $ with $k \ge 2$
- Many-objective (MaOO): $k \ge 4$

#### Axis C — By the **Time Horizon / Dynamics**
- **Static / Offline** — All inputs known in advance.
- **Dynamic / Online** — Decisions made as information arrives.
- **Stochastic** — Inputs are random variables with known distributions.
- **Robust** — Inputs are uncertain within bounded sets (worst-case).

#### Axis D — By the **Solver Paradigm**
- **Exact methods** (Branch-and-Bound, Dynamic Programming, MILP solvers)
- **Heuristic methods** (Greedy, Round-Robin, First-Fit)
- **Meta-heuristic methods** (GA, PSO, ACO, Simulated Annealing, Tabu Search)
- **Learning-based methods** (Reinforcement Learning, Deep RL, Federated Learning)
- **Hybrid methods** (combinations of the above)

#### Axis E — By the **Fog Layer Concern**
- **Application/Service Placement**
- **Computation Offloading**
- **Resource Allocation (compute, storage, network)**
- **Task Scheduling**
- **Load Balancing & Migration**
- **Energy Management**
- **Network & Communication Optimization**
- **Security & Trust Optimization**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these canonical formulations — they appear repeatedly in KTU examinations.**

| # | Concept | Canonical Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General SOO Form | $\min\limits_{\mathbf{x}\in\mathcal{F}} f(\mathbf{x})$ | Single objective, feasible set $\mathcal{F}$ |
| 2 | Constrained SOO | $\min f(\mathbf{x})$ s.t. $g_i(\mathbf{x}) \le 0,\;h_j(\mathbf{x})=0$ | Ineq. and eq. constraints |
| 3 | MOO Vector Form | $\min \mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = [f_1, f_2, \ldots, f_k]^{T}$ | Multi-objective vector |
| 4 | Weighted-Sum Scalarization | $f_{ws} = \sum_{m=1}^{k} w_m f_m(\mathbf{x}),\; \sum w_m=1$ | Converts MOO → SOO |
| 5 | Binary Placement | $x_{ij}\in\{0,1\}\;\forall i,j$ | 1 if service $i$ on node $j$ |
| 6 | Capacity Constraint | $\sum_{i=1}^{I} x_{ij}\,d_i \le C_j\;\forall j$ | Resource limit at fog node $j$ |
| 7 | Latency Objective | $T_{ij} = T^{proc}_{ij} + T^{trans}_{ij} + T^{queue}_{ij}$ | Total per-task latency |
| 8 | Energy Model | $E = \sum_j \bigl(P^{idle}_j + P^{comp}_j\bigr)\,\Delta t$ | Energy over interval $\Delta t$ |
| 9 | Pareto Dominance | $\mathbf{x}^\* \prec \mathbf{x} \iff \forall m: f_m(\mathbf{x}^\*)\le f_m(\mathbf{x}) \;\wedge\; \exists m: f_m(\mathbf{x}^\*)< f_m(\mathbf{x})$ | Defines Pareto front |
| 10 | Lagrangian Relaxation | $\mathcal{L} = f(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_i \mu_i g_i(\mathbf{x}) + \sum_j \lambda_j h_j(\mathbf{x})$ | Used in dual decomposition |
| 11 | Convergence Rate (PSO) | $\mathbf{v}_i^{t+1} = w\mathbf{v}_i^{t} + c_1 r_1(\mathbf{p}_i - \mathbf{x}_i) + c_2 r_2(\mathbf{g} - \mathbf{x}_i)$ | Meta-heuristic update |
| 12 | Markov Decision (DP) | $V(s) = \max_a \bigl[R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s'\vert s,a)\,V(s')\bigr]$ | Bellman optimality |
| 13 | Makespan | $C_{max} = \max_j \sum_i x_{ij}\,p_{ij}$ | Load balancing metric |
| 14 | Service Placement Cardinality | $\vert\mathcal{S}\vert = \sum_{i,j} x_{ij}$ | Number of active placements |

> **Standard Units (use SI):** Latency $T$ in **seconds (s)** or **milliseconds (ms)**; Energy $E$ in **Joules (J)** or **kWh**; Bandwidth $B$ in **bits per second (bps)**; Compute capacity $C$ in **MIPS / vCPU / GHz**; Storage in **bytes**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The taxonomy is not academic ornamentation — it directly enables:

- **Algorithm selection**: Knowing that a problem is a *binary, single-objective, static* problem allows immediate use of MILP or branch-and-bound.
- **Complexity reasoning**: A continuous single-objective problem is tractable; a multi-objective stochastic binary problem is NP-hard.
- **Benchmark design**: A common taxonomy enables reproducible comparison across research papers.
- **System design**: A network architect can pick the right optimization layer (e.g., NSO for SDN, K8s scheduler for k8s clusters) based on taxonomy classification.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Generic Form of a Fog Optimization Problem

We start from first principles. Consider a fog infrastructure with:

- $I$ — total number of incoming tasks / service instances.
- $J$ — total number of fog nodes.
- $K$ — total number of cloud data centers.

Let the **decision matrix** $\mathbf{X} = [x_{ij}]$ be defined as:

$$
x_{ij} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if task (or service) } i \text{ is assigned to fog node } j \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

We also define a *cloud offload* indicator $y_i \in \{0,1\}$ equal to 1 iff task $i$ is offloaded to a remote cloud. The fundamental constraints are:

**C1 — Assignment constraint** (each task assigned to exactly one location):

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{J} x_{ij} + y_i = 1 \quad \forall i \in \{1,\ldots,I\}
$$

**C2 — Capacity constraint** at fog node $j$ (no over-subscription of compute):

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{I} x_{ij}\,d_i \le C_j \quad \forall j \in \{1,\ldots,J\}
$$

where $d_i$ is the computational demand of task $i$ and $C_j$ is the capacity of node $j$.

**C3 — Latency constraint** for latency-sensitive task $i$:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{J} x_{ij}\,L_{ij} + y_i\,L^{cloud}_i \le L^{max}_i \quad \forall i
$$

**C4 — Energy budget** of node $j$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{I} x_{ij}\,e_{ij} \le E^{max}_j \quad \forall j
$$

The **canonical objective** (latency minimization) becomes:

$$
f(\mathbf{X}, \mathbf{y}) = \sum_{i=1}^{I}\left[\sum_{j=1}^{J} x_{ij}\,L_{ij} + y_i\,L^{cloud}_i\right]
$$

Putting it all together, the **complete optimization problem** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\min_{\mathbf{X},\mathbf{y}} \quad & \sum_{i=1}^{I}\left[\sum_{j=1}^{J} x_{ij}\,L_{ij} + y_i\,L^{cloud}_i\right] \\
\text{s.t.} \quad & \sum_{j=1}^{J} x_{ij} + y_i = 1, \quad \forall i \\
& \sum_{i=1}^{I} x_{ij}\,d_i \le C_j, \quad \forall j \\
& \sum_{i=1}^{I} x_{ij}\,e_{ij} \le E^{max}_j, \quad \forall j \\
& \sum_{j=1}^{J} x_{ij}\,L_{ij} + y_i\,L^{cloud}_i \le L^{max}_i, \quad \forall i \\
& x_{ij} \in \{0,1\},\; y_i \in \{0,1\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic Step 1**: We start from a business goal ("process all tasks with minimum average latency"). **Step 2**: We formalize latency as a function of assignment. **Step 3**: We translate physical/operational rules (one task → one node, no over-subscription, no SLA violation) into mathematical constraints. **Step 4**: We declare the decision variables and their domains. The result is a **Binary Integer Linear Program (BILP)**, a member of the NP-hard class.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multi-Objective Conversion via Weighted Sum

When latency *and* energy *and* cost must be jointly optimized, we have:

$$
\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{bmatrix} f_1(\mathbf{x}) \\ f_2(\mathbf{x}) \\ f_3(\mathbf{x}) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Total Latency} \\ \text{Total Energy} \\ \text{Total Cost} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The **weighted-sum scalarization** is derived as follows. Let weights $w_1, w_2, w_3 \ge 0$ with $\sum_m w_m = 1$. The scalar surrogate is:

$$
f_{ws}(\mathbf{x}) = w_1 f_1(\mathbf{x}) + w_2 f_2(\mathbf{x}) + w_3 f_3(\mathbf{x})
$$

**Step 1** Normalize each $f_m$ to a common range $[0,1]$ by dividing by a reference value $f_m^{ref}$ to prevent scale dominance. **Step 2** Choose weights reflecting user priorities (e.g., $w_1 = 0.5, w_2 = 0.3, w_3 = 0.2$). **Step 3** Solve the resulting SOO via MILP solver. **Step 4** Vary the weights to trace the Pareto front.

The **limitation**: weighted-sum cannot generate non-convex regions of the Pareto front — for those, use $\varepsilon$-constraint method or NSGA-II.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Fog Problem Classes to Taxonomic Axes

| Fog Problem | Decision Var | # Objectives | Time Horizon | Solver Family | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Service Placement** | Binary | 1–3 | Static/Dynamic | MILP / Heuristic | NP-hard |
| **Computation Offloading** | Binary + Continuous | 1–2 | Dynamic | DP / RL | NP-hard |
| **Task Scheduling** | Discrete | 1–2 | Dynamic | Heuristic / GA | NP-hard |
| **VM Consolidation** | Integer | 1–2 | Static | Bin-Packing / MILP | NP-hard |
| **Bandwidth Allocation** | Continuous | 1 | Static | Convex Opt. | Polynomial |
| **Power Control (5G)** | Continuous | 1–2 | Dynamic | Convex / Game Theory | Polynomial |
| **Network Routing** | Integer | 1 | Static | Shortest-Path / ILP | Polynomial (Dijkstra) |
| **Trust / Security** | Mixed | 1 | Static | Game Theory / Heuristic | NP-hard |
| **Load Balancing** | Discrete | 1 | Online | Round-Robin / RL | Polynomial |
| **Data Caching** | Binary | 1–2 | Dynamic | Markov Decision | NP-hard |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Binary Placement)

Suppose $I=2$ tasks, $J=2$ fog nodes, with parameters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& d = [3, 5] \text{ units}, \quad C = [4, 5] \text{ units}, \quad L = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ ms}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Enumerate all feasible assignments**:

**Option 1** — Task 1 → Node 1, Task 2 → Node 2: $\sum d = [3, 5] \le [4, 5]$ ✓. Latency $= 2 + 1 = 3$ ms.

**Option 2** — Task 1 → Node 1, Task 2 → Node 1: $5+3 = 8 > 4$ ✗ (infeasible).

**Option 3** — Task 1 → Node 2, Task 2 → Node 2: $3+5 = 8 > 5$ ✗.

**Option 4** — Task 1 → Node 2, Task 2 → Node 1: $[5,3] \le [4,5]$ ✗ (Node 1 overloaded).

**Option 5** — Task 1 → Cloud, Task 2 → Node 2: $L^{cloud}_1 = 10$ ms (assumed), $L_{22}=1$ ms. Latency $= 10 + 1 = 11$ ms.

**Option 6** — Task 1 → Node 1, Task 2 → Cloud: Latency $= 2 + 10 = 12$ ms.

**Optimal**: **Option 1**, with **latency = 3 ms**. The optimal policy is $x_{11}=1, x_{22}=1$, all other $x_{ij}=0$.

This tiny example illustrates the *combinatorial explosion*: with $I$ tasks and $J$ nodes, the number of feasible assignments is $O((J+1)^I)$, motivating the need for **heuristics** in real systems.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
