## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
>
> **Q1.** *What is FogTorch? Mention two of its distinguishing features that differentiate it from a conventional cloud scheduler.* **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
FogTorch is an open-source Java prototype that performs **proactive Quality-of-Service (QoS) forecasting** for fog applications at *design time*. It evaluates whether a set of candidate fog nodes can satisfy an application's hardware, software, bandwidth, latency, and cost constraints over a **future time horizon** $[t_s, t_e]$.

**Two distinguishing features:**

1. **Forecast-based, not snapshot-based:** Unlike Kubernetes or OpenStack schedulers that schedule on the *current* state, FogTorch consumes *time-varying predicted profiles* and ensures feasibility for the **entire window**.
2. **Multi-constraint AND semantics:** A configuration is *eligible* only if **all** five constraint classes (hardware, software, bandwidth, latency, cost) hold simultaneously across **all** time slices — an intersection-of-conditions check.

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
>
> **Q2.** *List the five constraint classes checked by FogTorch during a deployment feasibility check.* **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The five constraint classes are:

1. **Hardware** — CPU cores, RAM, storage, I/O capacity.
2. **Software** — OS, libraries, container runtimes, drivers.
3. **Bandwidth** — Data rate between sensor and module host.
4. **Latency** — End-to-end propagation + processing delay budget.
5. **Monetary Cost** — Total hourly/daily cost of all rented fog nodes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question A (Choice 1):**
> A smart-building deployment consists of **3 modules** $m_1, m_2, m_3$ with hardware demands as given below:
>
> | Module | CPU | RAM | Bandwidth |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | $m_1$ | 1 vCPU | 2 GB | 5 Mbps |
> | $m_2$ | 2 vCPU | 4 GB | 10 Mbps |
> | $m_3$ | 3 vCPU | 6 GB | 15 Mbps |
>
> Candidate fog nodes:
>
> | Node | CPU | RAM | BW | Price |
> |---|---|---|---|---|
> | $n_1$ | 4 vCPU | 8 GB | 20 Mbps | 5 INR/hr |
> | $n_2$ | 6 vCPU | 12 GB | 30 Mbps | 8 INR/hr |
> | $n_3$ | 3 vCPU | 6 GB | 15 Mbps | 4 INR/hr |
>
> **Constraints:** $\mathcal{L}_{max} = 25$ ms, $\mathcal{C}_{max} = 16$ INR/hr. Each node can host at most one module.
>
> **(a)** Enumerate **all feasible 1-to-1 mappings** $f: M \to N$ using a feasibility check. Identify and justify those that are eligible. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Discuss how the answer would change if FogTorch were given a *time-varying* profile $R_{n_2}(t) = 12 - 4 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\{t \in \text{peak}\}}$ GB. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Define feasibility predicate:**

$$
\Phi(f) = \big[C_{f(m_i)} \ge c_i\big] \cdot \big[R_{f(m_i)} \ge r_i\big] \cdot \big[B_{f(m_i)} \ge b_i\big] \cdot \big[\mathcal{C}(f) \le 16\big] \quad \forall i \in \{1,2,3\}
$$

**Step 2 — Enumerate promising mappings** (sorted by cost):

| # | $f(m_1)$ | $f(m_2)$ | $f(m_3)$ | CPU OK? | RAM OK? | BW OK? | Cost | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| A1 | $n_1$ | $n_2$ | $n_3$ | 4≥1, 6≥2, 3≥3 ✓ | 8≥2, 12≥4, 6≥6 ✓ | 20≥5, 30≥10, 15≥15 ✓ | 5+8+4=17 | ✗ (cost) |
| A2 | $n_1$ | $n_3$ | $n_2$ | 4≥1, 3≥2, 6≥3 ✓ | 8≥2, 6≥4, 12≥6 ✓ | 20≥5, 15≥10, 30≥15 ✓ | 5+4+8=17 | ✗ (cost) |
| A3 | $n_3$ | $n_1$ | $n_2$ | 3≥1, 4≥2, 6≥3 ✓ | 6≥2, 8≥4, 12≥6 ✓ | 15≥5, 20≥10, 30≥15 ✓ | 4+5+8=17 | ✗ (cost) |
| A4 | $n_2$ | $n_1$ | $n_3$ | 6≥1, 4≥2, 3≥3 ✓ | 12≥2, 8≥4, 6≥6 ✓ | 30≥5, 20≥10, 15≥15 ✓ | 8+5+4=17 | ✗ (cost) |
| A5 | $n_3$ | $n_2$ | $n_1$ | 3≥1, 6≥2, 4≥3 ✓ | 6≥2, 12≥4, 8≥6 ✓ | 15≥5, 30≥10, 20≥15 ✓ | 4+8+5=17 | ✗ (cost) |
| A6 | $n_2$ | $n_3$ | $n_1$ | 6≥1, 3≥2? **NO** | — | — | — | ✗ (CPU) |

> **Conclusion:** All "spread-out" mappings cost **17 INR/hr**, which exceeds the budget of 16 INR/hr by 1 INR/hr. Therefore, **no 1-to-1 mapping is eligible** under the given cost ceiling.

**Step 3 — Try collocated mappings** (multiple modules on one node, scaled cost per module-host):

If $m_1, m_2$ are hosted together on $n_2$ (combined demand CPU=3, RAM=6, BW=15) and $m_3$ on $n_1$ (3,6,15 → needs CPU=3, RAM=6, BW=15) — but $n_1$ has only 4 CPU, 8 GB, 20 Mbps. The 1-to-1 constraint in the problem disallows collocation; we flag this as a *design issue — the budget is too tight*.

**Final Verdict for Part (a):** **No eligible 1-to-1 mapping exists** at $\mathcal{C}_{max} = 16$ INR/hr. The designer should either (i) raise the budget to ≥17 INR/hr, or (ii) relax the 1-to-1 constraint and allow collocation.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

With $R_{n_2}(t) = 12 - 4 \cdot \mathbb{1}_{\{t \in \text{peak}\}}$ GB:

- During **off-peak**: $R_{n_2} = 12$ GB.
- During **peak** (say 09:00–18:00): $R_{n_2} = 8$ GB.

Consider mapping **A5** ($m_1 \!\to\! n_3, m_2 \!\to\! n_2, m_3 \!\to\! n_1$):
- $n_2$ hosts $m_2$ (needs 4 GB).
- During peak: $8 \ge 4$ ✓ (still feasible).
- During off-peak: $12 \ge 4$ ✓.

So A5 remains eligible **in both slices** — the 4 GB requirement is still met during the reduced 8 GB window.

However, if the design demanded 10 GB on $n_2$, the peak window would fail ($8 < 10$), and FogTorch would emit a **time-sliced warning**:
> *"Configuration A5 is INFEASIBLE during peak hours [09:00–18:00]; consider migration to $n_1$ or adding a temporary backup node."*

**Engineering implication:** Time-varying profiles make the *forecast horizon critical*. A static check at $t=t_s$ would falsely certify A5 as feasible, leading to runtime crashes during peak hours. This is precisely the *value-add* of FogTorch.

> **Marks distribution for (a):** 2 marks feasibility predicate, 3 marks table of mappings, 2 marks correct conclusion. For (b): 3 marks derivation of time-sliced check, 2 marks interpretation, 2 marks engineering recommendation.

---

> **Question B (Choice 2):**
> **(a)** With a labelled block diagram, explain how FogTorch integrates into a smart-building fog deployment. Identify the input, processing, and output stages. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A smart-building operator currently uses a *reactive* (runtime) scheduler. Compare and contrast it with FogTorch's *proactive* (design-time) approach in terms of **(i)** decision timing, **(ii)** information available, **(iii)** failure modes, **(iv)** cost optimisation, and **(v)** scalability. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Block diagram (textual, as requested):**

```
+-------------------- SMART-BUILDING FOG DEPLOYMENT --------------------+
|                                                                        |
|  [Sensors] --> [Gateway Aggregator] --> [Candidate Fog Nodes N]        |
|                                          |                              |
|                                          v                              |
|                                  [FogTorch Engine]                     |
|                                          ^                              |
|            [App Modules M] -----------+   |                              |
|            [QoS Constraints Q] -------+   |                              |
|                                          v                              |
|                              [Eligible Configurations]                 |
|                                          |                              |
|                                          v                              |
|                              [Fog Orchestrator / Deployer]             |
|                                          |                              |
|                                          v                              |
|                            [Running Application on Fog]                |
|                                          |                              |
|                                          v                              |
|                              [Optional: Cloud Archive]                 |
+------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

**Three stages identified:**

- **Input stage:** Application modules $M$, candidate fog nodes $N$ (with profiles $P$), QoS constraints $Q$, time horizon $T$.
- **Processing stage:** FogTorch's combinatorial search and time-sliced feasibility check.
- **Output stage:** A ranked list of *eligible deployment configurations* handed to the fog orchestrator.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Dimension | Reactive (Runtime) Scheduler | Proactive (FogTorch) |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Decision timing** | At deployment or on failure | At design time, *before* deployment |
| **(ii) Information available** | Current node state snapshot | Time-varying predicted profile across $[t_s, t_e]$ |
| **(iii) Failure modes** | Mid-run SLA violations, costly migrations | Caught at design stage, avoided in production |
| **(iv) Cost optimisation** | Local, myopic — chooses cheapest *now* | Global — can pick slightly more expensive node that *stays* available |
| **(v) Scalability** | Scales with runtime events; thrashing possible | Scales with search space at design time; no runtime thrash |

> **Conclusion:** FogTorch is *complementary* to a runtime scheduler — it produces a *certificate of feasibility*; the runtime scheduler then enforces and monitors that certificate, triggering re-planning only when actual state diverges from the forecast.

> **Marks distribution for (a):** 3 marks diagram, 2 marks stage identification, 2 marks explanation. For (b): 1 mark per dimension × 5 = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for the synthesis conclusion.

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in FogTorch Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "AND over time" rule** — Students check constraints only at $t=t_s$, losing 2–3 marks. Always state: *"Constraint must hold for every $t \in [t_s, t_e]$."*
> 2. **Confusing design-time vs runtime** — FogTorch is *not* a runtime scheduler. Students who write "FogTorch will migrate the module at runtime" lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Missing the cost summation across all modules** — Cost is additive over the *chosen* nodes, not a single-node property. Show the summation explicitly.
> 4. **Ignoring software constraints** — Hardware alone is not enough; OS, Docker, library versions must also match. The 5-constraint AND must be drawn explicitly.
> 5. **Writing "fog" instead of "fog node"** — KTU expects precise vocabulary. Use *fog node*, *fog infrastructure*, *fog orchestrator* — never "the fog" as a single noun.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Smart-building predictive analysis** runs ML on IoT streams to forecast occupancy, energy, and faults.
- **Fog computing** places compute *near the data* — in the building premises — to cut latency, bandwidth, and privacy risk.
- **FogTorch** is a **design-time, Java-based QoS-forecasting prototype** — *not* a runtime scheduler.
- It consumes **5 input classes**: application modules $M$, fog nodes $N$, profiles $P$, constraints $Q$, time horizon $T$.
- It checks **5 constraint classes** in AND: Hardware, Software, Bandwidth, Latency, Cost.
- Feasibility is **time-sliced**: $\forall t \in [t_s, t_e] : \Phi(f, t) = 1$.
- The output is a **ranked list of eligible configurations** handed to a fog orchestrator.
- The canonical formula is:

$$
\Phi(f) = \prod_{c \in \{H,S,B,L,C\}} \mathbb{1}\!\left[\forall t \in [t_s, t_e]: \; c(f, t) \le c_{max}\right]
$$

- **Cost** is the only monetary dimension; $\mathcal{C}(f) = \sum_i \text{price}(f(m_i)) \le \mathcal{C}_{max}$.
- **Real-world value:** Prevents runtime SLA violations, enables cost-aware multi-node placement, supports privacy-by-design (data never leaves the building for inference).
- **KTU-favourite answers** always end with: *"FogTorch is a design-time decision-support tool that *forecasts* feasibility; the actual deployment and runtime monitoring are handled by a separate fog orchestrator."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning in Fog Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning in Fog Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Machine Learning (ML) in Fog Computing** refers to the deployment, training, inference, and continuous adaptation of data-driven learning algorithms across the hierarchical fog–cloud continuum (i.e., end devices → fog nodes → cloud) so that intelligent decisions (such as workload offloading, anomaly detection, predictive maintenance, resource allocation, and context awareness) can be made **close to the data source** with minimal latency, reduced bandwidth consumption, and improved privacy.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 scheme**, this is positioned as a transversal enabling technology that converts fog nodes from passive compute intermediaries into **autonomous, self-learning agents** capable of performing localized inference and collaborative model refinement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Machine Learning in Fog Computing is NOT a standalone ML course. It focuses on **how** classical and modern ML paradigms (Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement, Federated, and Deep Learning) are *re-architected* to operate under the constraints of fog nodes — limited compute, intermittent connectivity, heterogeneous hardware, and privacy sensitivity.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Hospital Triage Model"**
>
> Imagine a large hospital with:
> - **Patients (sensors / IoT devices)** generating symptoms (data) every second.
> - **Local Clinics (fog nodes)** where a trained nurse (ML model) quickly decides: *"Is this critical? Can it be treated here, or do we send it to the specialist?"*
> - **City Hospital (cloud)** that trains *senior doctors* (global ML models) using aggregated case histories.
> - **Trainee Doctors (federated learning)** who periodically visit clinics, learn locally, share only *insights* (model gradients — not patient records), and return upgraded.
>
> This is precisely how ML operates in fog: **local inference, collaborative training, privacy preservation, and reduced data travel.**

### 1.3 Why ML Becomes Necessary at the Fog Layer

The traditional cloud-centric analytics model breaks down under three pressures:

| Pressure | Cloud-Only Limitation | ML-at-Fog Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | Round-trip to cloud ≥ 50–200 ms | Local inference ≤ 1–10 ms |
| **Bandwidth** | Zettabytes of raw IoT data | Only predictions / gradients uploaded |
| **Privacy** | Raw data leaves device | Data stays local; federated learning |
| **Autonomy** | Cloud outage halts analytics | Models operate offline |
| **Energy** | Continuous transmission drains IoT | Event-driven local execution |

### 1.4 Core Taxonomy — Where Each ML Type Fits in Fog

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this taxonomy — it is **a high-frequency KTU question area** for Part A and direct 3-mark items.

1. **Supervised Learning in Fog** → Used when labeled sensor data exists (e.g., fault = yes/no). Algorithms: Decision Trees, SVM, k-NN, lightweight neural networks.
2. **Unsupervised Learning in Fog** → Used for clustering unlabeled IoT streams (e.g., anomaly detection, sensor grouping). Algorithms: k-Means, DBSCAN, Autoencoders.
3. **Reinforcement Learning (RL) in Fog** → Used for sequential decision-making (e.g., dynamic task offloading). Algorithms: Q-Learning, Deep Q-Networks (DQN), Actor-Critic.
4. **Federated Learning (FL)** → Distributed model training without centralizing raw data. Algorithms: FedAvg, FedProx, FedNova.
5. **Deep Learning at the Edge** → Compressed CNN/LSTM variants (TinyML, MobileNet, pruned models) deployed on fog gateways.

### 1.5 The Role of Standard Metrics

The following KTU-relevant metrics are highlighted throughout this module:

- **Latency** (in milliseconds)
- **Accuracy** (in %)
- **Communication Rounds** (for FL convergence)
- **Energy Consumption** (in Joules)
- **Model Size** (in MB / number of parameters)
- **Convergence Time** (in epochs / seconds)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The ML-on-Fog Operational Pipeline

The deployment of ML in a fog environment follows a deterministic six-stage pipeline:

1. **Data Acquisition at the Edge** — Sensors/actuators generate raw streams (temperature, vibration, video, audio).
2. **Preprocessing at the Fog Node** — Noise filtering, normalization, feature extraction (e.g., FFT, PCA).
3. **Local Inference** — A pre-trained model produces predictions in real time.
4. **Local Model Update** — Gradients are computed on freshly labeled (or pseudo-labeled) data.
5. **Collaborative Aggregation** — Local updates are sent to a fog orchestrator (parameter server) or peer fog nodes.
6. **Global Model Distribution** — The aggregated model is broadcast back to fog nodes for the next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> Steps 3 and 4 are *latency-critical*; Steps 5 and 6 are *bandwidth-critical*. KTU questions often ask how ML addresses each constraint separately.

### 2.2 The Fog-Aware ML Design Principles

When designing ML for fog, four constraints govern every algorithmic choice:

- **Lightweight Models** — Models must fit within the memory and compute envelope of fog gateways (typically **≤ 500 MB RAM**, **≤ 4 CPU cores**).
- **Asynchronous Updates** — Unlike synchronous cloud training, fog nodes may go offline; algorithms must tolerate stragglers.
- **Heterogeneity Tolerance** — Devices have unequal compute; personalization is required.
- **Privacy by Design** — Differential privacy, secure aggregation, homomorphic encryption may be layered on top.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every equation** that KTU examiners can realistically ask in Module 4 derivations. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | KTU Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)$ | $\theta$ = parameters, $N$ = samples, $\ell$ = loss | Foundation of all supervised training |
| 2 | Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$ | $\eta$ = learning rate, $t$ = iteration | Local training at fog node |
| 3 | Federated Averaging (FedAvg) — Local Update | $\theta_{t+1}^{k} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_k(\theta_t)$ | $k$ = client (fog node) index | Each fog node trains locally |
| 4 | FedAvg — Global Aggregation | $\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \theta_{t+1}^{k}$ | $n_k$ = samples at node $k$, $N$ = total | Parameter server combines updates |
| 5 | Q-Learning Bellman Update | $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]$ | $\alpha$ = LR, $\gamma$ = discount, $r$ = reward | RL-based offloading decisions |
| 6 | Anomaly Score (Autoencoder) | $s(x) = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^{2}$ | $x$ = input, $\hat{x}$ = reconstruction | Fog-level anomaly detection |
| 7 | Communication Cost of FL | $C_{comm} = 2 \cdot M \cdot R \cdot K$ | $M$ = model size, $R$ = rounds, $K$ = clients | Bandwidth estimation |
| 8 | Energy per Local Iteration | $E = \alpha \cdot c \cdot f^{2} \cdot V^{2}$ | $c$ = cycles, $f$ = frequency, $V$ = voltage | Energy-aware fog scheduling |
| 9 | Inference Latency | $T_{inf} = T_{compute} + T_{transmit}$ | $T$ in seconds | SLA evaluation |
| 10 | Model Compression Ratio | $CR = \frac{\lvert \theta_{orig} \rvert}{\lvert \theta_{comp} \rvert}$ | Ratio (unitless) | Pruning / quantization effect |
| 11 | Convergence Bound (FedAvg) | $\mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}(\theta_T)] - \mathcal{L}^{\star} \leq \mathcal{O}\!\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}} + \frac{1}{K}\right)$ | $T$ = rounds, $K$ = clients | Theoretical FL convergence |
| 12 | Differential Privacy Noise | $\tilde{\theta} = \theta + \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^{2} I)$ | $\sigma$ = noise scale | Privacy preservation in FL |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Rule Followed:** In the table above, $\lvert x \rvert$ denotes magnitude (not the markdown table pipe), preserving table integrity.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

In real engineering deployments, ML-in-Fog is used for:

- **Smart grids** — load forecasting at substation fog nodes.
- **Autonomous vehicles** — perception models on-board + V2X cooperative learning.
- **Healthcare wearables** — arrhythmia detection without cloud round-trip.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — predictive maintenance on factory fog gateways.
- **Smart cities** — adaptive traffic control via reinforcement learning at intersection fog nodes.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No shortcuts, no "it can be shown that…" placeholders. Each derivation reflects what a KTU examiner expects on a 14-mark question paper.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Empirical Risk Minimization to Gradient Descent in Fog

**Goal:** Show how a fog node locally updates its model parameters using SGD.

**Step 1 — Start with the ERM objective (per fog node $k$):**

$$
\mathcal{L}_k(\theta) = \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{D}_k} \ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gradient of the empirical loss with respect to parameters $\theta$:**

$$
\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_k(\theta) = \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{D}_k} \nabla_\theta \ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate the full gradient using a mini-batch $\mathcal{B}_k$ of size $B$ (this is SGD, applicable at fog nodes with limited memory):**

$$
\hat{g}_k(\theta) = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}_k} \nabla_\theta \ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the parameter update rule for one local epoch:**

$$
\theta_{t+1}^{k} = \theta_t^{k} - \eta \cdot \hat{g}_k(\theta_t)
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the engineering meaning:**

- Each fog node $k$ performs $E$ local epochs before uploading.
- The learning rate $\eta$ governs convergence speed; too high → divergence, too low → slow training.
- The mini-batch approximation $B$ reduces memory footprint on resource-constrained fog nodes.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Insight:** A 7-mark sub-question typically asks: *"Derive the SGD update rule starting from ERM."* Memorize Steps 1–4 in order.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Federated Averaging (FedAvg) Global Aggregation

**Goal:** Derive how a fog orchestrator (parameter server) combines updates from $K$ fog nodes.

**Step 1 — Total samples across all fog nodes:**

$$
N = \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k
$$

**Step 2 — Weight of each fog node proportional to its data size:**

$$
w_k = \frac{n_k}{N}
$$

**Step 3 — Global model after aggregation (weighted average of local models):**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \, \theta_{t+1}^{k}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $w_k$ explicitly:**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \, \theta_{t+1}^{k}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the local update from Section 3.1, Step 4:**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \left( \theta_t - \eta \hat{g}_k(\theta_t) \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Factor out the global model $\theta_t$:**

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \, \hat{g}_k(\theta_t)
$$

**Step 7 — Recognize that the weighted sum of local gradients approximates the global gradient:**

$$
\theta_{t+1} \approx \theta_t - \eta \, \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Steps 5–7 prove that FedAvg is mathematically equivalent to *distributed SGD* — but executed across heterogeneous fog nodes without sharing raw data.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Q-Learning Update for Fog Offloading Decisions

**Goal:** Derive the Bellman update used by a fog node to decide whether to offload a task.

**Step 1 — Define state $s$ (CPU load, queue length, network condition), action $a$ (offload locally / to peer fog / to cloud), and reward $r$ (negative of latency + energy).**

**Step 2 — Total expected discounted reward:**

$$
G_t = r_{t+1} + \gamma r_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} r_{t+3} + \ldots = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{i} r_{t+i+1}
$$

**Step 3 — Q-value function definition:**

$$
Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid s_t = s, a_t = a \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Bellman optimality equation:**

$$
Q^\star(s, a) = \mathbb{E} \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^\star(s_{t+1}, a') \mid s_t = s, a_t = a \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Convert expectation into iterative sample-based update:**

$$
Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a) \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Engineering tie-in:** At each time step, the fog node observes $(s, a, r, s')$, applies Step 5, and uses $\arg\max_a Q(s,a)$ to decide the offloading action.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse $\alpha$ (learning rate) with $\gamma$ (discount factor). $\alpha$ controls **how fast** the Q-value updates; $\gamma$ controls **how much future rewards matter**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Selection Cheat Sheet

| Fog Scenario | Best ML Algorithm | Reason | Trade-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Real-time anomaly detection in sensor stream | **Online k-Means / Autoencoder** | Unsupervised, streaming | May miss rare classes |
| Predictive maintenance (labeled fault data) | **Random Forest / LightGBM** | Robust, handles tabular | Slightly heavier than DT |
| Privacy-sensitive healthcare FL | **FedAvg + Differential Privacy** | Data never leaves device | Accuracy ↓ by 1–3 % |
| Adaptive task offloading | **Deep Q-Network (DQN)** | Handles high-dimensional state | Needs GPU on fog node |
| Tiny device (microcontroller) | **TinyML / Spiking Neural Net** | Sub-mW inference | Limited expressiveness |
| Heterogeneous fog with stragglers | **FedProx** | Tolerates partial work | Slower convergence |
| Video analytics at gateway | **MobileNet / EfficientNet (pruned)** | Balanced accuracy/size | Lower top-1 accuracy |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — ML Pipeline Across Fog–Cloud Continuum

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is rendered in Mermaid. All node IDs are alphanumeric (no reserved keywords), and labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without inline markdown.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef edge fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0284C7,color:#0C4A6E
    classDef fog fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#16A34A,color:#14532D
    classDef cloud fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#D97706,color:#78350F
    classDef data fill:#FAE8FF,stroke:#A21CAF,color:#581C87

    data1[IoT Sensors Layer]:::edge
    data2[Edge Gateways]:::edge

    fog1[Fog Node Inference Engine]:::fog
    fog2[Local Model Trainer]:::fog
    fog3[Fog Orchestrator / Parameter Server]:::fog

    cloud1[Cloud Global Trainer]:::cloud
    cloud2[Model Registry and Version Control]:::cloud

    db1[Anomaly Detection Model]:::data
    db2[Reinforcement Learning Policy]:::data
    db3[Federated Model Snapshot]:::data

    data1 --> data2
    data2 --> fog1
    fog1 --> fog2
    fog2 --> fog3
    fog3 --> cloud1
    cloud1 --> cloud2
    cloud2 --> fog3
    fog1 --> db1
    fog2 --> db2
    fog3 --> db3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Blue tier = **Edge** (sensors / gateways).
- Green tier = **Fog** (local inference + local training + orchestrator).
- Yellow tier = **Cloud** (heavy training + model versioning).
- Purple tier = **Model artifacts** that move between layers.

---

### 4.2 Federated Learning Round Sequence at Fog Nodes

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PS as Parameter Server
    participant F1 as Fog Node 1
    participant F2 as Fog Node 2
    participant F3 as Fog Node 3

    PS->>F1: Broadcast Global Model Theta T
    PS->>F2: Broadcast Global Model Theta T
    PS->>F3: Broadcast Global Model Theta T

    par Local Training Round
        F1->>F1: Train on Local Data D1
        F2->>F2: Train on Local Data D2
        F3->>F3: Train on Local Data D3
    end

    F1->>PS: Upload Update Theta 1 Local
    F2->>PS: Upload Update Theta 2 Local
    F3->>PS: Upload Update Theta 3 Local

    PS->>PS: Aggregate Weighted Average FedAvg
    PS->>F1: Broadcast Theta T Plus 1
    PS->>F2: Broadcast Theta T Plus 1
    PS->>F3: Broadcast Theta T Plus 1
```

**Explanation of each step:**
1. The **parameter server** (which may itself reside on a fog orchestrator or in the cloud) distributes the current global model.
2. Each **fog node** performs local SGD on its private dataset — raw data **never leaves**.
3. Only **parameter deltas / updated weights** are transmitted back.
4. The server performs **weighted aggregation** (Equation from Section 3.2, Step 4).
5. The new global model is broadcast, and the cycle repeats until convergence.

---

### 4.3 Reinforcement Learning Loop for Dynamic Task Offloading

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef agent fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#BE185D,color:#831843
    classDef env fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#4338CA,color:#312E81

    stateA[State S: CPU load, queue, network]:::env
    agentA[RL Agent at Fog Node]:::agent
    actionA[Action A: Local or Peer or Cloud]:::agent
    rewardA[Reward R: Negative latency plus energy]:::agent
    nextA[Next State S Prime]:::env

    stateA --> agentA
    agentA --> actionA
    actionA --> rewardA
    rewardA --> agentA
    actionA --> nextA
    nextA --> stateA
```

**Engineering reading:** The fog node acts as the *agent*; the underlying fog infrastructure is the *environment*. At every decision tick, the agent samples an action, observes a reward, transitions to a new state, and updates its Q-table using Equation (5) from the formula sheet.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *Define Machine Learning in the context of Fog Computing. List any two advantages of deploying ML at the fog layer instead of the cloud.*

**Model Answer (Expected ~80–100 words):**

Machine Learning in Fog Computing refers to the execution of data-driven learning algorithms — including inference, training, and model adaptation — at intermediate fog nodes between the edge devices and the cloud. The objective is to enable intelligent, latency-sensitive decisions close to the data source.

**Two Advantages:**

1. **Reduced Latency:** Local inference avoids the network round-trip to the cloud, enabling millisecond-level response for time-critical IoT applications.
2. **Bandwidth Conservation:** Only model parameters or predictions are transmitted upstream, reducing WAN bandwidth consumption by orders of magnitude.

*(A third advantage that may be offered: improved privacy, since raw data is retained locally and only model gradients are shared.)*

---

> **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
> *What is Federated Learning? Briefly explain the FedAvg aggregation rule used at the fog orchestrator.*

**Model Answer (Expected ~80–100 words):**

Federated Learning (FL) is a distributed ML paradigm in which multiple clients (fog nodes) collaboratively train a shared global model **without exposing their local raw data**. Each client trains locally and uploads only model updates.

The **FedAvg aggregation rule** at the orchestrator is:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \, \theta_{t+1}^{k}
$$

where $\theta_{t+1}^{k}$ is the locally trained model at fog node $k$, $n_k$ is the number of samples at that node, $N = \sum n_k$ is the total sample count, and $\theta_{t+1}$ is the new global model. The aggregation is a *weighted average* proportional to each node's data contribution.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Module Internal Choice):**
> Students answer **ONE** full question from the choice offered. Each question is divided into **sub-part (a) — 7 marks** and **sub-part (b) — 7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture for deploying Machine Learning models across the **Fog–Cloud continuum**. Discuss the role of each layer (Edge, Fog, Cloud) in the ML pipeline. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Layer 1 — Edge Layer (Data Acquisition):**
- Comprises IoT sensors, actuators, smartphones, and embedded devices.
- Generates high-velocity, high-volume raw data (e.g., temperature, vibration, video frames).
- May perform *minimal* preprocessing such as noise filtering or quantization.
- **Role in ML pipeline:** Acts as the *data source* and occasionally runs ultra-lightweight inference (TinyML).

**Layer 2 — Fog Layer (Local Intelligence):**
- Includes fog nodes / gateways / micro-data centers with moderate compute (8–32 GB RAM, GPU optional).
- Performs three ML operations:
  1. **Local inference** — runs pre-trained models (e.g., MobileNet for image classification).
  2. **Local training** — performs SGD on private local data (for FL).
  3. **Orchestration** — one fog node acts as the parameter server for FedAvg.
- **Role:** Provides low-latency decisions and privacy-preserving collaborative learning.

**Layer 3 — Cloud Layer (Heavy Training):**
- Houses powerful GPU/TPU clusters for training large foundation models.
- Maintains the **model registry** with versioning, A/B testing, and audit trails.
- Performs offline analytics on aggregated (non-privacy-sensitive) metrics.
- **Role:** Global model development, distribution, and lifecycle management.

**Data flow:** Sensors → Fog (real-time inference + local training) → Cloud (heavy training + registry) → Fog (updated model broadcast) → Sensors (improved decisions).

**Cross-reference:** See Section 4.1 diagram in these notes for the schematic.

**(b)** Explain **Federated Learning** in detail. Derive the **FedAvg aggregation formula** step by step starting from the per-client loss objective. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Federated Learning is a decentralized ML paradigm where multiple clients (fog nodes) collaboratively train a shared model under the coordination of a central server, while keeping training data localized. It addresses three pillars: **privacy, bandwidth efficiency, and personalization**.

**Per-client Loss (1 mark):**
For fog node $k$ with local dataset $\mathcal{D}_k$ of size $n_k$:

$$
\mathcal{L}_k(\theta) = \frac{1}{n_k} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{D}_k} \ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)
$$

**Local SGD Update (1 mark):**
Each fog node performs $E$ local epochs of SGD:

$$
\theta_{t+1}^{k} = \theta_t^{k} - \eta \, \nabla \mathcal{L}_k(\theta_t^{k})
$$

**Global Aggregation — Step-by-Step Derivation (3 marks):**

*Step 1:* Define total samples:

$$
N = \sum_{k=1}^{K} n_k
$$

*Step 2:* Assign weight proportional to data size:

$$
w_k = \frac{n_k}{N}
$$

*Step 3:* Weighted average of local models at the server:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} w_k \, \theta_{t+1}^{k}
$$

*Step 4:* Substitute $w_k$:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \, \theta_{t+1}^{k}
$$

*Step 5:* Substitute the local update:

$$
\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \left( \theta_t - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}_k(\theta_t) \right) = \theta_t - \eta \sum_{k=1}^{K} \frac{n_k}{N} \nabla \mathcal{L}_k(\theta_t)
$$

The aggregated gradient approximates the global gradient, proving FedAvg is mathematically equivalent to distributed SGD — but executed with **raw data staying local**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:**
> Students frequently write $\theta_{t+1} = \frac{1}{K}\sum \theta_{t+1}^{k}$ (simple average). This is **wrong** when fog nodes have unequal data sizes. The correct weighting **must** be $\frac{n_k}{N}$. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for this mistake.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]

**(a)** Explain **Reinforcement Learning** in the context of fog computing. Derive the **Q-Learning Bellman update equation** used for dynamic task offloading decisions at a fog node. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Reinforcement Learning (RL) at Fog — Concept (2 marks):**
RL enables a fog node to learn an optimal **policy** $\pi(s) \to a$ through interaction with its environment (neighboring fog nodes, cloud, network conditions), without requiring a labeled dataset. The agent receives a scalar *reward* signal after each action.

**Application — Task Offloading (1 mark):**
At each decision tick, the fog node decides:
- $a_1$ = execute task locally,
- $a_2$ = offload to peer fog node,
- $a_3$ = offload to cloud.

The reward is constructed as:

$$
r = -(\alpha \cdot \text{latency} + \beta \cdot \text{energy} + \delta \cdot \text{cost})
$$

**Q-Learning Derivation (4 marks):**

*Step 1 — Define the return (sum of discounted future rewards):*

$$
G_t = \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{i} r_{t+i+1}, \quad 0 \leq \gamma < 1
$$

*Step 2 — Define the action-value function:*

$$
Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ G_t \mid s_t = s, a_t = a \right]
$$

*Step 3 — Apply the recursive Bellman expectation:*

$$
Q^\pi(s,a) = \mathbb{E}_\pi \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma Q^\pi(s_{t+1}, a_{t+1}) \mid s_t = s, a_t = a \right]
$$

*Step 4 — Convert to the Bellman optimality form (greedy over actions):*

$$
Q^\star(s,a) = \mathbb{E} \left[ r_{t+1} + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^\star(s_{t+1}, a') \mid s_t = s, a_t = a \right]
$$

*Step 5 — Convert to a sample-based iterative update rule:*

$$
\boxed{Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha \left[ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) \right]}
$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = learning rate (step size of update),
- $\gamma$ = discount factor (importance of future rewards),
- the bracketed term is the **TD-error** (temporal-difference error).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:**
> Common errors: (i) omitting the $\max$ operator in the target value, (ii) swapping $\alpha$ and $\gamma$, (iii) using $\gamma > 1$ which makes the series diverge.

**(b)** Discuss any **four challenges** of deploying Machine Learning models in fog computing environments. For each challenge, suggest one suitable mitigation strategy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (each item carries ~1.75 marks):**

**Challenge 1 — Resource Constraints:**
Fog nodes have limited memory, CPU, and power compared to cloud servers. Heavy deep models (e.g., ResNet-152) cannot run directly.
→ **Mitigation:** Use **model compression** techniques — pruning, quantization (INT8), knowledge distillation — to deploy TinyML or MobileNet-class models.

**Challenge 2 — Heterogeneity:**
Fog nodes differ in hardware capability, OS, and available libraries. A model trained on one node may fail to run on another.
→ **Mitigation:** Use **containerization** (Docker) and **ONNX / TFLite** portable model formats; adopt hardware-aware Neural Architecture Search (NAS).

**Challenge 3 — Data Heterogeneity (Non-IID):**
Local data at each fog node follows different distributions (concept drift, user-specific behavior). FedAvg accuracy may degrade.
→ **Mitigation:** Use **FedProx** (adds a proximal term) or **personalized FL** with per-node model heads.

**Challenge 4 — Privacy and Security:**
Raw data, even if not uploaded, may be inferable from gradients (gradient inversion attacks).
→ **Mitigation:** Apply **Differential Privacy** noise $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$ to gradients, or use **Secure Aggregation** cryptographic protocols.

*(Optional 5th — if examiner expects 5 items in 14 marks: Communication cost → mitigation = gradient sparsification or top-k compression.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:**
> Students list challenges but **fail to pair** them with mitigations. Examiners award partial credit only when challenge ↔ mitigation is clearly *one-to-one mapped*. Write them as pairs in the answer sheet.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **ML in Fog ≠ Cloud ML.** The deployment layer, constraints, and optimization goals differ.
- [x] The **six-stage pipeline**: Acquire → Preprocess → Infer → Update → Aggregate → Distribute.
- [x] **Supervised** = labeled; **Unsupervised** = unlabeled; **Reinforcement** = reward-driven; **Federated** = privacy-driven.
- [x] **SGD update:** $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla \mathcal{L}(\theta_t)$.
- [x] **FedAvg aggregation:** $\theta_{t+1} = \sum_{k} \frac{n_k}{N} \theta_{t+1}^{k}$ — **weighted by data size**, not simple average.
- [x] **Q-Learning update:** $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha [ r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a) ]$.
- [x] **Anomaly score:** $s(x) = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert_2^{2}$ — reconstruction error in autoencoders.
- [x] **Differential privacy** adds Gaussian noise $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2 I)$ to gradients.
- [x] **Convergence bound:** $\mathbb{E}[\mathcal{L}(\theta_T)] - \mathcal{L}^{\star} \leq \mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T} + 1/K)$.
- [x] **Communication cost:** $C_{comm} = 2 M R K$ — depends on model size, rounds, and clients.
- [x] **Compression ratio:** $CR = \lvert \theta_{orig} \rvert / \lvert \theta_{comp} \rvert$ — measures deployment friendliness.
- [x] **TinyML** is the extreme edge of the spectrum — sub-mW inference on microcontrollers.
- [x] **FedProx** is preferred over FedAvg in heterogeneous, non-IID fog environments.
- [x] Always mention **privacy + bandwidth + latency** as the trinity motivating fog-ML.
- [x] In answers, **draw the architecture diagram** even if not asked — KTU examiners award visibility marks.
- [x] When asked about challenges, **always pair** them with mitigations (one-to-one mapping).
- [x] Memorize the **naming difference** between $\alpha$ (learning rate) and $\gamma$ (discount factor) in Q-Learning.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "FLATS-R":**
> **F**ederated, **L**ightweight, **A**synchronous, **T**inyML, **S**ecure, **R**einforcement.
> These six pillars summarize the *entire* design philosophy of ML in fog computing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Analytics in the Fog
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Analytics in the Fog

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Data Analytics in the Fog** refers to the application of analytical techniques, statistical modelling, machine learning inference, and data-mining operations on the intermediate computational layer (the **Fog Layer**) that exists between the resource-constrained edge devices (sensors, IoT endpoints) and the distant centralized cloud data centres. The fog layer is characterized by its **hierarchical, distributed, and latency-aware** nature, comprising gateways, micro-data-centres, and intermediate networking nodes.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus terminology, fog analytics transforms the traditional "sense → transmit → store in cloud → analyse" pipeline into an optimized **"sense → filter → pre-process → analyse locally → transmit only insights"** pipeline. This is governed by the **Quality of Service (QoS)** triad: **latency**, **bandwidth conservation**, and **decision freshness**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECIT867 Module 4):**
> The OpenFog Consortium / IEEE 1934 reference model treats analytics as a first-class citizen of the fog architecture. The analytics functions are decomposed into:
> - **In-Network Analytics** – executed on routing/switching nodes (L2/L3).
> - **In-Gateway Analytics** – executed on fog nodes (L1).
> - **Cross-Fog Collaboration** – distributed analytics across peer fog nodes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Hospital" Intuition

Imagine a city's traffic control room. The cloud is like a **distant headquarters** in another country — it has the most powerful computers, but every car-report must travel thousands of kilometres to reach it, causing fatal delays in a crash. The **fog layer is the city-level regional control room** located just minutes away from the streets. It receives continuous feeds from thousands of local cameras and speed sensors, performs immediate analytics (e.g., "accident detected in 4 seconds at Junction 7", "average speed dropped by 40%"), and only sends *summarized, high-value insights* to the headquarters.

**The Parallel Mapping:**

| Hospital Concept | Fog Analytics Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Patient vitals from bedside monitor | Raw IoT sensor data streams |
| Triage nurse (filters critical cases) | Edge pre-processing & filtering |
| City-level command centre | Fog node analytics engine |
| National health statistics database | Cloud data warehouse |
| Ambulance dispatch decision | Real-time prescriptive analytics |

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Analytics in Fog Computing

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — The 4 Levels of Data Analytics:**
> 1. **Descriptive Analytics** – *"What happened?"* Aggregation, summarization, KPI computation on historical/recent data.
> 2. **Diagnostic Analytics** – *"Why did it happen?"* Root-cause analysis, correlation discovery, drill-down operations.
> 3. **Predictive Analytics** – *"What will happen?"* Regression, time-series forecasting, classification using ML models.
> 4. **Prescriptive Analytics** – *"What should we do?"* Optimization, reinforcement learning, decision recommendation engines.

### 1.4 Why Fog Analytics? — The Compelling Drivers

- **Latency-critical decisions**: Industrial IoT requires sub-**10 ms** response times which are physically impossible from a cloud located >**1000 km** away (speed of light in fibre ≈ **200,000 km/s**).
- **Bandwidth economics**: A single autonomous vehicle generates ≈ **4 TB/day**. Transmitting 100% raw data is economically and technically infeasible.
- **Privacy and sovereignty**: GDPR, HIPAA, and India's DPDP Act 2023 mandate *data minimization* — process locally, transmit only anonymized aggregates.
- **Resilience**: Fog analytics continues operating during cloud disconnections (a fog node can survive 24–72 hours of WAN outage).

### 1.5 Standard Performance Metrics in Fog Analytics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Standard Metrics (must memorize for KTU 2024):**
> - **E2E Latency (L):** Total delay from sensor event → action decision. Unit: **milliseconds (ms)**.
> - **Jitter (J):** Variation in latency. Unit: **ms**.
> - **Data Reduction Ratio (DRR):** (Bytes_in – Bytes_out) / Bytes_in. Unitless, range **[0, 1]**.
> - **Throughput (T):** Events processed per second. Unit: **EPS (Events Per Second)**.
> - **Accuracy Retention (AR):** Decision accuracy of fog vs cloud. Unit: **%**.
> - **Energy per Inference (E_pi):** Joules consumed per analytics decision. Unit: **J/inference**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Decomposition of Fog Analytics

The fog analytics pipeline is structured into **five sequential logical stages**. Each stage is independently mapped onto different physical layers of the fog hierarchy.

#### Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Edge Layer)
- Sensors (temperature, vibration, camera, LiDAR) sample the physical world.
- Sampling rate is governed by the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**: to reconstruct a signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$, the sampling rate $f_s$ must satisfy:
$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
- A typical vibration sensor in a predictive-maintenance system samples at **$f_s = 50 \text{ kHz}$** to capture fault-bearing frequencies up to **$f_{max} = 25 \text{ kHz}$**.

#### Stage 2 — Pre-Processing & Filtering (Edge/Fog Gateway)
Operations performed: noise removal, outlier detection, normalization, feature extraction.
- Common filters: **Moving Average (MA)**, **Exponential Moving Average (EMA)**, **Kalman Filter**, **Median Filter**.
- The Exponential Moving Average at time $t$ is:
$$EMA_t = \alpha \cdot x_t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot EMA_{t-1}$$
where $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ is the smoothing constant. Higher $\alpha$ → more reactive, lower $\alpha$ → smoother.

#### Stage 3 — Local Analytics Engine (Fog Node)
- Runs ML models: regression, decision trees, neural networks, anomaly detectors.
- Performs **window-based aggregations** on streaming data (tumbling, sliding, session windows).
- Deploys the **Complex Event Processing (CEP)** engine to detect temporal patterns across multiple streams.

#### Stage 4 — Collaborative/ Distributed Analytics (Fog-to-Fog)
- Peer fog nodes exchange **partial results** (not raw data) to build a global view.
- Implemented using **MapReduce-over-fog** or **parameter server** architecture.
- Communication uses gossip protocols, MQTT, or DDS (Data Distribution Service).

#### Stage 5 — Insight Transmission & Actuation (Cloud/Actuator)
- Compressed insights (e.g., anomaly scores, forecasted values) are streamed to cloud.
- Cloud performs long-term analytics, model retraining, and pushes updated ML models back to fog.
- Closed-loop control: actuator receives control signal within the latency budget.

### 2.2 Streaming vs. Batch Analytics in Fog

| Property | Streaming Analytics | Batch Analytics |
|---|---|---|
| **Data Window** | Continuous, unbounded | Finite, bounded historical |
| **Latency Tolerance** | < 100 ms | Minutes to hours |
| **Typical Algorithms** | Sliding window stats, CEP, online ML | MapReduce, Spark, offline training |
| **Fog Suitability** | Excellent (real-time decisions) | Limited (fog is not a data lake) |
| **Example** | Bearing fault detection in CNC | Monthly energy consumption report |

### 2.3 Analytical Models & Their Mathematical Foundations

#### 2.3.1 Linear Regression for Predictive Analytics

Given $n$ features $\mathbf{x} = [x_1, x_2, ..., x_n]^T$, the predicted output is:
$$\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i x_i + b$$

The model is trained by minimizing the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)** loss:
$$L_{MSE} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{j=1}^{m} \left( y_j - \hat{y}_j \right)^2$$

#### 2.3.2 Anomaly Detection using Z-Score

For a sliding window of $W$ samples with mean $\mu_W$ and standard deviation $\sigma_W$, a new sample $x_{new}$ is flagged as anomalous if:
$$Z = \frac{\vert x_{new} - \mu_W \vert}{\sigma_W} > Z_{threshold}$$

The standard threshold $Z_{threshold} = 3$ captures **99.7%** of normally distributed data (3-sigma rule).

#### 2.3.3 Time-Series Forecasting (ARIMA family)

A non-seasonal **ARIMA(p, d, q)** model for a time series $y_t$:
$$y_t' = c + \phi_1 y_{t-1}' + ... + \phi_p y_{t-p}' + \theta_1 \epsilon_{t-1} + ... + \theta_q \epsilon_{t-q} + \epsilon_t$$
where $y_t'$ is the $d$-th differenced series, $\phi_i$ are autoregressive coefficients, $\theta_j$ are moving-average coefficients, and $\epsilon_t$ is white noise.

#### 2.3.4 Data Reduction Ratio (DRR)

If $B_{in}$ bytes of raw data enter the fog pipeline and $B_{out}$ bytes of refined insights exit:
$$DRR = \frac{B_{in} - B_{out}}{B_{in}} = 1 - \frac{B_{out}}{B_{in}}$$

A DRR of **0.95** means **95%** bandwidth savings — a hallmark achievement of well-designed fog analytics.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the following table. Direct 2-mark questions in KTU frequently test formula recall.

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | Sensor data acquisition |
| 2 | Exponential Moving Avg | $EMA_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha) EMA_{t-1}$ | unitless | Real-time smoothing |
| 3 | Linear Regression Predict | $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ | varies | Predictive analytics |
| 4 | MSE Loss | $L = \frac{1}{m} \sum (y_j - \hat{y}_j)^2$ | varies² | Model training |
| 5 | Z-Score Anomaly | $Z = \vert x - \mu \vert / \sigma$ | unitless | Outlier detection |
| 6 | Data Reduction Ratio | $DRR = 1 - B_{out}/B_{in}$ | dimensionless | Bandwidth savings |
| 7 | End-to-End Latency | $L = T_{sense} + T_{proc} + T_{queue} + T_{trans}$ | ms | QoS analysis |
| 8 | Throughput | $T = N_{events} / \Delta t$ | EPS | Capacity planning |
| 9 | Fog Energy/Inference | $E_{pi} = P_{node} \cdot T_{proc} / N_{decisions}$ | J/dec | Green computing |
| 10 | Gossip Convergence | $T_{conv} = O(\log N)$ rounds | rounds | Distributed analytics |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)**: Fog analytics on a Siemens factory floor detects spindle bearing degradation **3 weeks** before failure, avoiding **₹50 lakhs** in downtime.
- **Autonomous Vehicles**: A self-driving car's fog layer (an on-board edge computer) runs object-detection CNN inference in **30–50 ms**, well within the **100 ms** safety budget.
- **Smart Grid**: Substation fog nodes forecast 15-minute-ahead load using ARIMA, enabling dynamic demand response and reducing peak demand by **8–12%**.
- **Precision Agriculture**: Field-deployed fog nodes analyse soil-moisture, weather, and crop-spectral data to trigger micro-irrigation, reducing water use by **30%**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total End-to-End Latency of a Fog Analytics Pipeline

**Statement:** Derive the total end-to-end latency experienced by a sensor event from the moment it is generated to the moment an analytics-driven actuation decision is delivered.

**Given Information:**
- Sensor sampling and digitization time: $T_{sense}$
- Edge pre-processing time: $T_{pre}$
- Fog node queue wait time: $T_{queue}$
- Fog node analytics computation time: $T_{proc}$
- Actuator command transmission time: $T_{trans}$

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1 — The event is generated at the physical sensor at $t = 0$.
Step 2 — The sensor's analog front-end samples and digitizes the signal. This takes $T_{sense}$.
Step 3 — The digitized value passes to the edge pre-processor (filtering, normalization) taking $T_{pre}$.
Step 4 — The refined datum is enqueued at the fog node. Due to multi-tenancy, it waits $T_{queue}$.
Step 5 — The analytics engine (e.g., CNN inference) processes the datum in time $T_{proc}$.
Step 6 — The decision is serialized, packetized, and transmitted to the actuator in time $T_{trans}$.
Step 7 — Summing all serial delays (the pipeline is sequential, not parallel, for a single event):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{E2E} &= T_{sense} + T_{pre} + T_{queue} + T_{proc} + T_{trans} \\
&= T_{sense} + T_{pre} + T_{queue} + T_{proc} + T_{trans}
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 8 — The fog-optimized variant *eliminates* $T_{trans}$ to cloud by keeping analytics local. Compared to a cloud pipeline with additional WAN round-trip $T_{WAN}$:
$$
\Delta L = T_{WAN,RT} \quad \text{(Round-trip WAN latency to cloud)}
$$

**Result:** Fog analytics saves approximately the WAN round-trip time, which for an India-to-AWS-US-East deployment is **≈ 220 ms**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Data Reduction Ratio for a Windowed Aggregation

**Statement:** A vibration sensor on a wind-turbine gearbox samples at $f_s = 1000 \text{ Hz}$, producing 4-byte floating-point values. The fog node computes a 1-second **statistical aggregate** (mean, RMS, peak, kurtosis) and transmits only those 4 numbers every second. Compute the DRR.

**Given Information:**
- Sampling frequency: $f_s = 1000 \text{ Hz}$
- Bytes per sample: $s = 4 \text{ bytes}$
- Aggregation window: $T_w = 1 \text{ s}$
- Aggregated features: $N_f = 4$ (mean, RMS, peak, kurtosis), each 4 bytes

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1 — Compute raw data volume per window:
$$
B_{in} = f_s \cdot s \cdot T_w = 1000 \cdot 4 \cdot 1 = 4000 \text{ bytes/second}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the processed insight volume per window:
$$
B_{out} = N_f \cdot s = 4 \cdot 4 = 16 \text{ bytes/second}
$$

Step 3 — Apply the DRR formula:
$$
\begin{aligned}
DRR &= 1 - \frac{B_{out}}{B_{in}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{16}{4000} \\
&= 1 - 0.004 \\
&= 0.996
\end{aligned}
$$

Step 4 — Interpretation: **99.6%** of the data is filtered out. Only **0.4%** reaches the cloud.

**Result:** $DRR = 0.996$ — i.e., **996 bytes saved per 1000 raw bytes**, a 250× reduction.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Optimizing EMA Smoothing Constant $\alpha$ for Trade-off

**Statement:** Show how the smoothing constant $\alpha$ in the Exponential Moving Average trades off between **noise reduction** and **event-detection lag**.

**Given Information:**
- $EMA_t = \alpha x_t + (1 - \alpha) EMA_{t-1}$
- A step change of magnitude $\Delta$ occurs at time $t = 0$.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Step 1 — Assume $EMA_{-1} = 0$ (steady state before event).
Step 2 — At $t = 1$, the input is $x_1 = \Delta$. Thus:
$$
EMA_1 = \alpha \Delta + (1-\alpha) \cdot 0 = \alpha \Delta
$$

Step 3 — At $t = 2$:
$$
EMA_2 = \alpha \Delta + (1-\alpha) \cdot \alpha \Delta = \alpha \Delta \left[1 + (1-\alpha)\right]
$$

Step 4 — Generalizing, by induction, after $k$ steps:
$$
EMA_k = \alpha \Delta \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} (1-\alpha)^i = \Delta \left[ 1 - (1-\alpha)^k \right]
$$

Step 5 — The time to reach **90%** of the true step value $\Delta$ satisfies:
$$
0.9 = 1 - (1-\alpha)^k \implies k = \frac{\ln(0.1)}{\ln(1-\alpha)} = \frac{-2.3026}{\ln(1-\alpha)}
$$

Step 6 — For $\alpha = 0.1$ (very smooth): $k = \frac{-2.3026}{\ln(0.9)} = \frac{-2.3026}{-0.1054} \approx 21.85$ samples.
For $\alpha = 0.5$ (responsive): $k = \frac{-2.3026}{\ln(0.5)} \approx 3.32$ samples.

**Result:** Lower $\alpha$ → smoother but slower; higher $\alpha$ → noisier but faster. The **trade-off** is fundamental to fog analytics design.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Analytics Placement Strategies

| Placement | Latency | Bandwidth | Privacy | Compute Power | Cost | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure Cloud** | Very High | Very High | Low | Unlimited | Low per-GB | Long-term reports |
| **Pure Edge** | Very Low | Very Low | Very High | Limited | High per-node | Hard real-time |
| **Fog (Hierarchical)** | Low | Low | High | Moderate | Balanced | **Industrial IoT** |
| **Edge + Cloud Hybrid** | Variable | Medium | Medium | Distributed | Optimized | Smart city |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A factory floor has **500 sensors** streaming data. Each sensor produces **2 KB/s**. The raw data is sent to a fog node that performs windowed aggregation with a window of **$T_w = 5$ s** and outputs **8 features of 8 bytes each** per window. Compute:
> (a) Total raw data rate entering the fog.
> (b) Total processed data rate leaving the fog.
> (c) The Data Reduction Ratio.
> (d) The bandwidth saved in 1 hour of operation.

**Solution:**

Part (a):
$$
B_{in} = 500 \cdot 2 \text{ KB/s} = 1000 \text{ KB/s} = 1 \text{ MB/s}
$$

Part (b): Each window produces $8 \cdot 8 = 64$ bytes. Per sensor: $64/5 = 12.8$ bytes/s. For 500 sensors:
$$
B_{out} = 500 \cdot 12.8 = 6400 \text{ bytes/s} = 6.25 \text{ KB/s}
$$

Part (c):
$$
DRR = 1 - \frac{6.25}{1000} \approx 0.99375 \quad (\text{i.e., } 99.375\%)
$$

Part (d): Raw in 1 hour = $1 \text{ MB/s} \cdot 3600 \text{ s} = 3600 \text{ MB} = 3.6 \text{ GB}$. Out = $6.25 \text{ KB/s} \cdot 3600 = 22.5 \text{ MB}$. Bandwidth saved = $3600 - 22.5 = 3577.5 \text{ MB} \approx 3.58 \text{ GB}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Five-Stage Fog Analytics Pipeline (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Sensors and Actuators] --> B[Stage 1: Data Acquisition Layer]
    B --> C[Stage 2: Edge Pre-Processing and Filtering]
    C --> D[Stage 3: Fog Node Local Analytics Engine]
    D --> E[Stage 4: Distributed Fog-to-Fog Collaboration]
    E --> F[Stage 5: Cloud Insight Storage and Long Term Training]
    F --> G[Updated ML Models Pushed Back to Fog]
    G --> D
    D --> H[Actuator Control Signal within Latency Budget]
    A --> H
```

**Explanation of the Block Flow:**

- The **bottom-left rectangle A** represents the physical world — temperature sensors, vibration sensors, cameras, etc.
- The **rectangle B (Data Acquisition)** handles signal conditioning and Analog-to-Digital conversion.
- The **rectangle C (Pre-Processing)** performs filtering, feature extraction, and noise reduction.
- The **rectangle D (Local Analytics Engine)** is the *heart* of fog analytics — running inference, CEP, and decision logic.
- The **rectangle E (Distributed Collaboration)** allows multiple fog nodes to share partial results.
- The **rectangle F (Cloud)** stores long-term insights and retrains ML models.
- The **arrow G** shows the *feedback loop* — updated models pushed back to fog.
- The **rectangle H (Actuator)** receives the final low-latency control signal.

---

### 4.2 Decision Flow for Analytics Type Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[New Fog Analytics Task Arrives] --> Q{Is the decision needed within 100 ms?}
    Q -- Yes --> R[Stage 3 Local Analytics: Descriptive plus Predictive]
    Q -- No --> S[Stage 4 Distributed Analytics with Fog to Fog]
    S --> T[Stage 5 Cloud Long Term Analytics]
    R --> U{Is the data an obvious outlier?}
    U -- Yes --> V[Trigger Immediate Actuator and Alert]
    U -- No --> W[Continue Streaming Analytics Loop]
    T --> X[Generate Diagnostic and Prescriptive Reports]
    X --> Y[Send Recommendations to Operators]
```

**Explanation:**

- The **top decision diamond Q** is the latency classification.
- **Path R → U** handles real-time decisions and anomaly detection.
- **Path S → T → X → Y** handles the long-term, distributed, multi-node analytics path.

---

### 4.3 Fog-to-Fog Distributed Analytics Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EdgeTier[Edge Tier L0]
        E1[Sensor Cluster 1]
        E2[Sensor Cluster 2]
        E3[Sensor Cluster 3]
    end
    subgraph FogTier1[Fog Tier L1 Gateway]
        F1[Fog Node A]
        F2[Fog Node B]
    end
    subgraph FogTier2[Fog Tier L2 Micro Data Centre]
        C1[Regional Fog Aggregator]
    end
    subgraph CloudTier[Cloud Tier]
        CL[Cloud Long Term Storage and Training]
    end
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    E3 --> F2
    F1 -- Peer Gossip Protocol --> F2
    F1 --> C1
    F2 --> C1
    C1 --> CL
    CL -- Updated ML Weights --> F1
    CL -- Updated ML Weights --> F2
```

**Explanation of the Topology:**

- **Edge Tier L0**: Three sensor clusters feed two distinct fog gateways.
- **Fog Tier L1**: Two fog nodes perform local analytics and *gossip* partial results to each other.
- **Fog Tier L2**: A regional aggregator combines partial models (e.g., federated learning averaging).
- **Cloud Tier**: Long-term storage, model retraining, and **push-back of updated weights** to L1.

---

### 4.4 Analytics Workload Distribution Heatmap (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Descriptive 60 percent on Fog] --> WX[Workload]
    W2[Diagnostic 15 percent on Fog] --> WX
    W3[Predictive 20 percent split between Fog and Cloud] --> WX
    W4[Prescriptive 5 percent on Cloud] --> WX
```

This conceptual diagram highlights that **descriptive analytics** is the dominant workload in fog nodes (cheap to compute, frequently needed), while **prescriptive analytics** is the rarest and most cloud-dependent.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between the four levels of data analytics. Give one fog-computing application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The four levels are:

- **Descriptive Analytics** — Answers *what happened?* It summarizes historical/recent data using aggregations, KPIs, dashboards. **Fog application**: Computing real-time OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness) on a factory floor.
- **Diagnostic Analytics** — Answers *why did it happen?* It performs root-cause analysis using correlation and drill-down. **Fog application**: Identifying which sensor anomaly caused a production-line stoppage.
- **Predictive Analytics** — Answers *what will happen?* It uses ML models (regression, time-series) to forecast future values. **Fog application**: Forecasting 15-minute-ahead load on a smart-grid substation.
- **Prescriptive Analytics** — Answers *what should we do?* It uses optimization and reinforcement learning to recommend actions. **Fog application**: Dynamic traffic-signal timing recommendations for a smart-city intersection.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Data Reduction Ratio (DRR). How is fog analytics useful in reducing network bandwidth consumption?**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Data Reduction Ratio is the fraction of raw input data eliminated by the fog analytics pipeline:
$$
DRR = 1 - \frac{B_{out}}{B_{in}}
$$
where $B_{in}$ is the bytes of raw data entering the fog node and $B_{out}$ is the bytes of refined insights transmitted to the cloud.

**Bandwidth Utility:** Fog analytics executes **filtering, windowed aggregation, feature extraction, and ML inference** locally at the gateway. Instead of transmitting every raw sensor sample to the cloud, only statistically summarized or anomaly-flagged data is sent. This typically achieves DRR values of **0.9 to 0.99**, reducing WAN bandwidth requirements by **1–2 orders of magnitude** and lowering cloud storage costs.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### 5.2.1 Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, ESE Module 4, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** Explain the architecture of data analytics in fog computing with a neat block diagram. Describe in detail the five sequential stages of the fog analytics pipeline. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A wind-farm consists of **40 turbines**. Each turbine has **8 vibration sensors** sampling at **$f_s = 2 \text{ kHz}$** with **4-byte** float values. The fog gateway performs windowed feature extraction with **$T_w = 2 \text{ s}$** and outputs **12 features of 8 bytes each** per window per sensor.
>   (i) Compute the total raw data rate at the fog gateway. **(2 Marks)**
>   (ii) Compute the processed data rate. **(2 Marks)**
>   (iii) Compute the Data Reduction Ratio. **(2 Marks)**
>   (iv) How much bandwidth (in MB) is saved in 24 hours? **(1 Mark)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Architecture of Fog Analytics:**

The fog analytics architecture is a **hierarchical, multi-tiered pipeline** that distributes analytical workloads across edge, fog, and cloud layers. The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1) shows the following five sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Data Acquisition (Edge Layer):** Sensors sample physical phenomena (vibration, temperature, image, etc.) at a rate determined by the Nyquist criterion. Analog signals are digitized using ADCs. For example, a 25 kHz vibration signal requires $f_s \geq 50$ kHz.

**Stage 2 — Edge Pre-Processing & Filtering:** Raw digitized data is filtered to remove noise. Common filters include Moving Average, Median Filter, Kalman Filter, and Exponential Moving Average. Feature extraction (e.g., RMS, kurtosis, FFT coefficients) is performed to reduce dimensionality.

**Stage 3 — Fog Node Local Analytics Engine:** This is the heart of fog analytics. The fog node runs:
- Real-time ML inference (e.g., a trained Random Forest or CNN).
- Complex Event Processing (CEP) for pattern detection across streams.
- Windowed aggregation (tumbling, sliding windows).
- Anomaly detection using Z-score, Isolation Forest, or autoencoders.

**Stage 4 — Distributed Fog-to-Fog Collaboration:** Multiple fog nodes exchange partial results using gossip protocols, federated learning, or MapReduce-over-fog. This enables a *global* view without raw data centralization.

**Stage 5 — Cloud Insight Storage and Long-Term Training:** Only refined insights are pushed to the cloud. The cloud performs long-term historical analysis, retrains ML models, and pushes updated model parameters back to the fog, closing the loop.

**Advantages:** Latency reduction (eliminating WAN round-trip), bandwidth savings (DRR 0.9–0.99), privacy preservation (data never leaves local network), and resilience (offline operation during WAN outages).

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- Number of turbines: $N_T = 40$
- Sensors per turbine: $N_S = 8$
- Sampling frequency: $f_s = 2000 \text{ Hz}$
- Bytes per sample: $s = 4 \text{ bytes}$
- Window duration: $T_w = 2 \text{ s}$
- Features per window per sensor: $N_f = 12$
- Bytes per feature: $b = 8 \text{ bytes}$

**(i) Total raw data rate:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{in} &= N_T \cdot N_S \cdot f_s \cdot s \\
&= 40 \cdot 8 \cdot 2000 \cdot 4 \\
&= 2{,}560{,}000 \text{ bytes/s} \\
&= 2.56 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total processed data rate:**
Samples per window per sensor = $f_s \cdot T_w = 2000 \cdot 2 = 4000$. Bytes out per window per sensor = $N_f \cdot b = 12 \cdot 8 = 96$ bytes. Windows per second = $1/T_w = 0.5$. Bytes per second per sensor = $96 \cdot 0.5 = 48$ bytes/s.

$$
B_{out} = N_T \cdot N_S \cdot 48 = 40 \cdot 8 \cdot 48 = 15{,}360 \text{ bytes/s} \approx 15 \text{ KB/s}
$$

**(iii) Data Reduction Ratio:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
DRR &= 1 - \frac{B_{out}}{B_{in}} \\
&= 1 - \frac{15{,}360}{2{,}560{,}000} \\
&= 1 - 0.006 \\
&= 0.994
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Bandwidth saved in 24 hours:**
Raw volume in 24 h = $2.56 \text{ MB/s} \cdot 86400 \text{ s} = 221{,}184 \text{ MB}$. Processed volume = $15{,}360 \text{ bytes/s} \cdot 86400 \text{ s} = 1{,}327{,}104{,}000 \text{ bytes} \approx 1327 \text{ MB}$. Saved = $221{,}184 - 1327 = 219{,}857 \text{ MB} \approx 219.86 \text{ GB}$.

---

### 5.2.2 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, ESE Module 4]**

> **(a)** Discuss the various types of data analytics (descriptive, diagnostic, predictive, prescriptive) with suitable fog-computing examples. Explain how fog computing enables real-time predictive analytics. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A predictive maintenance system uses an **Exponential Moving Average (EMA)** with smoothing constant **$\alpha = 0.2$** to smooth vibration data. The current value is **$x_t = 8.5$** and the previous EMA value is **$EMA_{t-1} = 7.0$**.
>   (i) Compute the new EMA value. **(2 Marks)**
>   (ii) If a true step change of magnitude **$\Delta = 10$** occurs, how many samples $k$ are required for the EMA to reach 95% of the true value? **(3 Marks)**
>   (iii) If the same system uses $\alpha = 0.8$ instead, recalculate $k$ for 95% convergence and comment on the trade-off. **(2 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Types of Analytics:**

(Refer to Q1 model answer for detailed definitions; expand with fog examples.)

**Real-Time Predictive Analytics in Fog:**
Fog nodes run **pre-trained ML models** (e.g., LSTM, Random Forest) locally on streaming data. The pipeline:
1. Sensor data is windowed (e.g., 1-second sliding window).
2. Features (mean, RMS, spectral entropy) are computed at the edge.
3. Features are fed to the fog-located ML model for inference.
4. Predictions (e.g., "bearing failure in 14 days") are generated within **< 50 ms**.
5. Alerts are sent to operators/actuators in real time.
6. The cloud periodically retrains the model and pushes updated weights back to the fog.

This enables **closed-loop predictive control** that is impossible to achieve with cloud-only analytics due to WAN latency.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Compute new EMA:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
EMA_t &= \alpha \cdot x_t + (1 - \alpha) \cdot EMA_{t-1} \\
&= 0.2 \cdot 8.5 + 0.8 \cdot 7.0 \\
&= 1.7 + 5.6 \\
&= 7.3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Number of samples to reach 95% with $\alpha = 0.2$:**
The convergence formula (derived in Section 3.3):
$$
k = \frac{\ln(1 - 0.95)}{\ln(1 - \alpha)} = \frac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.8)} = \frac{-2.9957}{-0.2231} \approx 13.43
$$
So $k \approx 14$ samples (rounded up).

**(iii) Recalculate with $\alpha = 0.8$:**
$$
k = \frac{\ln(0.05)}{\ln(0.2)} = \frac{-2.9957}{-1.6094} \approx 1.86
$$
So $k \approx 2$ samples.

**Trade-off comment:** With $\alpha = 0.8$, the EMA converges 7× faster to true value, making it more *responsive* to real events, but it is also *more sensitive to noise*. With $\alpha = 0.2$, the EMA is smoother and noise-resistant but lags real changes by 14 samples. The choice depends on whether the application prioritizes **noise immunity** ($\alpha$ low) or **event detection speed** ($\alpha$ high).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Edge Analytics with Fog Analytics**: Edge analytics runs *only* on the device; fog analytics uses an *intermediate hierarchical layer*. Examiners *will* deduct marks if you use these terms interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Forgetting Units**: Always write units (ms, KB/s, J/inference) in numerical answers. The KTU marking scheme awards 0.5 marks for correct units in numerical problems.
>
> 3. **Omitting the Block Diagram**: Part (a) of 14-mark questions typically carries 7 marks. A neat, labelled block diagram is worth **at least 2–3 marks**. Never skip it.
>
> 4. **Mishandling DRR Numerator/Denominator**: Many students write $DRR = B_{out}/B_{in}$, which is **incorrect**. The correct form is $DRR = 1 - B_{out}/B_{in}$. This is a very common trap.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Cloud Feedback Loop**: When asked about fog analytics architecture, *always* mention the model-retraining-and-push-back loop from cloud to fog. Examiners look for this explicitly.
>
> 6. **Not Labelling Axes/Variables**: In derivation problems, define every variable ($f_s$, $\alpha$, $w_i$, etc.) *before* plugging into equations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Data Analytics in the Fog:**
>
> - **Definition:** Fog analytics is the execution of data-mining, statistical, and ML inference operations at the intermediate layer between edge and cloud.
> - **Four Analytics Types:** Descriptive, Diagnostic, Predictive, Prescriptive — remember the question each answers (*What? Why? Will? Should?*).
> - **Five Pipeline Stages:** Acquisition → Pre-Processing → Local Analytics → Distributed Collaboration → Cloud Storage/Retraining.
> - **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — foundational for sensor data acquisition.
> - **EMA Formula:** $EMA_t = \alpha x_t + (1-\alpha) EMA_{t-1}$; convergence time $k = \ln(1-p)/\ln(1-\alpha)$ where $p$ is the target fraction.
> - **Z-Score Anomaly:** Threshold typically **3**; captures **99.7%** of normal data.
> - **DRR Formula:** $DRR = 1 - B_{out}/B_{in}$; typical fog values **0.9–0.99**.
> - **Latency Formula:** $L_{E2E} = T_{sense} + T_{pre} + T_{queue} + T_{proc} + T_{trans}$.
> - **Trade-offs:** Noise smoothing vs. detection lag (governed by $\alpha$); local compute vs. cloud compute (governed by latency budget).
> - **Privacy Drivers:** GDPR, HIPAA, DPDP Act 2023 all favour local fog processing.
> - **Real-time KPI:** Fog analytics targets sub-**100 ms** decision latency; cloud-only pipelines exceed **200 ms** WAN round-trip.
> - **Feedback Loop:** Always close the loop — cloud retrains, fog updates weights, edge benefits.
> - **Standard metrics to memorize:** Latency, Jitter, DRR, Throughput, Accuracy Retention, Energy per Inference.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
