topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Fog and Edge Computing**, the term **Architecture** refers to the **structured, layered blueprint** that defines how computational, storage, networking, and communication resources are hierarchically organized, distributed, and orchestrated across the continuum spanning **end devices (Edge)**, **intermediate fog nodes (Fog layer)**, and **centralized hyperscale infrastructure (Cloud)**. It specifies the **physical topology**, the **logical component interactions**, the **data flow pathways**, the **service orchestration mechanisms**, and the **interface protocols** that collectively enable low-latency, context-aware, geographically distributed computing at the periphery of the network.

The architecture is typically modeled as a **multi-tier (3-tier or N-tier) framework** with clearly defined responsibilities at each tier:
- **Tier 0 — Edge / End Device Layer** (Sensors, actuators, IoT devices, smartphones)
- **Tier 1 — Fog / Gateway Layer** (Fog nodes, micro-data centers, gateways, routers with compute capability)
- **Tier 2 — Cloud / Enterprise Layer** (Central data centers, hyperscale clouds)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **Module 4 – Architecture** of PECIT867, which expects the student to understand the structural blueprint of fog/edge ecosystems, the role of each architectural layer, the **OpenFog Reference Architecture (FRA)** pillars, and how latency-sensitive workloads are distributed across the edge-fog-cloud continuum.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Human Reflex vs. Conscious Thinking**
>
> Imagine your nervous system.
>
> 1. **Edge** is your **sensory nerve ending** in the fingertip that touches a hot stove. It detects the danger *instantly* and triggers a reflex arc — there is **zero round-trip to the brain**.
> 2. **Fog** is your **spinal cord**. It receives the signal, processes it locally, and sends a quick motor command back to your hand to pull away — fast, but slightly less immediate than a pure reflex.
> 3. **Cloud** is your **brain**. It gets the information *later*, decides that "this is dangerous, I should remember to be careful with hot stoves," and stores the long-term lesson.
>
> **Edge** handles the sub-millisecond reaction. **Fog** handles the intelligent local coordination. **Cloud** handles the heavy, slow, archival analysis. The architecture of fog/edge computing is exactly this — distributing cognition along the path between the data source and the distant data center, with the **right amount of intelligence at the right distance**.

## 1.3 Core Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **End Device (ED)** | Resource-constrained device generating or consuming data (latency: $\approx$ **sub-ms to 1 ms**) |
| **Edge Node** | Compute resource co-located with or near the data source (latency: $\approx$ **1–10 ms**) |
| **Fog Node** | Intermediate compute/storage node between edge and cloud (latency: $\approx$ **5–20 ms**) |
| **Cloud Data Center** | Hyperscale centralized facility (latency: $\approx$ **50–200 ms** WAN) |
| **Orchestrator** | Software entity that manages resource placement, scaling, and lifecycle across tiers |
| **Virtualization / Containerization** | Technique to abstract compute (VMs, containers) on fog/edge nodes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule of Fog/Edge Architecture:** *Push computation as close to the data source as the application's latency, privacy, and bandwidth budget permits — and no closer.*

## 1.4 The Architectural Imperative

Traditional cloud-centric architecture suffers from three fundamental deficits addressed by the fog/edge architecture:

1. **Latency Violation** — Autonomous vehicles require end-to-end response times under **10 ms**; a cloud round-trip over WAN ($\approx$ **50–200 ms**) is fatal.
2. **Bandwidth Saturation** — A single autonomous car produces $\approx$ **4 TB/day**; streaming all raw data to the cloud is economically and physically infeasible.
3. **Privacy & Sovereignty** — GDPR, HIPAA, and India's DPDP Act require data to remain within geographic / jurisdictional boundaries, which centralized clouds often violate.

The **Architecture** module provides the structural solution to these three problems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Reference Architecture

The canonical fog/edge architecture is decomposed into **three logical tiers**. Each tier has well-defined responsibilities, ownership, and Quality-of-Service (QoS) expectations.

### Tier 0 — The Edge / Perception Layer

- **Devices:** Sensors, RFID tags, cameras, actuators, wearables, smartphones, industrial PLCs, smart meters.
- **Compute capability:** Often minimal (microcontrollers, 8-bit MCUs, low-power SoCs like ESP32, ARM Cortex-M0/M4).
- **Primary functions:**
  - Data acquisition (sampling the physical world)
  - Preliminary filtering and thresholding
  - Local actuator control (closing loops locally)
  - Secure identity attestation
- **Networking:** BLE, Zigbee, LoRa, 6LoWPAN, NB-IoT, Wi-Fi HaLow.
- **Why it exists:** Eliminates a network hop for time-critical reactions. Latency: **< 1 ms**.

### Tier 1 — The Fog / Intermediate Layer

This is the **heart of the architecture** and the most architecturally interesting tier. It includes:

- **Fog Nodes:** Industrial PCs, edge gateways, micro data centers, cellular base stations with MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing) capability, smart routers.
- **Compute capability:** Moderate (multi-core ARM, x86, GPUs for inference).
- **Primary functions:**
  - Stream processing and aggregation
  - Local analytics, ML inference (e.g., running a quantized CNN on a Jetson Nano)
  - Stateful coordination among nearby edge devices
  - Protocol translation (MQTT ↔ HTTP, CoAP ↔ AMQP)
  - Transient storage and caching
  - Local decision-making and actuation
- **Networking:** 4G/5G, Wi-Fi 6, wired Ethernet, time-sensitive networking (TSN).
- **Latency budget:** **1 – 20 ms** (one hop from the device).

### Tier 2 — The Cloud / Enterprise Layer

- **Infrastructure:** Hyperscale data centers (AWS, Azure, GCP, on-premise private clouds).
- **Compute capability:** Elastic, virtually unlimited (CPU, GPU, TPU, quantum in future).
- **Primary functions:**
  - Long-term archival storage (data lakes)
  - Heavy batch analytics and training of deep models
  - Global orchestration and policy enforcement
  - Cross-fog correlation and digital twin aggregation
- **Networking:** High-bandwidth fiber backbone, $\geq$ **10 Gbps**.
- **Latency budget:** **50 – 200 ms** (multi-hop WAN).

## 2.2 Architectural Dimensions (Cross-Cutting Concerns)

Beyond the three vertical tiers, the architecture must be evaluated along **eight horizontal dimensions** (the **OpenFog Consortium's eight pillars**, IEEE 1934 standard):

1. **Security** — Hardware root-of-trust, encrypted channels, zero-trust networking
2. **Scalability** — Horizontal addition of fog nodes without architectural change
3. **Openness** — Vendor-neutral interfaces (avoiding lock-in)
4. **Autonomy** — A fog node must operate when disconnected from cloud
5. **RAS** (Reliability, Availability, Serviceability) — Fault tolerance, redundancy
6. **Agility** — Rapid redeployment of services (closely tied to containerization)
7. **Heterogeneity** — Support for diverse hardware, OS, and protocols
8. **Hierarchy** — Multi-level nesting of fog domains (fog-of-fogs)

## 2.3 Service Orchestration and Placement Logic

The **most architecturally critical decision** is: *Given a service, where should it run?* The answer is governed by the following placement function:

> **Service Placement Rule (Intuition):** Place service $S_i$ at tier $T$ such that the joint objective $\mathcal{J}$ is minimized, where $\mathcal{J}$ is a weighted sum of latency, bandwidth, energy, and SLA violation cost.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table summarizes every quantitative relationship relevant to fog/edge architecture that a KTU 2024 student **must memorize** for the End Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | End-to-end latency (single-hop) | $L_{e2e} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans} + L_{prop}$ | **ms** | Total response time budget |
| 2 | Round-Trip Time (WAN cloud) | $RTT_{cloud} \approx 2 \cdot \frac{d_{WAN}}{v_{fiber}} + 2 \cdot t_{proc}$ | **ms** | $\approx$ **50–200 ms** for intercontinental |
| 3 | Speed of light in fiber | $v_{fiber} \approx 2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$ | **m/s** | Fundamental physical limit |
| 4 | Data reduction ratio at edge | $R_{edge} = \frac{D_{in} - D_{out}}{D_{in}} \times 100\%$ | **%** | Bandwidth saved by edge filtering |
| 5 | Energy per bit (transmission) | $E_{tx}(d) = E_{elec} + \epsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ | **J/bit** | $n = 2$ (free space), $n \in [3,4]$ (multipath) |
| 6 | Aggregate bandwidth (parallel) | $B_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_i$ | **Mbps / Gbps** | Sum of edge link capacities |
| 7 | Service placement cost (general) | $\mathcal{C} = w_1 L + w_2 E + w_3 B + w_4 V_{SLA}$ | dimensionless | Weighted objective for orchestrator |
| 8 | Fog node capacity (compute) | $C_{node} = n_{vCPU} \times f_{CPU} \times \eta_{util}$ | **vCPU-cycles/s** | Effective compute envelope |
| 9 | Throughput (Little's Law) | $\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Requests in system}}{\text{Avg. residence time}}$ | **req/s** | Architectural capacity check |
| 10 | Network distance – light travel | $d = v_{fiber} \cdot \frac{RTT}{2}$ | **km** | Max physical distance for a latency SLA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Note:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is **forbidden** inside markdown tables because it breaks the table syntax. In the table above, all absolute-value and conditional notations have been rewritten using contextual words (e.g., "d in [3,4]") to remain KTU-PDF safe.

## 2.5 Real-World Architectural Instances

- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** PLCs (Edge) → Shop-floor gateway (Fog) → MES/ERP cloud. Latency SLA: **< 1 ms** for safety interlocks.
- **Connected Vehicles (V2X):** Vehicle ECU (Edge) → Roadside Unit / MEC (Fog) → Traffic cloud. Latency SLA: **< 10 ms** for collision avoidance.
- **Smart Grid:** Smart meter (Edge) → Substation concentrator (Fog) → Utility cloud. Bandwidth reduction: **$\approx$ 90%** via edge aggregation.
- **Healthcare Telemetry:** Wearable biosensor (Edge) → Hospital fog server (Fog) → Hospital cloud. Privacy: PHI must never leave the fog tier.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fundamental Latency Budget of an Architectural Tier

The end-to-end latency experienced by a single request as it propagates from a sensor through a chosen architectural tier is the **sum of four orthogonal time penalties**. We derive each term in detail.

**Step 1: Identify the four latency components.**
A request travelling from source to processing site and back incurs:
- $L_{proc}$ — processing time on the compute node
- $L_{queue}$ — waiting time in the scheduler's ready queue
- $L_{trans}$ — serialization and transmission time on the link
- $L_{prop}$ — propagation delay across the physical medium

**Step 2: Express the processing latency.**
If a job of $W$ instructions runs on a processor of $f$ cycles/second and the IPC (instructions per cycle) is $k$:

$$L_{proc} = \frac{W}{k \cdot f}$$

**Step 3: Express the queuing latency.**
By the **M/M/1 queueing model**, when arrival rate is $\lambda$ (req/s) and service rate is $\mu$ (req/s), the average waiting time in the queue is:

$$L_{queue} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} \quad \text{for } \rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} < 1$$

**Step 4: Express the transmission latency.**
For a payload of $P$ bits on a link of bandwidth $B$ bits/s:

$$L_{trans} = \frac{P}{B}$$

**Step 5: Express the propagation latency.**
For a physical link of length $d$ meters through a medium of propagation speed $v$ m/s:

$$L_{prop} = \frac{d}{v}$$

**Step 6: Compose the end-to-end latency.**

$$L_{e2e} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans} + L_{prop}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $L_{trans}$ and $L_{prop}$ are **governed by different physics**. $L_{trans}$ is reduced by upgrading bandwidth $B$. $L_{prop}$ is reduced by **physically shortening the distance $d$** — which is the *entire architectural motivation* for fog and edge computing. You cannot defeat the speed of light with bandwidth; you must defeat it with **proximity**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Why Cloud-Only Architecture Fails Real-Time Workloads

A key KTU-style analytical question is to prove **quantitatively** that a cloud-only architecture violates a tight latency SLA.

**Step 1: Set up a representative problem.**
A connected car at the outskirts of Bengaluru must send a perception snapshot (size $P = 250$ KB) to a cloud data centre in Virginia, USA, and receive a braking decision.

**Step 2: Compute the propagation latency.**

$$L_{prop} = \frac{d_{WAN}}{v_{fiber}} = \frac{13{,}000 \text{ km}}{2 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}} = \frac{1.3 \times 10^{7}}{2 \times 10^{8}} \text{ s}$$

$$L_{prop} = 6.5 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 65 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3: Compute the round-trip propagation latency.**

$$L_{prop, RTT} = 2 \times 65 \text{ ms} = 130 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4: Compute the transmission latency on a 100 Mbps uplink.**

$$L_{trans, up} = \frac{250 \times 1024 \times 8 \text{ bits}}{100 \times 10^{6} \text{ bits/s}} = 20.48 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5: Compute the transmission latency on a 1 Gbps downlink.**

$$L_{trans, down} = \frac{20 \text{ KB (decision)}}{10^{9}} = 0.16 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 6: Sum all contributions.**

$$L_{e2e} = 130 + 20.48 + 0.16 + L_{proc} \approx 150.64 \text{ ms} + L_{proc}$$

**Step 7: Compare to the SLA.**

The autonomous driving SLA is typically **$L_{SLA} \leq 10$ ms**. We have **$\approx$ 150 ms** which is **15$\times$ over budget**. Conclusion: **A cloud-only architecture is mathematically infeasible** for this workload, *irrespective of how fast the cloud servers are*. Only edge/fog placement can satisfy the SLA.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Edge Data Reduction and Bandwidth Savings

**Step 1: Define the data reduction ratio $R_{edge}$.**

$$R_{edge} = \frac{D_{in} - D_{out}}{D_{in}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2: A CCTV camera generates $D_{in} = 4$ Mbps of raw H.264 video.**

**Step 3: Edge analytics detects that 90% of frames contain no event of interest, and only object-detection metadata is forwarded.**

$$D_{out} = 0.10 \times D_{in} = 0.4 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4: Compute the reduction.**

$$R_{edge} = \frac{4 - 0.4}{4} \times 100\% = 90\%$$

**Step 5: Compute the economic impact.**
For a fleet of $N = 10{,}000$ cameras streaming 24/7:

$$B_{saved} = N \times (D_{in} - D_{out}) = 10{,}000 \times 3.6 \text{ Mbps} = 36 \text{ Gbps}$$

At a transit cost of \$0.02/GB, **monthly savings exceed \$200,000** for a city-wide surveillance deployment — a strong architectural justification.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Architectural Tier Comparison

| Property | Edge (Tier 0) | Fog (Tier 1) | Cloud (Tier 2) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | **< 1 ms** | **1 – 20 ms** | **50 – 200 ms** |
| **Compute (relative)** | $10^{0}$ (1$\times$) | $10^{1}$ – $10^{2}$ | $10^{4}$ – $10^{6}$ |
| **Storage horizon** | Seconds – Minutes | Hours – Days | Months – Years |
| **Power budget** | mW – W | W – kW | MW (data centre) |
| **Connectivity dependence** | Often intermittent | Periodic | Always-on assumed |
| **Autonomy requirement** | Mandatory | Mandatory | Optional |
| **Cost per compute unit** | High | Moderate | Low |
| **Data volume handled** | kB – MB/s | MB – GB/s | GB – TB/s |
| **Failure impact** | Local | Regional | Global |
| **Security perimeter** | Device-attested | Network-segmented | Perimeter + IAM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The numbers in this matrix are **typical engineering ranges** often cited in survey papers. In a KTU 14-mark question, reproducing the **trend** (Edge < Fog < Cloud latency and opposite for compute) with one representative value per cell earns full credit.

## 3.5 Analytical Reasoning — The Placement Decision Tree

A KTU 14-mark question frequently asks the student to **justify** why a given service is placed at a particular tier. The decision follows this hierarchy:

1. **Is the SLA latency < 10 ms?** → **Must be Edge**.
2. **Is the SLA latency 10–100 ms AND the workload stateful across multiple devices?** → **Fog**.
3. **Is the workload long-running, batch, or globally aggregating?** → **Cloud**.
4. **Is there a regulatory constraint on data sovereignty?** → **Place at the lowest tier that satisfies the constraint** (often Fog).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Three-Tier Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tier0["Tier 0 - Edge Perception Layer"]
        s1["Sensor: Temperature"]
        s2["Sensor: Camera"]
        s3["Actuator: Motor"]
        s4["Wearable: ECG"]
    end

    subgraph tier1["Tier 1 - Fog Intermediate Layer"]
        g1["Fog Node A: Industrial Gateway"]
        g2["Fog Node B: MEC Server"]
        g3["Fog Node C: Micro Data Center"]
        orch["Orchestrator: Placement Engine"]
    end

    subgraph tier2["Tier 2 - Cloud Enterprise Layer"]
        c1["Cloud Region: Analytics"]
        c2["Cloud Region: Model Training"]
        c3["Cloud Region: Long-term Storage"]
    end

    s1 -->|BLE| g1
    s2 -->|WiFi 6| g2
    s4 -->|5G| g3
    s3 <-->|TSN| g1
    g1 -->|MQTT TLS| orch
    g2 -->|MQTT TLS| orch
    g3 -->|MQTT TLS| orch
    orch -->|Kafka| c1
    orch -->|S3 API| c3
    c1 -->|Feedback Model| orch
    c2 -->|Updated Weights| orch
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Tier 0 devices speak lightweight, low-power protocols (BLE, Wi-Fi, 5G) to the fog.
- The orchestrator is the **brain** of the architecture; it decides *where* each service runs.
- The cloud feeds back *refined models* to the fog, which is the canonical **edge–fog–cloud learning loop**.

## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture of a Single Fog Node

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph fn["Fog Node Internal Block Diagram"]
        ni["Network Ingress: NIC, 5G modem"]
        sec["Security Module: TPM, Secure Boot"]
        vt["Virtualization Layer: Hypervisor or Container Runtime"]
        sa["Service A: Stream Processor"]
        sb["Service B: ML Inference Engine"]
        sc["Service C: Protocol Translator"]
        sto["Local Storage: NVMe SSD"]
        mq["Message Queue: Kafka or MQTT Broker"]
        no["Network Egress: Switched uplink to cloud"]
    end

    ni --> sec
    sec --> vt
    vt --> sa
    vt --> sb
    vt --> sc
    sa <--> mq
    sb <--> mq
    sc <--> mq
    sa <--> sto
    sb <--> sto
    mq --> no
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every fog node contains a **secure ingress**, a **virtualization layer** that hosts multiple co-resident services, a **local message bus** for inter-service communication, a **persistent store** for state, and an **egress** toward the cloud. This block is the **atomic architectural unit** of the fog tier.

## 4.3 Architectural Data Flow — Request Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Dev as Edge Device
    participant Fg as Fog Node
    participant Orc as Orchestrator
    participant Cl as Cloud

    Dev->>Fg: 1. Publish telemetry (MQTT)
    Fg->>Fg: 2. Local inference and threshold check
    alt Anomaly detected
        Fg->>Dev: 3a. Trigger local actuator immediately
        Fg->>Orc: 3b. Notify anomaly metadata
    else Routine data
        Fg->>Cl: 4. Forward aggregated batch
    end
    Orc->>Cl: 5. Sync global model weights (periodic)
    Cl-->>Fg: 6. Push refined model
    Fg-->>Dev: 7. Update local configuration
```

**Reading the diagram:** This sequence captures the **bi-directional nature** of the architecture — the data flows *up* (telemetry, anomalies) while intelligence flows *down* (updated models, policies). This **closed loop** is the hallmark of a well-designed fog/edge architecture.

## 4.4 Architectural Style Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Pure Cloud-Centric"] -->|shift left| B["Cloud-Fog Hybrid"]
    B -->|shift left| C["Fog-Cloud Balanced"]
    C -->|shift left| D["Fog-Dominant"]
    D -->|shift left| E["Pure Edge"]
    A -.violates 10 ms SLA.-> A
    E -.violates heavy training.-> E
```

**Reading the diagram:** The architecture is a **spectrum**, not a binary choice. The optimal point depends on the workload mix — KTU 14-mark questions often ask the student to *locate a given use case* on this spectrum with justification.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECIT867]`

**Differentiate between the three architectural tiers (Edge, Fog, Cloud) of a fog computing system based on latency, compute capacity, and storage horizon.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Edge Tier | Fog Tier | Cloud Tier |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | **< 1 ms** | **1 – 20 ms** | **50 – 200 ms** |
| **Compute capacity** | Minimal (MCUs, low-power SoCs) | Moderate (multi-core, GPUs for inference) | Elastic (hyperscale CPU/GPU/TPU) |
| **Storage horizon** | Seconds to minutes | Hours to days | Months to years |
| **Connectivity** | Often intermittent | Periodic sync | Always-on |

The **Edge** tier sits closest to the data source and is optimised for sub-millisecond reactions. The **Fog** tier provides intermediate compute and acts as a stateful coordinator among nearby edge devices. The **Cloud** tier handles long-term archival, heavy training, and global aggregation. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECIT867]`

**What are the eight pillars of the OpenFog Reference Architecture (IEEE 1934)?**

**Model Answer:**
The OpenFog Consortium (now merged with the Industrial Internet Consortium) defined **eight pillars** as cross-cutting concerns that every compliant fog architecture must address:

1. **Security** — End-to-end protection of data, devices, and identities
2. **Scalability** — Ability to add fog nodes without architectural change
3. **Openness** — Vendor-neutral, standards-based interfaces
4. **Autonomy** — Operation during cloud disconnection
5. **RAS** (Reliability, Availability, Serviceability) — Fault tolerance and recovery
6. **Agility** — Rapid redeployment of services via containerisation
7. **Heterogeneity** — Support for diverse hardware, OS, and protocols
8. **Hierarchy** — Multi-level nesting of fog domains

**(3 marks — 0.375 per pillar, full credit for naming all eight)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECIT867]`

#### (a) **Explain in detail the three-tier architecture of fog computing with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each tier with at least one real-world example per tier.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
The three-tier fog computing architecture distributes intelligence across **Edge (Tier 0)**, **Fog (Tier 1)**, and **Cloud (Tier 2)**, with each tier owning a distinct portion of the latency–bandwidth–storage trade-off.

**2. Tier 0 — Edge / Perception Layer (2 marks):**
- **Devices:** Sensors, cameras, actuators, wearables, PLCs.
- **Role:** Acquire physical-world data, perform sub-millisecond threshold checks, and execute local actuator control without involving any other tier.
- **Example:** A **Cobot (collaborative robot) on a shop floor** that uses an on-board vision sensor to detect a human entering its safety zone and applies an emergency stop within **< 1 ms** — faster than any external round-trip could deliver.

**3. Tier 1 — Fog / Intermediate Layer (2 marks):**
- **Devices:** Industrial gateways, MEC servers, micro data centers, smart routers.
- **Role:** Aggregate streams from many edge devices, run ML inference, perform protocol translation, host stateful coordination, and forward only metadata to the cloud.
- **Example:** A **Roadside Unit (RSU) in a V2X intersection** that aggregates telemetry from 50 nearby vehicles, runs a collision-prediction LSTM, and broadcasts hazard warnings to the cluster in **< 10 ms**.

**4. Tier 2 — Cloud / Enterprise Layer (2 marks):**
- **Devices:** Hyperscale data centres, on-premise private clouds.
- **Role:** Long-term archival, model training, global orchestration, cross-region policy.
- **Example:** A **national traffic management cloud** that ingests aggregate data from thousands of RSUs, retrains the collision-prediction model nightly, and pushes the refined model back to the fog tier.

**5. Block Diagram — Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.** *(Reproducible in exam for full credit.)*

---

#### (b) **Derive the end-to-end latency equation for a fog-deployed service. Show numerically that a cloud-only deployment violates a 10 ms autonomous-driving SLA, while a fog deployment at 50 km distance satisfies it. Use $v_{fiber} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Write the end-to-end latency equation (1 mark).**

$$L_{e2e} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans} + L_{prop}$$

**Step 2: Express each term symbolically (1 mark).**
- $L_{proc} = \frac{W}{k \cdot f}$ (compute time)
- $L_{queue} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$ (M/M/1 waiting time)
- $L_{trans} = \frac{P}{B}$ (serialisation time)
- $L_{prop} = \frac{d}{v}$ (propagation delay)

**Step 3: Compute cloud-only path (2 marks).**
Distance Bengaluru → Virginia, USA: $d = 13{,}000$ km $= 1.3 \times 10^7$ m.

$$L_{prop, one-way} = \frac{1.3 \times 10^{7}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 0.065 \text{ s} = 65 \text{ ms}$$

$$L_{prop, RTT} = 2 \times 65 = 130 \text{ ms}$$

Adding $L_{trans} \approx 20$ ms, $L_{proc} \approx 5$ ms, $L_{queue} \approx 1$ ms:

$$L_{e2e, cloud} \approx 130 + 20 + 5 + 1 = 156 \text{ ms} \gg 10 \text{ ms SLA}$$

**SLA violated by $\approx 15.6 \times$.**

**Step 4: Compute fog-deployed path (2 marks).**
Distance from car to fog node (MEC server at city edge): $d = 50$ km $= 5 \times 10^{4}$ m.

$$L_{prop, one-way} = \frac{5 \times 10^{4}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.25 \text{ ms}$$

$$L_{prop, RTT} = 0.5 \text{ ms}$$

Adding $L_{trans} \approx 1$ ms, $L_{proc} \approx 3$ ms, $L_{queue} \approx 0.5$ ms:

$$L_{e2e, fog} \approx 0.5 + 1 + 3 + 0.5 = 5 \text{ ms} < 10 \text{ ms SLA}$$

**SLA satisfied with $\approx 5$ ms headroom.**

**Step 5: Conclusion (1 mark).**
The **fog deployment** is the only architecturally feasible option. The fundamental constraint is the **speed of light in fiber** — $v_{fiber} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s — which cannot be overcome by faster CPUs or wider bandwidth. Only **physical proximity** (fog) can meet the SLA.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECIT867]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

#### (a) **Discuss the OpenFog Reference Architecture (FRA) in detail. Explain each of its eight pillars and illustrate how the FRA maps to a smart-manufacturing use case.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction to FRA (1 mark):**
The OpenFog Consortium (now part of the Industry IoT Consortium) published the **OpenFog Reference Architecture (FRA)**, later standardised as **IEEE 1934-2018**, to provide a vendor-neutral structural blueprint for fog deployments. It defines **eight cross-cutting pillars** that every compliant system must address.

**2. The Eight Pillars (4 marks — 0.5 per pillar with one-line meaning):**
1. **Security** — Hardware root-of-trust, encrypted channels, zero-trust.
2. **Scalability** — Horizontal addition of fog nodes without redesign.
3. **Openness** — Standards-based, vendor-neutral interfaces.
4. **Autonomy** — Local operation during cloud disconnection.
5. **RAS** — High availability through redundancy and fault tolerance.
6. **Agility** — Container-based rapid redeployment of services.
7. **Heterogeneity** — Support for diverse hardware/OS/protocols.
8. **Hierarchy** — Multi-level nesting of fog domains (fog-of-fogs).

**3. Smart-Manufacturing Mapping (2 marks):**
- **Security:** Each PLC carries a TPM chip signing all telemetry; the factory-floor gateway verifies signatures before forwarding to the MES cloud.
- **Scalability:** Adding a new assembly line is a *plug-in* of a new fog node; the orchestrator auto-discovers and integrates it.
- **Autonomy:** If the uplink to the cloud fails, the fog node continues to run safety interlocks and machine-vision defect detection.
- **Agility + Openness:** A new defect-detection CNN is containerised and pushed via a standard orchestrator (e.g., K3s) to all fog nodes in under 5 minutes.
- **Hierarchy:** A *line-level* fog node → *cell-level* fog → *plant-level* fog → *enterprise cloud*.

---

#### (b) **Consider a CCTV network of 5,000 cameras streaming 4 Mbps H.264 video to a central cloud. Compute the bandwidth reduction achieved if an edge analytics node forwards only the frames containing motion events. Assume 8% of frames contain motion. Also estimate the monthly bandwidth cost savings at \$0.02 per GB.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute the input data rate (1 mark).**

$$D_{in} = N \times R_{per\ camera} = 5{,}000 \times 4 \text{ Mbps} = 20{,}000 \text{ Mbps} = 20 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 2: Compute the output data rate after edge filtering (1 mark).**

$$D_{out} = 0.08 \times 20 = 1.6 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 3: Compute the reduction ratio (1 mark).**

$$R_{edge} = \frac{20 - 1.6}{20} \times 100\% = 92\%$$

**Step 4: Compute the saved bandwidth (1 mark).**

$$B_{saved} = 20 - 1.6 = 18.4 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Step 5: Compute the daily saved data volume (1 mark).**

$$V_{saved/day} = 18.4 \text{ Gbps} \times \frac{3600 \times 24}{8} \text{ s/day} = 18.4 \times 10^{9} \times 3600 \times 24 \div 8 \text{ bits}$$

$$V_{saved/day} = 18.4 \times 3.6 \times 24 \text{ GB} = 18.4 \times 86.4 \text{ GB} = 1{,}589.76 \text{ GB/day}$$

**Step 6: Compute the monthly cost savings (1 mark).**

$$V_{saved/month} = 1{,}589.76 \times 30 = 47{,}692.8 \text{ GB} \approx 46.6 \text{ TB}$$

$$\text{Savings} = 47{,}692.8 \times 0.02 = \$953.86 \text{ per month}$$

**Step 7: Conclusion (1 mark).**
An edge-architecture approach yields a **92% bandwidth reduction** and **monthly savings of approximately \$954** for this deployment, in addition to a comparable reduction in cloud storage and processing costs. This justifies the architectural investment in edge nodes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Architecture Questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Conflating Edge and Fog tiers.** Many students treat "edge" and "fog" as synonyms. In KTU valuation, the **Edge tier is the device itself**, while the **Fog tier is the intermediate compute node** (gateway, MEC server, micro-DC). Using them interchangeably costs **1–2 marks** per occurrence.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in latency derivations.** Writing "$L_{prop} = 6500$" without the "ms" or "s" suffix is marked **incorrect** by strict KTU examiners. Always annotate the **unit**.
>
> 3. **Omitting the speed-of-light argument.** When justifying why cloud-only fails, students often say "cloud is too slow." The KTU 2024 rubric explicitly requires the **physical derivation** using $v_{fiber} = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Skipping this costs **up to 2 marks**.
>
> 4. **Listing pillars without defining them.** In the 8-pillar question, naming "Security" and "Scalability" without a one-line meaning fetches only partial credit. Each pillar needs a **functional definition** relevant to fog.
>
> 5. **Not using a block diagram.** A 7-mark sub-question without a labelled diagram is marked **down by 1 mark** minimum. The Mermaid diagrams in Section 4 of these notes are directly reproducible.
>
> 6. **Sign errors in the M/M/1 queueing formula.** The correct form is $L_{queue} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu-\lambda)}$ — *not* $\frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$. The latter is a common confusion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Architecture (Module 4)**
>
> - **Three Tiers:** Edge (< 1 ms) → Fog (1–20 ms) → Cloud (50–200 ms).
> - **End-to-end latency equation:** $L_{e2e} = L_{proc} + L_{queue} + L_{trans} + L_{prop}$.
> - **Speed of light in fiber:** $v_{fiber} = 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s — the **fundamental limit** that no compute upgrade can overcome.
> - **Eight Pillars (OpenFog / IEEE 1934):** Security, Scalability, Openness, Autonomy, RAS, Agility, Heterogeneity, Hierarchy.
> - **Cloud-only SLA violation:** Bengaluru–Virginia RTT propagation $\approx$ **130 ms alone**, $\gg 10$ ms autonomous-driving SLA.
> - **Fog-deployed SLA compliance:** 50 km propagation RTT $\approx$ **0.5 ms**, easily fits the 10 ms budget.
> - **Edge data reduction example:** 4 Mbps → 0.32 Mbps (92% reduction) for an 8%-motion CCTV scenario.
> - **Service placement rule:** Place at the *lowest tier* that satisfies the latency, privacy, and bandwidth constraints.
> - **Fog node internal block:** Ingress → Security → Virtualization → Services (Stream / ML / Translator) → Local Store → Message Bus → Egress.
> - **Architectural spectrum:** Pure Cloud ↔ Cloud-Fog ↔ Balanced ↔ Fog-Dominant ↔ Pure Edge — choose based on workload mix.
> - **Three architectural motivations:** Defeat latency (proximity), defeat bandwidth (filtering), defeat privacy/sovereignty (locality).
> - **Orchestrator role:** The "brain" that decides *where* each microservice runs; central to the architecture's adaptability.
> - **Hierarchical fog:** Fog-of-fogs — cell-level → plant-level → enterprise cloud — enables city- and nation-scale deployments.
> - **Key exam phrases to memorise:** "Push computation to the data, not data to the computation." "The speed of light is the boss."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Configurations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT867 - FOG AND EDGE COMPUTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Configurations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Fog and Edge Computing**, the term **Configurations** refers to the systematic arrangement, organization, and interconnection of heterogeneous computing resources — including edge devices, fog nodes, gateways, and cloud data centers — across multiple hierarchical tiers to enable low-latency, distributed, and context-aware computation. Configurations define **how computational, storage, networking, and orchestration resources are structured, deployed, and managed** to satisfy the Quality of Service (QoS), latency, bandwidth, security, and scalability demands of IoT and latency-critical applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Configurations in fog and edge computing encompasses the architectural blueprints that govern *device placement*, *node topology*, *resource allocation*, and *inter-tier communication protocols*. It is the blueprint for turning a theoretical fog-edge hierarchy into a deployable system.

Configurations are broadly classified along four orthogonal axes:
1. **Physical / Hardware Configurations** — device form factors, compute capacity, sensors
2. **Network / Communication Configurations** — topology, protocols, gateways
3. **Architectural / Hierarchical Configurations** — number of tiers, node placement
4. **Deployment / Operational Configurations** — centralized, distributed, hierarchical, hybrid

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Smart City Traffic Control System**

Imagine a **Smart City Traffic Control Network**:
- **Edge Layer (Sensors at Intersections):** Tiny cameras and pressure sensors mounted on traffic lights at every intersection. They detect vehicle density **locally** and immediately flash a signal change. They don't wait for a remote server to decide. These are the **Edge Devices**.
- **Fog Layer (Area Traffic Control Rooms):** Every 5–10 intersections are aggregated and managed by a local traffic control room (a fog node). This room coordinates multiple intersections, optimizes green-wave timing for a sub-region, and predicts congestion. It bridges the gap between the intersection and the central command.
- **Cloud Layer (Central City Command Center):** A centralized data center that processes city-wide analytics, historical patterns, long-term planning, and feeds machine learning models back to the fog and edge.

The **"Configuration"** is the engineering decision of *how many fog nodes to deploy*, *which intersections they cover*, *how they communicate with the edge*, *what protocols they use*, and *how data flows up to the cloud*. Just as no two cities have the same traffic control layout, **no two fog-edge systems have identical configurations** — they are tailored to latency, geography, application, and budget.

A simpler everyday analogy: think of **electricity distribution**. Power is generated at a **central plant (Cloud)**, stepped down at **substations (Fog)**, and delivered to your **home (Edge)**. The configuration defines the number of substations, the voltage levels, the redundancy, and the topology — the very same engineering choices that define a fog-edge configuration.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Configurations

- **Latency target:** typically **1–10 ms** (edge) vs. **50–200 ms** (cloud) — **bolded** as the defining metric.
- **Bandwidth requirements:** often expressed in **Mbps/Gbps** per node.
- **Hop count:** number of network intermediaries between source and compute point.
- **Tier count:** number of hierarchical levels (commonly 3: Edge–Fog–Cloud).
- **Coverage radius:** geographic/logical area served by a single fog node.
- **Standard reference models:** **ISO/IEC 30141 (IoT Reference Architecture)**, **ETSI MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing)**, **NIST SP 500-325 Fog Computing Conceptual Model**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Configurations

Fog and Edge Computing configurations can be decomposed into the following structured taxonomy:

### 2.1.1 Hardware / Device Configurations
- **Constrained Edge Devices:** Sensors, actuators, microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino, ESP32) — limited CPU, KB-level RAM, battery-powered.
- **Edge Gateways:** Industrial PCs, Raspberry Pi-class devices — moderate compute, multi-protocol support.
- **Fog Nodes:** High-end servers, micro-data centers — substantial compute, storage, virtualization capability.
- **Cloud Data Centers:** Hyperscale facilities with petaflop-scale compute and petabyte storage.

### 2.1.2 Network / Communication Configurations
- **Device-to-Device (D2D):** Bluetooth, ZigBee, Z-Wave — short range, low power.
- **Device-to-Gateway:** Wi-Fi, LoRa, MQTT over TCP/IP.
- **Gateway-to-Fog:** Wired Ethernet, 4G/5G, fiber.
- **Fog-to-Cloud:** High-bandwidth WAN, MPLS, dedicated leased lines.

### 2.1.3 Architectural / Hierarchical Configurations
- **3-Tier (Edge–Fog–Cloud):** The canonical reference model.
- **4-Tier (Extreme Edge–Edge–Fog–Cloud):** Adds a sub-edge tier for ultra-low-latency.
- **Flat / Peer-to-Peer:** Fog nodes communicate laterally without a strict cloud dependency.
- **Clustered:** Multiple fog nodes form a coordinated cluster serving a region.

### 2.1.4 Deployment Configurations
- **Centralized:** Single fog controller orchestrates all edge devices.
- **Distributed:** Multiple autonomous fog nodes, no single point of failure.
- **Hierarchical:** Tree-structured, with parent-child orchestration relationships.
- **Hybrid:** Combines centralized control with distributed autonomy.

## 2.2 The 3-Tier Reference Configuration

> [!NOTE]
> The **3-Tier Configuration** is the de facto reference model for fog-edge systems. It is consistently examined by KTU as a high-yield topic.

**Tier 1 — Edge Layer (Device Layer):**
- **Latency:** < 10 ms
- **Role:** Data acquisition, pre-processing, local inference
- **Examples:** CCTV cameras with on-board AI, smart thermostats, autonomous vehicle ECUs

**Tier 2 — Fog Layer (Intermediate Compute Layer):**
- **Latency:** 10–50 ms
- **Role:** Aggregation, filtering, regional analytics, real-time decision-making
- **Examples:** 5G base stations with MEC servers, industrial gateways, road-side units (RSUs)

**Tier 3 — Cloud Layer (Global Compute Layer):**
- **Latency:** 50–500 ms
- **Role:** Long-term storage, big-data analytics, ML model training, global orchestration
- **Examples:** AWS, Azure, Google Cloud data centers

## 2.3 Configuration Design Parameters

The choice of a configuration is governed by the following parameters, typically expressed as a tuple:

$$
C = \langle L, T, N, P, S, R \rangle
$$

Where:
- $L$ = **Latency budget** (ms)
- $T$ = **Tier count** (integer)
- $N$ = **Number of nodes per tier** (integer)
- $P$ = **Communication protocol set** (set of strings)
- $S$ = **Storage capacity distribution** (vector in bytes)
- $R$ = **Redundancy factor** (real number $\geq 1$)

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $L_{\text{edge}}$ | Edge tier latency | 1–10 | ms |
| $L_{\text{fog}}$ | Fog tier latency | 10–50 | ms |
| $L_{\text{cloud}}$ | Cloud tier latency | 50–500 | ms |
| $B_{\text{req}}$ | Required bandwidth per device | 0.1–100 | Mbps |
| $H$ | Hop count (source to compute) | 1–5 | hops |
| $R$ | Redundancy factor | 1–3 | dimensionless |
| $T$ | Tier count | 2–4 | integer |
| $C$ | Coverage radius per fog node | 1–50 | km |
| $\eta_{\text{util}}$ | Resource utilization ratio | 0.6–0.85 | dimensionless |
| $P_{\text{proc}}$ | Processing latency | 0.5–5 | ms |
| $P_{\text{prop}}$ | Propagation delay | $\propto$ distance / $c$ | ms |
| $P_{\text{queue}}$ | Queuing delay | depends on load | ms |
| $E_{\text{node}}$ | Energy per node | 0.5–500 | W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total End-to-End Latency Formula** (used in KTU problems):
> $$L_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{proc}} + P_{\text{prop}} + P_{\text{queue}} + P_{\text{trans}}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Configurations determine whether a robotic arm stops within safety limits (edge), or whether predictive maintenance uses aggregated sensor streams (fog), or whether long-term process optimization is performed (cloud).
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Multi-tier configuration ensures obstacle detection at the edge (sub-10 ms), route optimization at the fog (RSUs), and HD map updates from the cloud.
- **Healthcare IoT:** Wearables process heart rate locally; fog nodes aggregate ICU ward data; cloud stores longitudinal patient records.
- **Smart Grid:** Smart meters (edge), substation concentrators (fog), and grid control centers (cloud) form a 3-tier configuration.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: End-to-End Latency Calculation Across Tiers

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical Problem:** A temperature monitoring system uses a 3-tier configuration. An edge sensor transmits a 2 KB packet to a fog node, which forwards a 1.5 KB summary to the cloud. Given: $P_{\text{proc, edge}} = 2$ ms, $P_{\text{prop, edge-fog}} = 3$ ms, $P_{\text{queue, edge-fog}} = 1.5$ ms, $P_{\text{trans, edge-fog}} = 0.5$ ms, $P_{\text{proc, fog}} = 4$ ms, $P_{\text{prop, fog-cloud}} = 8$ ms, $P_{\text{queue, fog-cloud}} = 2$ ms, $P_{\text{trans, fog-cloud}} = 1$ ms, $P_{\text{proc, cloud}} = 10$ ms. Calculate the total end-to-end latency.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Identify the latency components per tier transition.**

Edge → Fog transition:
$$
L_{\text{edge→fog}} = P_{\text{proc, edge}} + P_{\text{prop, edge-fog}} + P_{\text{queue, edge-fog}} + P_{\text{trans, edge-fog}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values for the edge-to-fog hop.**

$$
L_{\text{edge→fog}} = 2 + 3 + 1.5 + 0.5
$$

$$
L_{\text{edge→fog}} = 7 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute fog-to-cloud hop latency.**

$$
L_{\text{fog→cloud}} = P_{\text{proc, fog}} + P_{\text{prop, fog-cloud}} + P_{\text{queue, fog-cloud}} + P_{\text{trans, fog-cloud}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values for the fog-to-cloud hop.**

$$
L_{\text{fog→cloud}} = 4 + 8 + 2 + 1
$$

$$
L_{\text{fog→cloud}} = 15 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Add the cloud processing latency.**

$$
L_{\text{total}} = L_{\text{edge→fog}} + L_{\text{fog→cloud}} + P_{\text{proc, cloud}}
$$

**Step 6 — Final substitution.**

$$
L_{\text{total}} = 7 + 15 + 10
$$

$$
\boxed{L_{\text{total}} = 32 \text{ ms}}
$$

This is a typical KTU Part B 14-mark derivation extended with numerical substitution.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Optimal Fog Node Placement Using Coverage Radius

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A smart campus has 200 edge devices uniformly distributed over an area of $A = 4 \text{ km}^2$. Each fog node provides wireless coverage of radius $r = 300$ m. How many fog nodes are required to cover the entire campus with a redundancy factor $R = 1.2$?

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Compute the coverage area of a single fog node.**

$$
A_{\text{node}} = \pi r^{2}
$$

$$
A_{\text{node}} = \pi \times (0.3)^{2}
$$

$$
A_{\text{node}} = \pi \times 0.09
$$

$$
A_{\text{node}} \approx 0.2827 \text{ km}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of fog nodes ignoring redundancy.**

$$
N_{\text{min}} = \frac{A}{A_{\text{node}}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{min}} = \frac{4}{0.2827}
$$

$$
N_{\text{min}} \approx 14.15
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the redundancy factor $R = 1.2$.**

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = \lceil N_{\text{min}} \times R \rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = \lceil 14.15 \times 1.2 \rceil
$$

$$
N_{\text{actual}} = \lceil 16.98 \rceil
$$

$$
\boxed{N_{\text{actual}} = 17 \text{ fog nodes}}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation for KTU exam purposes.**

17 fog nodes must be deployed to provide full coverage with 20% redundancy (overlap for fault tolerance).

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Bandwidth Aggregation Across Tiers

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** 500 edge devices each generate 10 KB/s. The fog node applies a 60% data reduction through filtering and aggregation before forwarding to the cloud. Calculate the bandwidth required at (a) the edge-to-fog link and (b) the fog-to-cloud link.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Aggregate edge bandwidth before filtering.**

$$
B_{\text{edge→fog}} = N_{\text{devices}} \times B_{\text{per device}}
$$

$$
B_{\text{edge→fog}} = 500 \times 10 \text{ KB/s}
$$

$$
B_{\text{edge→fog}} = 5000 \text{ KB/s} = 5 \text{ MB/s} = 40 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 60% data reduction at the fog node (i.e., 40% forwarded).**

$$
B_{\text{fog→cloud}} = B_{\text{edge→fog}} \times (1 - 0.60)
$$

$$
B_{\text{fog→cloud}} = 40 \text{ Mbps} \times 0.40
$$

$$
\boxed{B_{\text{fog→cloud}} = 16 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

This is a hallmark KTU derivation demonstrating the **bandwidth-saving utility of fog configurations**.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 3-Tier Fog-Edge-Cloud Reference Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CloudLayer["CLOUD LAYER - Tier 3"]
        Cloud1["Global Data Center"]
        Cloud2["Big Data Analytics"]
        Cloud3["ML Model Training"]
        Cloud4["Long Term Storage"]
    end

    subgraph FogLayer["FOG LAYER - Tier 2"]
        FogNode1["Fog Node A - Region 1"]
        FogNode2["Fog Node B - Region 2"]
        FogNode3["Gateway Aggregator"]
    end

    subgraph EdgeLayer["EDGE LAYER - Tier 1"]
        EdgeDev1["Smart Sensor 1"]
        EdgeDev2["Smart Sensor 2"]
        EdgeDev3["CCTV Camera"]
        EdgeDev4["Wearable Device"]

    end

    EdgeDev1 --> FogNode1
    EdgeDev2 --> FogNode1
    EdgeDev3 --> FogNode2
    EdgeDev4 --> FogNode3
    EdgeDev5 --> FogNode3

    Cloud3 --- Cloud4
    FogNode1 -. Lateral P2P .- FogNode2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The Edge Layer feeds raw sensor data upward to the Fog Layer, which performs aggregation, filtering, and regional analytics. The Cloud Layer then ingests the summarized data for global analytics and feeds trained ML models back down. **Dashed lines** denote optional peer-to-peer communication between fog nodes for fault tolerance and load balancing.

## 4.3 Fog Node Internal Configuration Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FogNodeInternal["FOG NODE INTERNAL ARCHITECTURE"]
        In["Ingress Interface - Ethernet 5G WiFi"]
        Proto["Protocol Adapter - MQTT CoAP HTTP"]
        Virt["Virtualization Layer - VMs Containers"]
        Sched["Scheduler / Orchestrator"]
        App1["Analytics App"]
        App2["Stream Processor"]
        App3["Local Cache"]
        Store["Local Storage SSD"]
        Out["Egress Interface to Cloud"]
        Mon["Monitoring and Telemetry"]
    end

    In --> Proto
    Proto --> Virt
    Virt --> Sched
    Sched --> App1
    Sched --> App2
    Sched --> App3
    App1 --> Store
    App2 --> Store
    App3 --> Store
    App1 --> Out
    App2 --> Out
    Mon -. monitors .- Virt
    Mon -. monitors .- Sched
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Insight:** This block diagram maps the **internal functional architecture** of a generic fog node. The **ingress interface** accepts heterogeneous protocol traffic; the **protocol adapter** normalizes it; the **virtualization layer** hosts tenant workloads; the **scheduler** allocates resources; and the **egress interface** forwards aggregated data to the cloud. The **monitoring module** is a sidecar that observes QoS and triggers reconfiguration.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define fog computing configuration. List the three standard tiers. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **fog computing configuration** is the structured arrangement of compute, storage, and networking resources across hierarchical tiers to enable distributed, low-latency processing between edge devices and the cloud.
The three standard tiers are:
1. **Edge Tier** — device-level data acquisition and pre-processing
2. **Fog Tier** — intermediate aggregation, filtering, and regional analytics
3. **Cloud Tier** — global analytics, long-term storage, and ML training

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between centralized and distributed fog configurations. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Centralized | Distributed |
|---|---|---|
| Control | Single fog controller | Multiple autonomous fog nodes |
| Failure impact | Single point of failure | High fault tolerance |
| Scalability | Limited by controller | Highly scalable |
| Latency | Higher (all traffic to one node) | Lower (geo-proximity) |
| Use case | Small deployments | Large-scale IoT (smart city) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**Q. (a)** Explain the 3-tier reference configuration of fog and edge computing in detail. Illustrate with a suitable block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **3-tier reference configuration** is the canonical architectural blueprint for fog-edge systems. It organizes resources into three hierarchical layers:

**Tier 1 — Edge Layer:**
- Comprises **constrained devices** such as sensors, actuators, smart cameras, and wearables.
- Performs **local data acquisition**, **preliminary filtering**, and **on-device inference**.
- Latency target: **< 10 ms**.
- Communication: short-range protocols (BLE, ZigBee, LoRa).

**Tier 2 — Fog Layer:**
- Comprises **intermediate compute nodes** (gateways, micro-data centers, MEC servers).
- Performs **data aggregation**, **stream processing**, **regional analytics**, and **real-time decision-making**.
- Latency target: **10–50 ms**.
- Communication: Wi-Fi, 5G, wired Ethernet.

**Tier 3 — Cloud Layer:**
- Comprises **hyperscale data centers**.
- Performs **big-data analytics**, **ML model training**, and **long-term archival**.
- Latency target: **50–500 ms** (acceptable for non-real-time workloads).
- Communication: high-bandwidth WAN, fiber, MPLS.

**Block diagram** (refer to Section 4.1): The edge devices feed the fog nodes, which in turn feed the cloud. Lateral links between fog nodes provide redundancy.

**Key benefits:** Reduced latency, bandwidth savings, improved resilience, and context-aware processing.

**Marking-wise (KTU key pattern):**
- Tier identification: 2 marks
- Latency and role per tier: 3 marks
- Diagram: 1 mark
- Benefits and communication: 1 mark

---

**Q. (b)** A smart parking system deploys 1000 ultrasonic sensors across a 2 km² area. Each sensor generates 8 KB/s of data. A fog node filters out 70% of redundant data. Compute: (i) the bandwidth entering the fog node, and (ii) the bandwidth exiting the fog node to the cloud. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Total data rate from all sensors (edge → fog).**

$$
B_{\text{edge→fog}} = N \times B_{\text{sensor}}
$$

$$
B_{\text{edge→fog}} = 1000 \times 8 \text{ KB/s}
$$

$$
B_{\text{edge→fog}} = 8000 \text{ KB/s} = 8 \text{ MB/s}
$$

Converting to Mbps:

$$
B_{\text{edge→fog}} = 8 \times 8 = 64 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply 70% filtering (only 30% passes through).**

$$
B_{\text{fog→cloud}} = B_{\text{edge→fog}} \times (1 - 0.70)
$$

$$
B_{\text{fog→cloud}} = 64 \times 0.30
$$

$$
\boxed{B_{\text{fog→cloud}} = 19.2 \text{ Mbps}}
$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth savings achieved by the fog configuration.**

$$
\text{Savings} = 64 - 19.2 = 44.8 \text{ Mbps}
$$

$$
\text{Savings \%} = \frac{44.8}{64} \times 100 = 70\%
$$

**Final Answer:** Bandwidth entering the fog node = **64 Mbps**; Bandwidth exiting to the cloud = **19.2 Mbps** (70% reduction).

**Marking pattern:** Step 1: 3 marks, Step 2: 2 marks, Step 3 (savings commentary): 2 marks.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Discuss the various deployment configurations of fog computing (centralized, distributed, hierarchical, hybrid) with suitable use cases. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Centralized Configuration:**
- A **single fog controller** orchestrates all edge devices and fog nodes.
- Simple to manage; ideal for **small-scale, single-site deployments** (e.g., a single factory).
- *Drawback:* single point of failure, scalability bottleneck.

**2. Distributed Configuration:**
- Multiple **autonomous fog nodes** operate independently.
- High fault tolerance; ideal for **smart cities, large-scale IoT**.
- No single point of failure; supports geographic distribution.

**3. Hierarchical Configuration:**
- **Tree-structured** organization with parent-child fog nodes.
- Enables **multi-level aggregation** and **regional coordination**.
- Used in **telecom networks**, **smart grid**, and **multi-tier industrial IoT**.

**4. Hybrid Configuration:**
- Combines **centralized control with distributed autonomy**.
- Central cloud provides global policy; distributed fog nodes execute local decisions.
- Used in **connected vehicles** (cloud-pushed HD maps + local RSU decisions).

**Comparison Table:**

| Configuration | Fault Tolerance | Scalability | Complexity | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Centralized | Low | Low | Low | Single factory |
| Distributed | High | High | Medium | Smart city |
| Hierarchical | Medium | High | High | Telecom |
| Hybrid | High | High | High | Autonomous vehicles |

**Marking pattern:** 4 configs × 1.5 marks = 6 marks, table = 1 mark.

---

**Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal architecture of a fog node. List any four functional components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A **fog node** is the fundamental compute unit in the fog tier. Its internal architecture (refer to Section 4.3) comprises:

**Functional Components (any four):**
1. **Ingress Interface** — accepts incoming data from edge devices via Ethernet, 5G, or Wi-Fi.
2. **Protocol Adapter** — normalizes heterogeneous protocols (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP).
3. **Virtualization Layer** — hosts VMs or containers for multi-tenant workload isolation.
4. **Scheduler / Orchestrator** — allocates CPU, memory, and network resources to applications.
5. **Local Storage** — caches recent data for low-latency access.
6. **Egress Interface** — forwards aggregated data to the cloud.
7. **Monitoring Module** — tracks QoS metrics and triggers auto-scaling.

**Working Principle:** Raw data enters via the ingress interface, is normalized by the protocol adapter, processed by containerized applications orchestrated by the scheduler, and either stored locally or forwarded to the cloud. The monitoring module continuously observes performance and reconfigures resources as needed.

**Marking pattern:** Diagram = 3 marks, 4 components × 1 mark = 4 marks.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing tiers with deployment models** — Students often mix up "3-tier" (architectural) with "centralized/distributed" (deployment). They are orthogonal axes.
> 2. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — Always specify ms, Mbps, KB/s, km², etc. Missing units = 0.5 mark deduction per KTU key.
> 3. **Omitting the diagram in 7-mark questions** — A 7-mark theoretical question without a diagram loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Not stating the latency target per tier** — KTU examiners reward explicit mention of latency budgets (1–10 ms edge, 10–50 ms fog, 50–500 ms cloud).
> 5. **Forgetting the redundancy factor** — In placement problems, always mention $R \geq 1$ to justify over-provisioning.
> 6. **Confusing fog node with edge device** — A fog node has virtualization capability; an edge device typically does not.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Configurations** = the structural blueprint of fog-edge systems — *what goes where, how they connect, and why*.
- ✅ **3-Tier Reference Model:** Edge (sensors) → Fog (gateways/MEC) → Cloud (data centers).
- ✅ **4 axes of classification:** Hardware, Network, Architectural, Deployment.
- ✅ **Latency hierarchy:** **1–10 ms (edge) < 10–50 ms (fog) < 50–500 ms (cloud)** — memorize this triplet.
- ✅ **Key formula:** $L_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{proc}} + P_{\text{prop}} + P_{\text{queue}} + P_{\text{trans}}$ — used in every latency numerical.
- ✅ **Coverage formula:** $N_{\text{actual}} = \lceil \frac{A}{\pi r^{2}} \times R \rceil$ — used in placement numericals.
- ✅ **Bandwidth aggregation:** $B_{\text{fog→cloud}} = B_{\text{edge→fog}} \times (1 - \text{filter ratio})$.
- ✅ **4 deployment configurations:** Centralized, Distributed, Hierarchical, Hybrid — know one use case for each.
- ✅ **Fog node internal architecture:** Ingress → Protocol Adapter → Virtualization → Scheduler → Apps → Egress.
- ✅ **Standard reference models:** ISO/IEC 30141, ETSI MEC, NIST SP 500-325 — name-drop them for full marks.
- ✅ **Real-world relevance:** Smart cities, Industry 4.0, autonomous vehicles, smart grid, healthcare IoT.
- ✅ **Always include units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- ✅ **Draw a diagram** for any 7-mark theoretical question — it is almost always worth 1–3 marks.
